items.title: From given figure, in ∆MNK, if ∠MNK = 90°, ∠M = 45°, MK = 6, then for finding value of MN and KN, complete the following activity.Activity: In ∆MNK, ∠MNK = 90°, ∠M = 45° …...[Given] ∴ ∠K = □ .....[R - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: From given figure, in ∆MNK, if ∠MNK = 90°, ∠M = 45°, MK = 6, then for finding value of MN and KN, complete the following activity. Activity: In ∆MNK, ∠MNK = 90°, ∠M = 45° …...[Given] ∴ ∠K = `square` .....[Remaining angle of ∆MNK] By theorem of 45° – 45° – 90° triangle, ∴ `square = 1/sqrt(2)` MK and `square = 1/sqrt(2)` MK ∴ MN = `1/sqrt(2) xx square` and KN = `1/sqrt(2) xx 6` ∴ MN = `3sqrt(2)` and KN = `3sqrt(2)`
items.solution_text: In ∆MNK, ∠MNK = 90°, ∠M = 45° …...[Given] ∴ ∠K = 45° .....[Remaining angle of ∆MNK] By theorem of 45° – 45° – 90° triangle, ∴ MN = `1/sqrt(2)` MK and KN = `1/sqrt(2)` MK ∴ MN = `1/sqrt(2) xx 6` and KN = `1/sqrt(2) xx 6` ∴ MN = `(1 xx 6 xx sqrt(2))/(sqrt(2) xx sqrt(2))` and KN = `(1 xx 6 xx sqrt(2))/(sqrt(2) xx sqrt(2))` .......[Multiply numerator and denominator by `sqrt(2)`] ∴ MN = `(6 xx sqrt(2))/2` and KN = `(6 xx sqrt(2))/2` ∴ MN = `3sqrt(2)` and KN = `3sqrt(2)`
items.solution_html:
In ∆MNK, ∠MNK = 90°, ∠M = 45°   …...[Given]

∴ ∠K = 45°      .....[Remaining angle of ∆MNK]

By theorem of 45° – 45° – 90° triangle,

∴ MN = `1/sqrt(2)` MK and KN = `1/sqrt(2)` MK

∴ MN = `1/sqrt(2) xx 6` and KN = `1/sqrt(2) xx 6`

∴ MN = `(1 xx 6 xx sqrt(2))/(sqrt(2) xx sqrt(2))` and KN = `(1 xx 6 xx sqrt(2))/(sqrt(2) xx sqrt(2))`    .......[Multiply numerator and denominator by `sqrt(2)`]

∴ MN = `(6 xx sqrt(2))/2` and KN = `(6 xx sqrt(2))/2`

∴ MN = `3sqrt(2)` and KN = `3sqrt(2)`

items.title: From given figure, in ∆PQR, if ∠QPR = 90°, PM ⊥ QR, PM = 10, QM = 8, then for finding the value of QR, complete the following activity.Activity: In ∆PQR, if ∠QPR = 90°, PM ⊥ QR, ......[Given] In ∆PM - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: From given figure, in ∆PQR, if ∠QPR = 90°, PM ⊥ QR, PM = 10, QM = 8, then for finding the value of QR, complete the following activity. Activity: In ∆PQR, if ∠QPR = 90°, PM ⊥ QR, ......[Given] In ∆PMQ, by Pythagoras Theorem, ∴ PM 2 + `square` = PQ 2 ......(I) ∴ PQ 2 = 10 2 + 8 2 ∴ PQ 2 = `square` + 64 ∴ PQ 2 = `square` ∴ PQ = `sqrt(164)` Here, ∆QPR ~ ∆QMP ~ ∆PMR ∴ ∆QMP ~ ∆PMR ∴ `"PM"/"RM" = "QM"/"PM"` ∴ PM 2 = RM × QM ∴ 10 2 = RM × 8 RM = `100/8 = square` And, QR = QM + MR QR = `square` + `25/2 = 41/2`
items.solution_text: In ∆PQR, if ∠QPR = 90°, PM ⊥ QR, ......[Given] In ∆PMQ, by Pythagoras Theorem, ∴ PM 2 + QM 2 = PQ 2 ......(I) ∴ PQ 2 = 10 2 + 8 2 ∴ PQ 2 = 100 + 64 ∴ PQ 2 = 164 ∴ PQ = `sqrt(164)` Here, ∆QPR ~ ∆QMP ~ ∆PMR ∴ ∆QMP ~ ∆PMR ∴ `"PM"/"RM" = "QM"/"PM"` ......[Corresponding sides of similar triangles] ∴ PM 2 = RM × QM ∴ 10 2 = RM × 8 RM = `100/8` = `25/2` And, QR = QM + MR QR = 8 + `25/2 = 41/2`
items.solution_html:
In ∆PQR, if ∠QPR = 90°, PM ⊥ QR,  ......[Given]

In ∆PMQ,

by Pythagoras Theorem,

∴ PM2 + QM2 = PQ2     ......(I)

∴ PQ2 = 102 + 82 

∴ PQ2 = 100 + 64

∴ PQ2 = 164

∴ PQ = `sqrt(164)`

Here, ∆QPR ~ ∆QMP ~ ∆PMR

∴ ∆QMP ~ ∆PMR

∴ `"PM"/"RM" = "QM"/"PM"`   ......[Corresponding sides of similar triangles]

∴ PM2 = RM × QM

∴ 102 = RM × 8

RM = `100/8` = `25/2`

And,

QR = QM + MR

QR = 8 + `25/2 = 41/2`

items.title: From the figure given alongside, find the length of the median AD of triangle ABC. Complete the activity. Solution: Here A(–1, 1), B(5, – 3), C(3, 5) and suppose D(x, y) are coordinates of point D. U - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: From the figure given alongside, find the length of the median AD of triangle ABC. Complete the activity. Solution: Here A(–1, 1), B(5, – 3), C(3, 5) and suppose D(x, y) are coordinates of point D. Using midpoint formula, x = `(5 + 3)/2` ∴ x = `square` y = `(-3 + 5)/2` ∴ y = `square` Using distance formula, ∴ AD = `sqrt((4 - square)^2 + (1 - 1)^2` ∴ AD = `sqrt((square)^2 + (0)^2` ∴ AD = `sqrt(square)` ∴ The length of median AD = `square`
items.solution_text: Here A(–1, 1), B(5, – 3), C(3, 5) and suppose D(x, y) are coordinates of point D. D is the midpoint of seg BC. Using midpoint formula, x = `(x_1 + x_2)/2` x = `(5 + 3)/2` ∴ x = `8/2` ∴ x = 4 y = `(y_1 + y_2)/2` y = `(-3 + 5)/2` ∴ y = `2/2` ∴ y = 1 Using distance formula, ∴ AD = `sqrt((4 - (-1))^2 + (1 - 1)^2` ∴ AD = `sqrt((5)^2 + (0)^2` ∴ AD = `sqrt(25)` ∴ The length of median AD = 5 cm
items.solution_html:
Here A(–1, 1), B(5, – 3), C(3, 5) and suppose D(x, y) are coordinates of point D. D is the midpoint of seg BC.

Using midpoint formula,

x = `(x_1 + x_2)/2`

x = `(5 + 3)/2`

∴ x = `8/2`

∴ x = 4

y = `(y_1 + y_2)/2`

y = `(-3 + 5)/2`

∴ y = `2/2`

∴ y = 1

Using distance formula,

∴ AD = `sqrt((4 - (-1))^2 + (1 - 1)^2`

∴ AD = `sqrt((5)^2 + (0)^2`

∴ AD = `sqrt(25)`

∴ The length of median AD = 5 cm

items.title: From the given figure, in ∆ABC, if AD ⊥ BC, ∠C = 45°, AC = 82 , BD = 5, then for finding value of AD and BC, complete the following activity.Activity: In ∆ADC, if ∠ADC = 90°, ∠C = 45° ......[Given] - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: From the given figure, in ∆ABC, if AD ⊥ BC, ∠C = 45°, AC = `8sqrt(2)` , BD = 5, then for finding value of AD and BC, complete the following activity. Activity: In ∆ADC, if ∠ADC = 90°, ∠C = 45° ......[Given] ∴ ∠DAC = `square` .....[Remaining angle of ∆ADC] By theorem of 45° – 45° – 90° triangle, ∴ `square = 1/sqrt(2)` AC and `square = 1/sqrt(2)` AC ∴ AD =`1/sqrt(2) xx square` and DC = `1/sqrt(2) xx 8sqrt(2)` ∴ AD = 8 and DC = 8 ∴ BC = BD +DC = 5 + 8 = 13
items.solution_text: In ∆ADC, if ∠ADC = 90°, ∠C = 45° ......[Given] ∴ ∠DAC = 45° .....[Remaining angle of ∆ADC] By theorem of 45° – 45° – 90° triangle, ∴ AD = `1/sqrt(2)` AC and DC = `1/sqrt(2)` AC ∴ AD =`1/sqrt(2) xx 8sqrt(2)` and DC = `1/sqrt(2) xx 8sqrt(2)` ∴ AD = 8 and DC = 8 ∴ BC = BD +DC = 5 + 8 = 13
items.solution_html:
In ∆ADC, if ∠ADC = 90°, ∠C = 45°    ......[Given]

∴ ∠DAC = 45°    .....[Remaining angle of ∆ADC]

By theorem of 45° – 45° – 90° triangle,

∴ AD = `1/sqrt(2)` AC and DC = `1/sqrt(2)` AC

∴ AD =`1/sqrt(2) xx 8sqrt(2)` and DC = `1/sqrt(2) xx 8sqrt(2)`

∴ AD = 8 and DC = 8

∴ BC = BD +DC

= 5 + 8

= 13

items.title: Given: A circle inscribed in a right angled ΔABC. If ∠ACB = 90° and the radius of the circle is r. To prove: 2r = a + b – c - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: Given: A circle inscribed in a right angled ΔABC. If ∠ACB = 90° and the radius of the circle is r. To prove: 2r = a + b – c
items.solution_text: Proof: In given figure, `{:("AF" = "AE"),("FB" = "BD"),("EC" = "DC"):}}` .....(i) [Tangent Segment theorem] In ▢ODCE, ∠ECD = 90° ......[∠ACB = 90°, A–E–C, B-D–C] `{:(∠"ODC" = 90^circ),(∠"OEC" = 90^circ):}}` ......[Tangent theorem] ∴ ∠EOD = 90° …[Ramining angle of ▢ODCE] ∴ ▢ODCE is a rectangle. . Also, OE = OD = r ......[Radii of the same circle] ∴ ▢ODCE is a square ......`[("A Rectangle is square if it's"),("adjcent sides are congruent")]` ∴ OE = OD = CD = CE = r ......(ii) [sides of the square] Consider R.H.S. = a + b – c = BC + AC – AB = (BD + DC) + (AE + EC) – (AF + FB) ......[B–D–C, A–E–C, A–F–B] = (FB + r) + (AF + r) – (AF + FB) ......[From (i) and (ii)] = FB + r + AF + r – AF - FB = 2r = L.H.S ∴ 2r = a + b – c
items.solution_html:
Proof: In given figure,

`{:("AF" = "AE"),("FB" = "BD"),("EC" = "DC"):}}`  .....(i) [Tangent Segment theorem]

In ▢ODCE,

∠ECD = 90°    ......[∠ACB = 90°, A–E–C, B-D–C]

`{:(∠"ODC" = 90^circ),(∠"OEC" = 90^circ):}}`  ......[Tangent theorem]

∴ ∠EOD = 90° …[Ramining angle of ▢ODCE]

∴ ▢ODCE is a rectangle.

. Also, OE = OD = r  ......[Radii of the same circle]

∴ ▢ODCE is a square     ......`[("A Rectangle is square if it's"),("adjcent sides are congruent")]`

∴ OE = OD = CD = CE = r    ......(ii) [sides of the square]

Consider R.H.S. = a + b – c

= BC + AC – AB

= (BD + DC) + (AE + EC) – (AF + FB) ......[B–D–C, A–E–C, A–F–B]

= (FB + r) + (AF + r) – (AF + FB)   ......[From (i) and (ii)]

= FB + r + AF + r – AF - FB

= 2r

= L.H.S

∴ 2r = a + b – c

items.title: Given ΔABC ~ ΔDEF, if ∠A = 45° and ∠E = 35° then ∠B = ? - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: Given ΔABC ~ ΔDEF, if ∠A = 45° and ∠E = 35° then ∠B = ?
items.solution_text: 35° In ΔABC and ΔDEF, ΔABC ~ ΔDEF ...(Given) ∴ ∠B ≅ ∠E ...(Corresponding angles of similar triangles) But ∠E = 35° ...(Given) ∴ ∠B = 35°.
items.solution_html:
35°

In ΔABC and ΔDEF,

ΔABC ~ ΔDEF    ...(Given)

∴ ∠B ≅ ∠E            ...(Corresponding angles of similar triangles)

But ∠E = 35°     ...(Given)

∴  ∠B = 35°.

items.title: Height and base of a right angled triangle are 24 cm and 18 cm. Find the length of its hypotenus? - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: Height and base of a right angled triangle are 24 cm and 18 cm. Find the length of its hypotenus?
items.solution_text: In ∆PQR, ∠Q = 90° ∴ PR 2 = PQ 2 + QR 2 ......[Pythagoras theorem] = 24 2 + 18 2 = 576 + 324 = 900 ∴ PR = `sqrt(900)` = 30 cm
items.solution_html:
In ∆PQR, ∠Q = 90°

∴ PR2 = PQ2 + QR2      ......[Pythagoras theorem]

= 242 + 182

= 576 + 324

= 900

∴ PR = `sqrt(900)`

= 30 cm

items.title: If 1 – cos2θ = 14, then θ = ? - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: If 1 – cos 2 θ = `1/4`, then θ = ?
items.solution_text: 1 – cos 2 θ = `1/4` ......[Given] ∴ sin 2 θ = `1/4` .....`[(because sin^2theta + cos^2theta = 1),(therefore 1 - cos^2theta = sin^2theta)]` ∴ sin θ = `1/2` ......[Taking square root of both sides] ∴ θ = 30° ......`[because sin 30^circ = 1/2]`
items.solution_html:
1 – cos2θ = `1/4`      ......[Given]

∴ sin2θ = `1/4`     .....`[(because sin^2theta + cos^2theta = 1),(therefore 1 - cos^2theta = sin^2theta)]`

∴ sin θ = `1/2`    ......[Taking square root of both sides]

∴ θ = 30°     ......`[because sin 30^circ = 1/2]`

items.title: If 2 sin θ = 3 cos θ, then tan θ = ? - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: If 2 sin θ = 3 cos θ, then tan θ = ?
items.solution_text: 2sin θ = 3cos θ ......[Given] ∴ `sintheta/costheta = 3/2` ∴ tan θ = `3/2`
items.solution_html:
2sin θ = 3cos θ    ......[Given]

∴ `sintheta/costheta = 3/2`

∴ tan θ = `3/2`

items.title: If 3 sin θ = 4 cos θ, then sec θ = ? - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: If 3 sin θ = 4 cos θ, then sec θ = ?
items.solution_text: 3 sin θ = 4cos θ .....[Given] ∴ `(sintheta)/(costheta) = 4/3` ∴ tan θ = `4/3` We know that, 1 + tan 2 θ = sec 2 θ ∴ `1 + (4/3)^2` = sec 2 θ ∴ `1 + 16/9` = sec 2 θ ∴ sec 2 θ = `(9 + 16)/9` ∴ sec 2 θ = `25/9` ∴ sec θ = `5/3` ......[Taking square root of both sides]
items.solution_html:
3 sin θ = 4cos θ   .....[Given]

∴ `(sintheta)/(costheta) = 4/3`

∴ tan θ = `4/3`

We know that,

1 + tan2θ = sec2θ

∴  `1 + (4/3)^2` = sec2θ

∴ `1 + 16/9` = sec2θ

∴ sec2θ = `(9 + 16)/9`

∴ sec2θ = `25/9`

∴ sec θ = `5/3`   ......[Taking square root of both sides]

items.title: If 3 sin A + 5 cos A = 5, then show that 5 sin A – 3 cos A = ± 3 - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: If 3 sin A + 5 cos A = 5, then show that 5 sin A – 3 cos A = ± 3
items.solution_text: 3 sin A + 5 cos A = 5 ....[Given] ∴ (3 sin A + 5 cos A) 2 = 25 ......[Squaring both the sides] ∴ 9 sin 2 A + 30 sin A cos A + 25 cos 2 A = 25 ∴ 9(1 – cos 2 A) + 30 sin A cos A + 25(1 – sin 2 A) = 25 ∴ 9 – 9 cos 2 A + 30 sin A cos A + 25 – 25 sin 2 A = 25 ∴ 25 sin 2 A – 30 sin A cos A + 9 cos 2 A = 9 ∴ (5 sin A – 3 cos A) 2 = 9 ......[∵ a 2 – 2ab + b 2 = (a – b) 2 ] ∴ 5 sin A – 3 cos A = ± 3 .....[Taking square root of both sides]
items.solution_html:
3 sin A + 5 cos A = 5   ....[Given]

∴ (3 sin A + 5 cos A)2 = 25   ......[Squaring both the sides]

∴ 9 sin2A + 30 sin A cos A + 25 cos2A = 25

∴ 9(1 – cos2A) + 30 sin A cos A + 25(1 – sin2A) = 25

∴ 9 – 9 cos2A + 30 sin A cos A + 25 – 25 sin2A = 25

∴ 25 sin2A – 30 sin A cos A + 9 cos2A = 9

∴ (5 sin A – 3 cos A)2 = 9   ......[∵ a2 – 2ab + b2 = (a – b)2]

∴ 5 sin A – 3 cos A = ± 3   .....[Taking square root of both sides]

items.title: If 5 sec θ – 12 cosec θ = 0, then find values of sin θ, sec θ - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: If 5 sec θ – 12 cosec θ = 0, then find values of sin θ, sec θ
items.solution_text: 5 sec θ – 12 cosec θ = 0 ......[Given] ∴ 5 sec θ = 12 cosec θ ∴ `5/costheta = 12/sintheta` ......`[because sectheta = 1/costheta, "cosec" theta = 1/sintheta]` ∴ `sintheta/costheta = 12/5` ∴ tan θ = `12/5` We know that, 1 + tan 2 θ = sec 2 θ ∴ `1 + (12/5)^2` = sec 2 θ ∴ `1 + 144/25` = sec 2 θ ∴ `(25 + 144)/25` = sec 2 θ ∴ sec 2 θ = `169/25` ∴ secθ = `13/5` ......[Taking square root of both sides] Now, cos θ = `1/sectheta` = `1/((13/5))` ∴ cos θ = `5/13` We know that, sin 2 θ + cos 2 θ = 1 ∴ `sin^2theta + (5/13)^2` = 1 ∴ `sin^2theta + 25/169` = 1 ∴ sec 2 θ = `1 - 25/169` ∴ sec 2 θ = `(169 - 25)/169` ∴ sec 2 θ = `144/169` ∴ sin θ = `12/13` ......[Taking square root of both sides] ∴ sin θ = `12/13`, sec θ = `13/5`.
items.solution_html:
5 sec θ – 12 cosec θ = 0    ......[Given]

∴ 5 sec θ = 12 cosec θ

∴ `5/costheta = 12/sintheta`    ......`[because sectheta = 1/costheta, "cosec"  theta = 1/sintheta]`

∴ `sintheta/costheta = 12/5`

∴ tan θ = `12/5`

We know that,

1 + tan2θ = sec2θ

∴ `1 + (12/5)^2` = sec2θ

∴ `1 + 144/25` = sec2θ

∴ `(25 + 144)/25` = sec2θ

∴ sec2θ = `169/25`

∴ secθ = `13/5`   ......[Taking square root of both sides]

Now, cos θ = `1/sectheta`

= `1/((13/5))`

∴ cos θ = `5/13`

We know that,

sin2θ + cos2θ = 1

∴ `sin^2theta + (5/13)^2` = 1

∴ `sin^2theta + 25/169` = 1

∴ sec2θ = `1 - 25/169`

∴ sec2θ = `(169 - 25)/169`

∴ sec2θ = `144/169`

∴ sin θ = `12/13` ......[Taking square root of both sides]

∴ sin θ = `12/13`, sec θ = `13/5`.

items.title: If A(5, 4), B(–3, –2) and C(1, –8) are the vertices of a ∆ABC. Segment AD is median. Find the length of seg AD: - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: If A(5, 4), B(–3, –2) and C(1, –8) are the vertices of a ∆ABC. Segment AD is median. Find the length of seg AD:
items.solution_text: Since segment AD is median, Point D is the midpoint of side BC. By midpoint formula, Co-ordinates of D = `((x_1 + x_2)/2, (y_1 + y_2)/2)` = `((-3 + 1)/2, (-2 - 8)/2)` = `((-2)/2, (-10)/2)` Co-ordinates of D = (–1, – 5) Distance between two points = `sqrt((x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2` By distance formula, d(A, D) = `sqrt([5 - (-1)]^2 + [4 - (-5)]^2` = `sqrt((5 + 1)^2 + (4 + 5)^2` = `sqrt(6^2 + 9^2)` = `sqrt(36 + 81)` = `sqrt(117)` ∴ The length of segment AD is `sqrt(117)` units.
items.solution_html:
Since segment AD is median, Point D is the midpoint of side BC.

By midpoint formula,

Co-ordinates of D = `((x_1 + x_2)/2, (y_1 + y_2)/2)`

= `((-3 + 1)/2, (-2 - 8)/2)`

= `((-2)/2, (-10)/2)`

Co-ordinates of D = (–1, – 5)

Distance between two points = `sqrt((x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2`

By distance formula,

d(A, D) = `sqrt([5 - (-1)]^2 + [4 - (-5)]^2`

= `sqrt((5 + 1)^2 + (4 + 5)^2`

= `sqrt(6^2 + 9^2)`

= `sqrt(36 + 81)`

= `sqrt(117)`

∴ The length of segment AD is `sqrt(117)` units.

items.title: If a triangle having sides 50 cm, 14 cm and 48 cm, then state wheather given triangle is right angled triangle or not. - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: If a triangle having sides 50 cm, 14 cm and 48 cm, then state whether given triangle is right angled triangle or not
items.solution_text: The sides of the triangle are 50 cm, 14 cm and 48 cm. The longest side of the triangle is 50 cm. ∴ (50) 2 = 2500 Now, sum of the squares of the remaining sides is, (14) 2 + (48) 2 = 196 + 2304 = 2500 ∴ (50) 2 = (14) 2 + (48) 2 ∴ Square of the longest side is equal to the sum of the squares of the remaining two sides. ∴ The given sides will form a right angled triangle. .......[Converse of Pythagoras theorem]
items.solution_html:
The sides of the triangle are 50 cm, 14 cm and 48 cm.

The longest side of the triangle is 50 cm.

∴ (50)2 = 2500

Now, sum of the squares of the remaining sides is,

(14)2 + (48)2 = 196 + 2304 = 2500

∴ (50)2 = (14)2 + (48)2

∴ Square of the longest side is equal to the sum of the squares of the remaining two sides.

∴ The given sides will form a right angled triangle. .......[Converse of Pythagoras theorem]

items.title: If a triangle having sides 8 cm, 15 cm and 17 cm, then state wheather given triangle is right angled triangle or not - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: If a triangle having sides 8 cm, 15 cm and 17 cm, then state whether given triangle is right angled triangle or not
items.solution_text: The sides of the triangle are 8 cm, 15 cm, and 17 cm. The longest side of the triangle is 17 cm. ∴ (17) 2 = 289 Now, sum of the squares of the remaining sides is, (8) 2 + (15) 2 = 64 + 225 = 289 ∴ (17) 2 = (8) 2 + (15) 2 ∴ Square of the longest side is equal to the sum of the squares of the remaining two sides. ∴ The given sides will form a right-angled triangle. ......[Converse of Pythagoras theorem]
items.solution_html:
The sides of the triangle are 8 cm, 15 cm, and 17 cm.

The longest side of the triangle is 17 cm.

∴ (17)2 = 289

Now, sum of the squares of the remaining sides is,

(8)2 + (15)2 = 64 + 225 = 289

∴ (17)2 = (8)2 + (15)2

∴ Square of the longest side is equal to the sum of the squares of the remaining two sides.

∴ The given sides will form a right-angled triangle.  ......[Converse of Pythagoras theorem]

items.title: If AB and CD are the common tangents in the circles of two unequal (different) radii, then show that seg AB ≅ seg CD. - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: If AB and CD are the common tangents in the circles of two unequal (different) radii, then show that seg AB ≅ seg CD.
items.solution_text: Given: AB and CD are tangents to both circles. To prove: seg AB ≅ seg CD Construction: Extend seg AB and seg CD to intersect each other at point E, such that A – B – E, C – D – E. Proof: In above figure, `{:("AE" = "CE"),("BE" = "DE"):}}` ......(i) [Tangent segment theorem] Consider, AE = CE ∴ AB + BE = CD + DE ......[A – B – E, C – D – E] ∴ AB + DE = CD + DE ......[From (i)] ∴ AB = CD ∴ Seg AB ≅ seg CD
items.solution_html:
Given: AB and CD are tangents to both circles.

To prove: seg AB ≅ seg CD

Construction: Extend seg AB and seg CD to intersect each other at point E, such that A – B – E, C – D – E.

Proof: In above figure,

`{:("AE" = "CE"),("BE" = "DE"):}}`  ......(i) [Tangent segment theorem]

Consider, AE = CE

∴ AB + BE = CD + DE     ......[A – B – E, C – D – E]

∴ AB + DE = CD + DE    ......[From (i)]

∴ AB = CD

∴ Seg AB ≅ seg CD

items.title: If ∆ABC ~ ∆PQR and AB : PQ = 3 : 4 then A(∆ABC) : A(∆PQR) = ? - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: If ∆ABC ~ ∆PQR and AB : PQ = 3 : 4 then A(∆ABC) : A(∆PQR) = ?
items.solution_text: 9: 16 In ∆ABC and ∆PQR, ∆ABC ~ ∆PQR AB : PQ = 3 : 4 ...(Given) by theorem of areas of similar triangles, `"A(∆ABC)"/"A(∆PQR)" = ("AB"^2)/("PQ"^2)` `"A(∆ABC)"/"A(∆PQR)" = 3^2/4^2` `"A(∆ABC)"/"A(∆PQR)" = 9/16`. ∴ A(∆ABC) : A(∆PQR) = 9 : 16.
items.solution_html:
9: 16

In ∆ABC and ∆PQR,

∆ABC ~ ∆PQR

AB : PQ = 3 : 4    ...(Given)

by theorem of areas of similar triangles,

`"A(∆ABC)"/"A(∆PQR)" = ("AB"^2)/("PQ"^2)`

`"A(∆ABC)"/"A(∆PQR)" = 3^2/4^2`

`"A(∆ABC)"/"A(∆PQR)" = 9/16`.

∴ A(∆ABC) :  A(∆PQR) = 9 : 16.

items.title: If cos θ = 2425, then sin θ = ? - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: If cos θ = `24/25`, then sin θ = ?
items.solution_text: cos θ = `24/25` ......[Given] We know that, sin 2 θ + cos 2 θ = 1 ∴ `sin^2theta + (24/25)^2` = 1 ∴ `sin^2theta + 576/625` = 1 ∴ sin 2 θ = `1 - 576/625` ∴ sin 2 θ = `(625 - 576)/625` ∴ sin 2 θ = `49/625` ∴ sin θ = `7/25` ......[Taking square root of both sides]
items.solution_html:
cos θ = `24/25`  ......[Given]

We know that,

sin2θ + cos2θ = 1

∴ `sin^2theta + (24/25)^2` = 1

∴ `sin^2theta + 576/625` = 1

∴ sin2θ = `1 - 576/625`

∴ sin2θ = `(625 - 576)/625`

∴ sin2θ = `49/625`

∴ sin θ = `7/25`    ......[Taking square root of both sides]

∴ cos(45° + x) = cos 60°   .....[∵ sin (90° – θ) = cos θ]

∴ 45° + x = 60°

∴ x = 60° – 45°

∴ x = 15°

items.solution_text: cos A + cos 2 A = 1 ......[Given] ∴ cos A = 1 – cos 2 A ∴ cos A = sin 2 A ......`[(because sin^2"A" + cos^2"A" = 1),(therefore 1 - cos^2"A" = sin^2"A")]` ∴ cos 2 A = sin 4 A .....[Squaring both the sides] ∴ 1 – sin 2 A = sin 4 A ∴ 1 = sin 4 A + sin 2 A ∴ sin 2 A + sin 4 A = 1
items.solution_html:
cos A + cos2A = 1   ......[Given]

∴ cos A = sin2A    ......`[(because sin^2"A" + cos^2"A" = 1),(therefore 1 - cos^2"A" = sin^2"A")]`

∴ 1 – sin2A = sin4A

∴ 1 = sin4A + sin2A

∴ sin2A + sin4A = 1

items.title: If point P(1, 1) divide segment joining point A and point B(–1, –1) in the ratio 5 : 2, then the coordinates of A are ______ - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: If point P(1, 1) divide segment joining point A and point B(–1, –1) in the ratio 5 : 2, then the coordinates of A are ______
items.solution_html:
(6, 6)

Let A(x1, y1) and B(x2, y2) = B(-1, -1)

P(x, y) = P(1, 1) divides AB in ratio 5 : 2.

∴ x1 = 1, y1 = 1, x2 = –1, y2 = –1, a = 5, b = 2.

∴ By section formula,

∴ x = `(ax_2 + bx_1)/(a + b)`

∴ `1 = (5(-1) + 2x_1)/(5 + 2)`

∴  7 = -5 + 2x1

∴ 2x1 = 7 + 5

∴ 2x1 = 12

∴ x1 = `12/2`

∴ x1 = 6

∴ Co-ordinates of A are (6, 6).

items.title: If point P divides segment AB in the ratio 1 : 3 where A(– 5, 3) and B(3, – 5), then the coordinates of P are ______ - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: If point P divides segment AB in the ratio 1 : 3 where A(– 5, 3) and B(3, – 5), then the coordinates of P are ______
items.solution_html:
(– 3, 1)

Let A(x1, y1) = A(-5, 3) and B(x2, y2) = B(3, -5),

a : b = 1 : 3

∴ x1 = -5, y1 = 3, x2 = 3, y2 = –5, a = 1, b = 3.

∴ By section formula,

∴ x = `(ax_2 + bx_1)/(a + b)`

∴ x = `(1(3) + 3(-5))/(1 + 3)`

∴ x = `(3 - 15)/(4)`

∴ x = `(- 12)/(4)`

∴ x = -3

∴ y = `(ay_2 + by_1)/(a + b)`

∴ y = `(1(-5) + 3(3))/(1 + 3)`

∴ y = `(-5 + 9)/(4)`

∴ y = `(4)/(4)`

∴ y = 1

∴ Co-ordinates of P are (-3, 1).

items.title: If point P is midpoint of segment joining point A(– 4, 2) and point B(6, 2), then the coordinates of P are ______ - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: If point P is midpoint of segment joining point A(– 4, 2) and point B(6, 2), then the coordinates of P are ______
items.solution_html:
(1, 2)

A(x1, y1) = A( –4, 2), B(x2, y2) = B(6, 2)

Here, x1 = – 4, y1 = 2, x2 = 6, y2 = 2

∴ Co-ordinates of the midpoint of seg AB

= `((x_1 + x_2)/2, (y_1 + y_2)/2)`

= `((-4 + 6)/2, (2 + 2)/2)`

= `(2/2, 4/2)`

= (1, 2)

items.title: If sec θ = 4140, then find values of sin θ, cot θ, cosec θ - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: If sec θ = `41/40`, then find values of sin θ, cot θ, cosec θ
items.solution_text: sec θ = `41/40` ......[Given] ∴ cos θ = `1/sectheta = 1/(41/40)` ∴ cos θ = `40/41` We know that, sin 2 θ + cos 2 θ = 1 ∴ `sin^2theta + (40/41)^2` = 1 ∴ `sin^2theta + 1600/1681` = 1 ∴ sin 2 θ = `1 - 1600/1681` ∴ sin 2 θ = `(1681- 1600)/1681` ∴ sin 2 θ = `81/1681` ∴ sin θ = `9/41` .......[Taking square root of both sides] Now, cosec θ = `1/sintheta` = `1/((9/41))` = `41/9` cot θ = `costheta/sintheta` = `((40/41))/((9/41))` = `40/9` ∴ sin θ = `9/41`, cot θ = `40/9`, cosec θ = `41/9`
items.solution_html:
sec θ = `41/40`     ......[Given]

∴ cos θ = `1/sectheta = 1/(41/40)`

∴ cos θ = `40/41`

We know that,

sin2θ + cos2θ = 1

∴ `sin^2theta + (40/41)^2` = 1

∴ `sin^2theta + 1600/1681` = 1

∴ sin2θ = `1 - 1600/1681`

∴ sin2θ = `(1681- 1600)/1681`

∴ sin2θ = `81/1681`

∴ sin θ = `9/41`   .......[Taking square root of both sides]

Now, cosec θ = `1/sintheta`

= `1/((9/41))`

= `41/9`

cot θ = `costheta/sintheta`

= `((40/41))/((9/41))`

= `40/9`

∴ sin θ = `9/41`, cot θ = `40/9`, cosec θ = `41/9`

items.title: If sec A = x+14x, then show that sec A + tan A = 2x or 12x - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: If sec A = `x + 1/(4x)`, then show that sec A + tan A = 2x or `1/(2x)`

items.title: If sec θ + tan θ = 3, complete the activity to find the value of sec θ – tan θ Activity: □ = 1 + tan2θ ......[Fundamental trigonometric identity] □ – tan2θ = 1 (sec θ + tan θ) . (sec θ – tan θ) = □ - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: If sec θ + tan θ = `sqrt(3)`, complete the activity to find the value of sec θ – tan θ Activity: `square` = 1 + tan 2 θ ......[Fundamental trigonometric identity] `square` – tan 2 θ = 1 (sec θ + tan θ) . (sec θ – tan θ) = `square` `sqrt(3)*(sectheta - tan theta)` = 1 (sec θ – tan θ) = `square`
items.solution_text: sec 2 θ = 1 + tan 2 θ ......[Fundamental trigonometric identity] sec 2 θ – tan 2 θ = 1 (sec θ + tan θ) . (sec θ – tan θ) = 1 `sqrt(3)*(sectheta - tan theta)` = 1 (sec θ – tan θ) = `1/sqrt(3)`
items.solution_html:
sec2θ = 1 + tan2θ    ......[Fundamental trigonometric identity]

sec2θ – tan2θ = 1

(sec θ + tan θ) . (sec θ – tan θ) = 1

`sqrt(3)*(sectheta - tan theta)` = 1

(sec θ – tan θ) = `1/sqrt(3)`

items.title: If segment AB is parallel Y-axis and coordinates of A are (1, 3), then the coordinates of B are ______ - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: If segment AB is parallel Y-axis and coordinates of A are (1, 3), then the coordinates of B are ______
items.solution_text: (1, – 3) Since, seg AB || Y-axis. ∴ x co-ordinate of all points on seg AB will be the same. x co-ordinate of A (1, 3) = 1 x co-ordinate of B (1, – 3) = 1 ∴ (1, – 3) is option correct.
items.solution_html:
(1, – 3) 

Since, seg AB || Y-axis.

∴ x co-ordinate of all points on seg AB will be the same.

x co-ordinate of A (1, 3) = 1

x co-ordinate of B (1, – 3) = 1

∴ (1, – 3) is option correct.

items.title: If sin 3A = cos 6A, then ∠A = ? - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: If sin 3A = cos 6A, then ∠A = ?
items.solution_html:
sin 3A = cos 6A     .....[Given]

∴ sin 3A = sin(90° – 6A)  .....[∵ cos θ = sin(90° – θ)]

∴ 3A = 90° – 6A

∴ 3A + 6A = 90°

∴ 9A = 90°

∴ A = `(90^circ)/9`

∴ A = 10°

items.title: If sin A = 35 then show that 4 tan A + 3 sin A = 6 cos A - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: If sin A = `3/5` then show that 4 tan A + 3 sin A = 6 cos A
items.solution_text: sin A = `3/5` ...(i) [Given] In ∆ABC, Let ∠ABC = 90° ∴ sin A = `"BC"/"AC"` .....(ii) [By definition] ∴ `"BC"/"AC" = 3/5` ......[From (i) and (ii)] Let BC = 3k, AC = 5k In ∆ABC, ∠B = 90° ∴ AB 2 + BC 2 = AC 2 ......[Pythagoras theorem] ∴ AB 2 + (3k) 2 = (5k) 2 ∴ AB 2 + 9k 2 = 25k 2 ∴ AB 2 = 25k 2 – 9k 2 ∴ AB 2 = 16k 2 ∴ AB = 4k ......[Taking square root of both sides] Now, tan A = `"BC"/"AB"` ......[By definition] ∴ tan A = `(3"k")/(4"k") = 3/4` cos A = `"AB"/"AC"` ......[By definition] ∴ cos A = `(4"k")/(5"k") = 4/5` ∴ 4 tan A + 3 sin A = `4(3/4) + 3(3/5)` = `3 + 9/5` =`(15 + 9)/5` = `24/5` ......(iii) 6cos A = `6(4/5) = 24/5` ......(iv) ∴ 4 tan A + 3 sin A = 6 cos A .....[From (iii) and (iv)]
items.solution_html:
sin A = `3/5`   ...(i) [Given]

In ∆ABC,

Let ∠ABC = 90°

∴ sin A = `"BC"/"AC"`    .....(ii) [By definition]

∴ `"BC"/"AC" = 3/5`   ......[From (i) and (ii)]

Let BC = 3k, AC = 5k

In ∆ABC, ∠B = 90°

∴ AB2 + BC2 = AC2    ......[Pythagoras theorem]

∴ AB2 + (3k)2 = (5k)2

∴ AB2 + 9k2 = 25k2

∴ AB2 = 25k2 – 9k2

∴ AB2 = 16k2 

∴ AB = 4k    ......[Taking square root of both sides]

Now, tan A = `"BC"/"AB"`  ......[By definition]

∴ tan A = `(3"k")/(4"k") = 3/4`

cos A = `"AB"/"AC"`   ......[By definition]

∴ cos A = `(4"k")/(5"k") = 4/5`

∴ 4 tan A + 3 sin A = `4(3/4) + 3(3/5)`

= `3 + 9/5`

=`(15 + 9)/5`

= `24/5`   ......(iii)

6cos A = `6(4/5) = 24/5`   ......(iv)

∴ 4 tan A + 3 sin A = 6 cos A    .....[From (iii) and (iv)]

items.title: If sin θ + cos θ = 3, then show that tan θ + cot θ = 1 - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: If sin θ + cos θ = `sqrt(3)`, then show that tan θ + cot θ = 1
items.solution_text: sin θ + cos θ = `sqrt(3)` ......[Given] ∴ (sin θ + cos θ) 2 = 3 ......[Squaring on both sides] ∴ sin 2 θ + 2sinθ cosθ + cos 2 θ = 3 ......[∵ (a + b) 2 = a 2 + 2ab + b 2 ] ∴ (sin 2 θ + cos 2 θ) + 2sinθ cosθ = 3 ∴ 1 + 2 sin θ cos θ = 3 ......[∵ sin 2 θ + cos 2 θ = 1] ∴ 2 sin θ cos θ = 2 ∴ sin θ cos θ = 1 ......(i) tan θ + cot θ = `sintheta/costheta + costheta/sintheta` = `(sin^2theta + cos^2theta)/(costhetasintheta)` = `1/(sintheta costheta)` ......[∵ sin 2 θ + cos 2 θ = 1] = `1/1` ......[From (i)] = 1
items.solution_html:
sin θ + cos θ = `sqrt(3)`     ......[Given]

∴ (sin θ + cos θ)2 = 3    ......[Squaring on both sides]

∴ sin2θ + 2sinθ cosθ + cos2θ = 3  ......[∵ (a + b)2 = a2 + 2ab + b2]

∴ (sin2θ + cos2θ) + 2sinθ cosθ = 3

∴ 1 + 2 sin θ cos θ = 3   ......[∵ sin2θ + cos2θ = 1]

∴ 2 sin θ cos θ = 2

∴ sin θ cos θ = 1   ......(i)

tan θ + cot θ = `sintheta/costheta + costheta/sintheta`

= `(sin^2theta + cos^2theta)/(costhetasintheta)`

= `1/(sintheta costheta)`   ......[∵ sin2θ + cos2θ = 1]

= `1/1`    ......[From (i)]

= 1

items.title: If tan θ = 1, then sin θ . cos θ = ? - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: If tan θ = 1, then sin θ . cos θ = ?
items.solution_html:
tan θ = 1    ......[Given]

∴ θ = 45°     ......[∵ tan45° = 1]

∴ sin θ . cos θ = sin 45° cos 45°

= `1/sqrt(2)*1/sqrt(2)`

= `1/2`

items.title: If tan θ = 1312, then cot θ = ? - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: If tan θ = `13/12`, then cot θ = ?
items.solution_html:
cot θ = `1/tantheta`

= `1/(13/12)`

∴ cot θ = `12/13`

items.title: If tan θ = 724, then to find value of cos θ complete the activity given below. Activity: sec2θ = 1 + □ ......[Fundamental tri. identity] sec2θ = 1 + □2 sec2θ = 1 + □576 sec2θ = □576 sec θ = □ cos θ - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: If tan θ = `7/24`, then to find value of cos θ complete the activity given below. Activity: sec 2 θ = 1 + `square` ......[Fundamental tri. identity] sec 2 θ = 1 + `square^2` sec 2 θ = 1 + `square/576` sec 2 θ = `square/576` sec θ = `square` cos θ = `square` .......`[cos theta = 1/sectheta]`
items.solution_text: sec 2 θ = 1 + tan 2 θ ......[Fundamental tri. identity] ∴ sec 2 θ = 1 + `(7/24)^2` ∴ sec 2 θ = 1 + `49/576` ∴ sec 2 θ =`(576 + 49)/576` ∴ sec 2 θ = `625/576` ∴ sec θ = `25/24` ∴ cos θ = `24/25` .......`[cos theta = 1/sectheta]`
items.solution_html:
sec2θ = 1 + tan2θ    ......[Fundamental tri. identity]

∴ sec2θ = 1 + `(7/24)^2`

∴ sec2θ = 1 + `49/576`

∴ sec2θ =`(576 + 49)/576`

∴ sec2θ = `625/576`

∴ sec θ = `25/24`

∴ cos θ = `24/25`     .......`[cos theta = 1/sectheta]`

items.title: If tan θ = 940, complete the activity to find the value of sec θ. Activity: sec2θ = 1 + □ ......[Fundamental trigonometric identity] sec2θ = 1 + □2 sec2θ = 1 + □ sec θ = □ - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: If tan θ = `9/40`, complete the activity to find the value of sec θ. Activity: sec 2 θ = 1 + `square` ......[Fundamental trigonometric identity] sec 2 θ = 1 + `square^2` sec 2 θ = 1 + `square` sec θ = `square`
items.solution_text: sec 2 θ = 1 + tan 2 θ ......[Fundamental trigonometric identity] ∴ sec 2 θ = 1 + `(9/40)^2` ∴ sec 2 θ = 1 + `81/1600` ∴ sec 2 θ = `1681/1600` ∴ sec θ = `41/40`
items.solution_html:
sec2θ = 1 + tan2θ     ......[Fundamental trigonometric identity]

∴ sec2θ = 1 + `(9/40)^2`

∴ sec2θ = 1 + `81/1600` 

∴ sec2θ = `1681/1600`

∴ sec θ = `41/40`

items.title: If tan θ × A = sin θ, then A = ? - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: If tan θ × A = sin θ, then A = ?
items.solution_text: tan θ × A = sin θ .....[Given] ∴ `(sin theta)/(cos theta) xx "A"` = sin θ ∴`1/(cos theta) xx "A"` = 1 ∴ A = cos θ
items.solution_html:
tan θ × A = sin θ    .....[Given]

∴ `(sin theta)/(cos theta) xx "A"` = sin θ

∴`1/(cos theta) xx "A"` = 1

∴ A = cos θ

items.title: If tan θ + cot θ = 2, then tan2θ + cot2θ = ? - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: If tan θ + cot θ = 2, then tan 2 θ + cot 2 θ = ?
items.solution_text: tan θ + cot θ = 2 ....[Given] ∴ (tan θ + cot θ) 2 = 4 .....[Squaring both sides] ∴ tan 2 θ + 2tan θ.cot θ + cot 2 θ = 4 ......[∵ (a + b) 2 = a 2 + 2ab + b 2 ] ∴ tan 2 θ + 2(1) + cot 2 θ = 4 ......[∵ tan θ ⋅ cot θ = 1] ∴ tan 2 θ + cot 2 θ = 4 – 2 ∴ tan 2 θ + cot 2 θ = 2
items.solution_html:
tan θ + cot θ = 2   ....[Given]

∴ (tan θ + cot θ)2 = 4    .....[Squaring both sides]

∴ tan2θ + 2tan θ.cot θ + cot2θ = 4   ......[∵ (a + b)2 = a2 + 2ab + b2]

∴ tan2θ + 2(1) + cot2θ = 4    ......[∵ tan θ ⋅ cot θ = 1]

∴ tan2θ + cot2θ = 4 – 2

∴ tan2θ + cot2θ = 2

items.title: If tan θ – sin2θ = cos2θ, then show that sin2 θ = 12 - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: If tan θ – sin 2 θ = cos 2 θ, then show that sin 2 θ = `1/2`.
items.solution_text: tan θ – sin 2 θ = cos 2 θ ......[Given] ∴ tan θ = sin 2 θ + cos 2 θ ∴ tan θ = 1 ....[∵ sin 2 θ + cos 2 θ = 1] But, tan 45° = 1 ∴ tan θ = tan 45° ∴ θ = 45° sin 2 θ = sin 2 45° = `(1/sqrt(2))^2` = `1/2`
items.solution_html:
tan θ – sin2θ = cos2θ   ......[Given]

∴ tan θ = sin2θ + cos2θ

∴ tan θ = 1    ....[∵ sin2θ + cos2θ = 1]

But, tan 45° = 1

∴ tan θ = tan 45°

∴ θ = 45°

sin2θ = sin245°

= `(1/sqrt(2))^2`

= `1/2`

items.title: If the distance between point L(x, 7) and point M(1, 15) is 10, then find the value of x - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: If the distance between point L(x, 7) and point M(1, 15) is 10, then find the value of x
items.solution_text: Let L(x 1 , y 1 ) = L(x, 7) and M (x 2 , y 2 ) = M(1, 15) x 1 = x, y 1 = 7, x 2 = 1, y 2 = 15 By distance formula, d(L, M) = `sqrt((x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2` ∴ d(L, M) = `sqrt((1 - x)^2 + (15 - 7)^2` ∴ 10 = `sqrt((1 - x)^2 + 8^2` ∴ 100 = (1 – x) 2 + 64 ......[Squaring both sides] ∴ (1 – x) 2 = 100 – 64 ∴ (1 – x) 2 = 36 ∴ 1 – x = `+- sqrt(36)` .....[Taking square root of both sides] ∴ 1 – x = ± 6 ∴ 1 – x = 6 or 1 – x = – 6 ∴ x = – 5 or x = 7 ∴ The value of x is – 5 or 7.
items.solution_html:
Let L(x1, y1) = L(x, 7) and M (x2, y2) = M(1, 15)

x1 = x, y1 = 7, x2 = 1, y2 = 15

By distance formula,

d(L, M) = `sqrt((x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2`

∴ d(L, M) = `sqrt((1 - x)^2 + (15 - 7)^2`

∴ 10 = `sqrt((1 - x)^2 + 8^2`

∴ 100 = (1 – x)2 + 64    ......[Squaring both sides]

∴ (1 – x)2 = 100 – 64

∴ (1 – x)2 = 36

∴ 1 – x = `+-  sqrt(36)`  .....[Taking square root of both sides]

∴ 1 – x = ± 6

∴ 1 – x = 6 or 1 – x = – 6

∴ x = – 5 or x = 7

∴ The value of x is – 5 or 7.

items.title: If the length of the segment joining point L(x, 7) and point M(1, 15) is 10 cm, then the value of x is ______ - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: If the length of the segment joining point L(x, 7) and point M(1, 15) is 10 cm, then the value of x is ______
items.solution_text: 7 or – 5 Here, x 1 = x, y 1 = 7, x 2 = 1, y 2 = 15 By distance formula, d(L, M) = `sqrt((x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2)` ∴ d(L, M) = `sqrt((1 - x)^2 + (15 - 7)^2)` ∴ 10 = `sqrt((1 - x)^2 + 8^2)` ∴ 100 = (1 - x) 2 + 64 ...[Squaring both sides] ∴ (1 - x) 2 = 100 - 64 ∴ (1 - x) 2 = 36 ∴ 1 - x = `+-sqrt(36)` ...[Taking square root of both sides] ∴ 1 - x = `+-6` ∴ 1 - x = 6 or 1 - x = -6 ∴ x = -5 or x = 7 ∴ The value of x is -5 or 7.
items.solution_html:
7 or – 5

Here, x1 = x, y1 = 7, x2 = 1, y2 = 15

By distance formula,

d(L, M) = `sqrt((x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2)`

∴ d(L, M) = `sqrt((1 - x)^2 + (15 - 7)^2)`

∴ 10 = `sqrt((1 - x)^2 + 8^2)`

∴ 100 = (1 - x)2 + 64      ...[Squaring both sides]

∴ (1 - x)2 = 100 - 64

∴ (1 - x)2 = 36

∴ 1 - x = `+-sqrt(36)`  ...[Taking square root of both sides]

∴ 1 - x = `+-6`

∴ 1 - x = 6 or 1 - x = -6

∴ x = -5 or x = 7

∴ The value of x is -5 or 7.

items.title: If the point P (6, 7) divides the segment joining A(8, 9) and B(1, 2) in some ratio, find that ratio Solution: Point P divides segment AB in the ratio m : n. A(8, 9) = (x1, y1), B(1 - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: If the point P (6, 7) divides the segment joining A(8, 9) and B(1, 2) in some ratio, find that ratio Solution: Point P divides segment AB in the ratio m: n. A(8, 9) = (x 1 , y 1 ), B(1, 2 ) = (x 2 , y 2 ) and P(6, 7) = (x, y) Using Section formula of internal division, ∴ 7 = `("m"(square) - "n"(9))/("m" + "n")` ∴ 7m + 7n = `square` + 9n ∴ 7m – `square` = 9n – `square` ∴ `square` = 2n ∴ `"m"/"n" = square`
items.solution_text: Point P divides segment AB in the ratio m : n. A(8, 9) = (x 1 , y 1 ), B(1, 2 ) = (x 2 , y 2 ) and P(6, 7) = (x, y) Using Section formula of internal division, y = `("m"y_2 + "n"y_1)/("m" + "n")` ∴ 7 = `("m"(2) - "n"(9))/("m" + "n")` ∴ 7m + 7n = 2m + 9n ∴ 7m – 2m = 9n – 7n ∴ 5m = 2n ∴ `"m"/"n"` = `bb(2/5)`
items.solution_html:
Point P divides segment AB in the ratio m : n.

A(8, 9) = (x1, y1), B(1, 2 ) = (x2, y2) and P(6, 7) = (x, y)

Using Section formula of internal division,

y = `("m"y_2 + "n"y_1)/("m" + "n")`

∴ 7 = `("m"(2) - "n"(9))/("m" + "n")`

∴ 7m + 7n = 2m + 9n

∴ 7m – 2m = 9n – 7n

∴ 5m = 2n

∴ `"m"/"n"` = `bb(2/5)`

items.title: If the sum of X-coordinates of the vertices of a triangle is 12 and the sum of Y-coordinates is 9, then the coordinates of centroid are - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: If the sum of X-coordinates of the vertices of a triangle is 12 and the sum of Y-coordinates is 9, then the coordinates of centroid are ______
items.solution_text: (4, 3) Let P(x 1 , y 1 ), Q(x 2 , y 2 ), R(x 3 , y 3 ) be the co-ordinates of the vertices of a triangle. Given that x 1 + x 2 + x 3 = 12 and y 1 + y 2 + y 3 = 9 ∴ Co-ordinates of the centroid S(x, y) are `x = (x_1 + x_2 + x_3)/3 "and" y = (y_1 + y_2 + y_3)/3` `x = (12)/3 "and" y = (9)/3` `x = 4 "and" y = 3` ∴ S(x, y) = (4, 3).
items.solution_html:
(4, 3)

Let P(x1, y1), Q(x2, y2), R(x3, y3) be the co-ordinates of the vertices of a triangle.

Given that x1 + x2 + x3 = 12 and y1 + y2 + y3 = 9

∴ Co-ordinates of the centroid S(x, y) are

`x = (x_1 + x_2 + x_3)/3  "and"  y = (y_1 + y_2 + y_3)/3`

`x = (12)/3  "and"  y = (9)/3`

`x = 4  "and"  y = 3`

∴ S(x, y) = (4, 3).

items.title: If ∆XYZ ~ ∆PQR and A(∆XYZ) = 25 cm2, A(∆PQR) = 4 cm2 then XY : PQ = ? - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: If ∆XYZ ~ ∆PQR and A(∆XYZ) = 25 cm 2 , A(∆PQR) = 4 cm 2 then XY : PQ = ?
items.solution_text: 5: 2 In ∆XYZ and ∆PQR, ∆XYZ ~ ∆PQR A(∆XYZ) = 25 cm 2 , A(∆PQR) = 4 cm 2 ...(Given) by the theorem of areas of similar triangles, `"A(∆XYZ)"/"A(∆PQR)" = "XY"^2/"PQ"^2` ∴ `25/4 = "XY"^2/"PQ"^2` ∴ `"XY"/"PQ" = 5/2` ∴ XY : PQ = 5 : 2.
items.solution_html:
5: 2

In ∆XYZ and ∆PQR,

∆XYZ ~ ∆PQR  

A(∆XYZ) = 25 cm2, A(∆PQR) = 4 cm2   ...(Given)

by the theorem of areas of similar triangles,

`"A(∆XYZ)"/"A(∆PQR)" = "XY"^2/"PQ"^2`

∴ `25/4 = "XY"^2/"PQ"^2`

∴ `"XY"/"PQ" = 5/2`

∴ XY : PQ = 5 : 2.

items.title: If ΔXYZ ~ ΔPQR then XYPQYZQRXYPQ=YZQR = ? - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: If ΔXYZ ~ ΔPQR then `"XY"/"PQ" = "YZ"/"QR"` = ?
items.solution_text: `"XZ"/"PR"` In ΔXYZ and ΔPQR, ΔXYZ ~ ΔPQR ...(Given) `"XY"/"PQ" = "YZ"/"QR" = "XZ"/"PR"` ...[Corresponding sides of similar triangles.]
items.solution_html:
`"XZ"/"PR"`

In ΔXYZ and ΔPQR,

ΔXYZ ~ ΔPQR    ...(Given)

`"XY"/"PQ" = "YZ"/"QR" = "XZ"/"PR"`   ...[Corresponding sides of similar triangles.]

items.title: In a circle with centre P, chord AB is parallel to a tangent and intersects the radius drawn from the point of contact to its midpoint. If AB = 163, then find the radius of the circle - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: In a circle with centre P, chord AB is parallel to a tangent and intersects the radius drawn from the point of contact to its midpoint. If AB = `16sqrt(3)`, then find the radius of the circle
items.solution_text: Given: Chord AB || tangent XY AB = `16sqrt(3)` units PQ is radius of the circle. PC = CQ To find: Radius of the circle, i.e., l(PQ) Construction: Draw seg PB. In given figure, ∠PQY = 90° ......(i) [Tangent theorem] Chord AB || line XY .....[Given] ∴ ∠PCB ≅ ∠PQY .....[Corresponding angles] ∴ ∠PCB = 90° .....(ii) [From (i)] Now CB = `1/2` AB ∴ CB = `1/2 xx 16sqrt(3)` .....`[("A perpendicular drawn from the"),("centre of a circle on its chord"),("bisects the chord")]` CB = `8sqrt(3)` units .....(iii) Let the radius of the circle be x units .....(iv) ∴ PQ = x ∴ `"PC" = 1/2 "PQ"` ........[PC = CQ, P–C–Q] ∴ `"PC" = 1/2 x` .......(v) In ∆PCB, ∠PCB = 90° .....[From (ii)] ∴ PB 2 = PC 2 + CB 2 .....[Pythagoras theorem] ∴ x 2 = `(1/2 x)^2 + (8sqrt(3))^2` .....[From (iii), (iv) and (v)] ∴ x 2 = `x^2/4 + 64 xx 3` ∴ 4x 2 = `(x^2)/4 + 192` ∴ `(4x^2 – x^2)/4` = 192 ∴ `(3x^2)/4` = 192 ∴ x 2 = `192/3 xx 4` ∴ x 2 = 256 ∴ `sqrt(x^2)` = `sqrt256` ∴ x = 16 units ......[Taking square root of both sides] ∴ The radius of the circle is 16 units.
items.solution_html:
Given: Chord AB || tangent XY

AB = `16sqrt(3)` units

PQ is radius of the circle.

PC = CQ

To find: Radius of the circle, i.e., l(PQ)

Construction: Draw seg PB.

In given figure, ∠PQY = 90°     ......(i) [Tangent theorem]

Chord AB || line XY     .....[Given]

∴ ∠PCB ≅ ∠PQY    .....[Corresponding angles]

∴ ∠PCB = 90°   .....(ii) [From (i)]

Now CB = `1/2` AB

∴ CB = `1/2 xx 16sqrt(3)`   .....`[("A perpendicular drawn from the"),("centre of a circle on its chord"),("bisects the chord")]`

CB = `8sqrt(3)` units    .....(iii)

Let the radius of the circle be x units   .....(iv)

∴ PQ = x

∴ `"PC" = 1/2  "PQ"`  ........[PC = CQ, P–C–Q]

∴ `"PC" = 1/2 x`    .......(v)

In ∆PCB,

∠PCB = 90°    .....[From (ii)]

∴ PB2 = PC2 + CB2   .....[Pythagoras theorem]

∴ x2 = `(1/2 x)^2 + (8sqrt(3))^2`   .....[From (iii), (iv) and (v)]

∴ x2 = `x^2/4 + 64 xx 3`

∴ 4x2 = `(x^2)/4 + 192`

∴ `(4x^2 – x^2)/4` = 192

∴ `(3x^2)/4` = 192

∴ x2 = `192/3 xx 4`

∴ x2 = 256

∴ `sqrt(x^2)` = `sqrt256`

∴ x = 16 units   ......[Taking square root of both sides]

∴ The radius of the circle is 16 units.

items.title: In a cyclic ▢ABCD, twice the measure of ∠A is thrice the measure of ∠C. Find the measure of ∠C? - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: In a cyclic ▢ABCD, twice the measure of ∠A is thrice the measure of ∠C. Find the measure of ∠C?
items.solution_text: 72º Explanation: ABCD is a cyclic quadrilateral. 2∠A = 3∠C ...(1) (Given) Now, ∠A + ∠C = 180º ....(Opposite angles of a cyclic quadrilateral are supplementary) ⇒ `3/2` ∠C + ∠C = 180º ...[From (1)] ​⇒ `5/2`∠C = 180º ⇒ ∠C = `(2 xx 180º)/5` ⇒ ∠C = 72º Thus, the measure of ∠C is 72º.
items.solution_html:
72º

Explanation:

ABCD is a cyclic quadrilateral.

2∠A = 3∠C        ...(1) (Given)

Now,

∠A + ∠C = 180º     ....(Opposite angles of a cyclic quadrilateral are supplementary)

⇒ `3/2` ∠C + ∠C = 180º     ...[From (1)]

​⇒ `5/2`∠C = 180º

⇒ ∠C = `(2 xx 180º)/5`

⇒ ∠C = 72º

Thus, the measure of ∠C is 72º.

items.title: In a right angled triangle, if length of hypotenuse is 25 cm and height is 7 cm, then what is the length of its base? - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: In a right angled triangle, if length of hypotenuse is 25 cm and height is 7 cm, then what is the length of its base?
items.solution_text: Let ∆ABC be the given right angled triangle. AC = 25 cm, AB = 7 cm In ∆ABC, ∠B = 90° ......[Given] ∴ AC 2 = AB 2 + BC 2 .......[Pythagoras theorem] ∴ 25 2 = 7 2 + BC 2 ∴ 625 = 49 + BC 2 ∴ BC 2 = 625 – 49 ∴ BC 2 = 576 ∴ BC = 24 cm .......[Taking square root of both sides] ∴ The length of the base of the given right angle triangle is 24 cm.
items.solution_html:
Let ∆ABC be the given right angled triangle.

AC = 25 cm, AB = 7 cm

In ∆ABC, ∠B = 90°     ......[Given]

∴ AC2 = AB2 + BC2    .......[Pythagoras theorem]

∴ 252 = 72 + BC2

∴ 625 = 49 + BC2

∴ BC2 = 625 – 49

∴ BC2 = 576

∴ BC = 24 cm    .......[Taking square root of both sides]

∴ The length of the base of the given right angle triangle is 24 cm.

items.title: In ∆ABC, 2 AC = BC, sin A = 1, sin2A + sin2B + sin2C = 2, then ∠A = ? , ∠B = ?, ∠C = ? - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: In ∆ABC, `sqrt(2)` AC = BC, sin A = 1, sin 2 A + sin 2 B + sin 2 C = 2, then ∠A = ? , ∠B = ?, ∠C = ?
items.solution_text: sin A = 1 .....[Given] But, sin 90° = 1 ∴ sin A = sin 90° ∴ A = 90° `sqrt(2)` AC = BC .....[Given] ∴ `"AC"/"BC" = 1/sqrt(2)` .....(i) ∴ sin B = `"AC"/"BC"` ......(ii) [By definition] ∴ sin B = `1/sqrt(2)` .....[From (i) and (ii)] But, sin 45° = `1/sqrt(2)` ∴ sin B = sin 45° ∴ B = 45° sin 2 A + sin 2 B + sin 2 C = 2 .....[Given] ∴ `(1)^2 + (1/sqrt(2))^2 + sin^2"C"` = 2 ∴ `1 + 1/2 + sin^2"C"` = 2 ∴ sin 2 C = `2 - 3/2` ∴ sin 2 C = `1/2` ∴ sin C = `1/sqrt(2)` But, sin 45° = `1/sqrt(2)` ∴ sin C = sin 45° ∴ C = 45° ∴ ∠A = 90°, ∠B = 45°, ∠C = 45°
items.solution_html:
sin A = 1    .....[Given]

But, sin 90° = 1

∴ sin A = sin 90°

∴ A = 90°

`sqrt(2)` AC = BC  .....[Given]

∴ `"AC"/"BC" = 1/sqrt(2)`    .....(i)

∴ sin B = `"AC"/"BC"`   ......(ii) [By definition]

∴ sin B = `1/sqrt(2)`   .....[From (i) and (ii)]

But, sin 45° = `1/sqrt(2)`

∴ sin B = sin 45°

∴ B = 45°

sin2A + sin2B + sin2C = 2   .....[Given]

∴ `(1)^2 + (1/sqrt(2))^2 + sin^2"C"` = 2

∴ `1 + 1/2 + sin^2"C"` = 2

∴ sin2C = `2 - 3/2`

∴ sin2C = `1/2`

∴ sin C = `1/sqrt(2)`

But, sin 45° = `1/sqrt(2)`

∴ sin C = sin 45°

∴ C = 45°

∴ ∠A = 90°, ∠B = 45°, ∠C = 45°

items.title: In ΔABC, B − D − C and BD = 7, BC = 20, then find the following ratio. (i) A(ΔABD)A(ΔADC)A(ΔABD)A(ΔADC) (ii) A(ΔABD)A(ΔABC)A(ΔABD)A(ΔABC) (iii) A(ΔADC)A(ΔABC)A(ΔADC)A(ΔABC) - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: In ΔABC, B − D − C and BD = 7, BC = 20, then find the following ratio. (i) `"A(ΔABD)"/"A(ΔADC)"` (ii) `"A(ΔABD)"/"A(ΔABC)"` (iii) `"A(ΔADC)"/"A(ΔABC)"`
items.solution_text: Draw AE ⊥ BC, B – E – C. BC = BD + DC ......[B – D – C] ∴ 20 = 7 + DC ∴ DC = 20 − 7 = 13 (i) ΔABD and ΔADC have same height AE. `"A(ΔABD)"/"A(ΔADC)" = "BD"/"DC"` .....[Triangles having equal height] ∴ `"A(ΔABD)"/"A(ΔADC)" = 7/13` (ii) ΔABD and ΔABC have same height AE. `"A(ΔABD)"/"A(ΔABC)" = "BD"/"BC"` ......[Triangles having equal height] ∴ `"A(ΔABD)"/"A(ΔABC)" = 7/20` (iii) ΔADC and ΔABC have same height AE. `"A(ΔADC)"/"A(ΔABC)" = "DC"/"BC"` ......[Triangles having equal height] ∴ `"A(ΔADC)"/"A(ΔABC)" = 13/20`
items.solution_html:
Draw AE ⊥ BC, B – E – C.

BC = BD + DC       ......[B – D – C]

∴ 20 = 7 + DC

∴ DC = 20 − 7 = 13

(i) ΔABD and ΔADC have same height AE.

`"A(ΔABD)"/"A(ΔADC)" = "BD"/"DC"` .....[Triangles having equal height]

∴ `"A(ΔABD)"/"A(ΔADC)" = 7/13`

(ii) ΔABD and ΔABC have same height AE.

`"A(ΔABD)"/"A(ΔABC)" = "BD"/"BC"`   ......[Triangles having equal height]

∴ `"A(ΔABD)"/"A(ΔABC)" = 7/20`

(iii) ΔADC and ΔABC have same height AE.

`"A(ΔADC)"/"A(ΔABC)" = "DC"/"BC"`  ......[Triangles having equal height]

∴ `"A(ΔADC)"/"A(ΔABC)" = 13/20`

items.title: In ∆ABC, cos C = 1213 and BC = 24, then AC = ? - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: In ∆ABC, cos C = `12/13` and BC = 24, then AC = ?
items.solution_html:
cos C = `12/13`     .....(i) [Given]

In ∆ABC,

Let ∠ABC = 90°

∴ cos C = `"BC"/"AC"`    .....(ii) [By definition]

∴ `"BC"/"AC" = 12/13`  ......[From (i) and (ii)]

∴ `24/"AC" = 12/13`

∴ `(24 xx 13)/12` = AC

∴ `312/12` = AC

∴ AC = 26 units

items.title: In DABC, AP ⊥ BC and BQ ⊥ AC, B−P−C, A−Q−C, then show that ΔCPA ~ ΔCQB. If AP = 7, BQ = 8, BC = 12, then AC = ?In ΔCPA and ΔCQB ∠CPA ≅ [∠ ______] .......[each 90 - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: In ΔABC, AP ⊥ BC and BQ ⊥ AC, B−P−C, A−Q−C, then show that ΔCPA ~ ΔCQB. If AP = 7, BQ = 8, BC = 12, then AC = ? In ΔCPA and ΔCQB ∠CPA ≅ [∠ ______] ...[each 90°] ∠ACP ≅ [∠ ______] ...[common angle] ΔCPA ~ ΔCQB ......[______ similarity test] `"AP"/"BQ" = (["______"])/"BC"` .......[corresponding sides of similar triangles] `7/8 = (["______"])/12` AC × [______] = 7 × 12 AC = 10.5
items.solution_text: In ΔCPA and ΔCQB ∠CPA ≅ ∠CQB ...[each 90°] ∠ACP ≅ ∠BCQ ...[common angle] ΔCPA ~ ΔCQB ...[ AA similarity test] `"AP"/"BQ"` = `"AC"/"BC"` ...[corresponding sides of similar triangles] `7/8` = `"AC"/12` AC × 8 = 7 × 12 AC = `(7 xx 12)/8` AC = `(7 xx 3)/2` AC = `21/2` AC = 10.5.
items.solution_html:
In ΔCPA and ΔCQB

∠CPA ≅ ∠CQB    ...[each 90°]

∠ACP ≅ ∠BCQ   ...[common angle]

ΔCPA ~ ΔCQB     ...[AA similarity test]

`"AP"/"BQ"` = `"AC"/"BC"`   ...[corresponding sides of similar triangles]

`7/8` = `"AC"/12`

AC × 8 = 7 × 12

AC = `(7 xx 12)/8`

AC = `(7 xx 3)/2`

AC = `21/2`

AC = 10.5.

items.title: In ΔDEF and ΔXYZ, DEXY=FEYZ and ∠E ≅ ∠Y. _______ test gives similarity between ΔDEF and ΔXYZ. - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: In ΔDEF and ΔXYZ, `"DE"/"XY" = "FE"/"YZ"` and ∠E ≅ ∠Y. _______ test gives similarity between ΔDEF and ΔXYZ.
items.solution_text: SAS In ΔDEF and ΔXYZ, `"DE"/"XY" = "FE"/"YZ"` ...(Given) ∠E ≅ ∠Y ...(Given) ∴ ΔDEF ≅ ΔXYZ ...(SAS Similarity triangle test)
items.solution_html:
SAS 

In ΔDEF and ΔXYZ,

`"DE"/"XY" = "FE"/"YZ"`     ...(Given)

∠E ≅ ∠Y         ...(Given)

∴ ΔDEF ≅ ΔXYZ  ...(SAS Similarity triangle test)

items.title: In fig., AB ⊥ BC and DC ⊥ BC, AB = 6, DC = 4 then A(ΔABC)A(ΔBCD) = ? - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: In fig., AB ⊥ BC and DC ⊥ BC, AB = 6, DC = 4 then `("A"(Δ"ABC"))/("A"(Δ"BCD"))` = ?
items.solution_text: ΔABC and ΔBCD have same base BC. ∴ `"A(ΔABC)"/"A(ΔBCD)" = "AB"/"DC"` .....[Triangles having equal base] ∴ `"A(ΔABC)"/"A(ΔBCD)" = 6/4` .....[Given] ∴ `("A"(Δ"ABC"))/("A"(Δ"BCD")) = 3/2`
items.solution_html:
ΔABC and ΔBCD have same base BC.

∴ `"A(ΔABC)"/"A(ΔBCD)" = "AB"/"DC"`   .....[Triangles having equal base]

∴ `"A(ΔABC)"/"A(ΔBCD)" = 6/4`     .....[Given]

∴ `("A"(Δ"ABC"))/("A"(Δ"BCD")) = 3/2`

items.title: In fig. BD = 8, BC = 12, B-D-C, then A(ΔABC)A(ΔABD) = ? - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: In fig. BD = 8, BC = 12, B-D-C, then `"A(ΔABC)"/"A(ΔABD)"` = ?
items.solution_text: 3: 2 In ΔABC and ΔABD, ΔABC and ΔABD have the same height. ...(Given) The ratio of the areas of two triangles with equal heights is equal to the ratio of their corresponding bases. ∴ `"A(ΔABC)"/"A(ΔABD)" = "BC"/"BD"` ∴ `"A(ΔABC)"/"A(ΔABD)" = 12/8` ∴ `"A(ΔABC)"/"A(ΔABD)" = 3/2`.
items.solution_html:
3: 2

In ΔABC and ΔABD,

ΔABC and ΔABD have the same height.   ...(Given)

The ratio of the areas of two triangles with equal heights is equal to the ratio of their corresponding bases.

∴ `"A(ΔABC)"/"A(ΔABD)" = "BC"/"BD"`

∴ `"A(ΔABC)"/"A(ΔABD)" = 12/8`

 ∴ `"A(ΔABC)"/"A(ΔABD)" = 3/2`.

items.title: In fig. BP ⊥ AC, CQ ⊥ AB, A−P−C and A−Q−B then show that ΔAPB and ΔAQC are similar.In ΔAPB and ΔAQC ∠APB = [ ]° ......(i) ∠AQC = [ ]° ......(ii) ∠APB - Geometry Mathematics 2

items.solution_text: In ΔAPB and ΔAQC ∠APB = 90 ° ......(i) ∠AQC = 90 ° ......(ii) ∠APB ≅ ∠AQC .....[From (i) and (ii)] ∠PAB ≅ ∠QAC .....[ Common angle ] ΔAPB ~ ΔAQC .....[ AA test of similarity ]
items.solution_html:
In ΔAPB and ΔAQC

∠PAB ≅ ∠QAC    .....[Common angle]

ΔAPB ~ ΔAQC     .....[AA test of similarity]

items.title: In fig., line BC || line DE, AB = 2, BD = 3, AC = 4 and CE = x, then find the value of x - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: In fig., line BC || line DE, AB = 2, BD = 3, AC = 4 and CE = x, then find the value of x
items.solution_text: In ΔADE, line BC || seg DE ......[Given] ∴ by Basic proportionality theorem ∴ `"AB"/"BD" = "AC"/"CE"` ∴ `2/3 = 4/x` ......[Given] ∴ x = `4 xx 3/2` ∴ x = 2 × 3 ∴ x = 6
items.solution_html:
In ΔADE,

line BC || seg DE    ......[Given]

∴ by Basic proportionality theorem

∴ `"AB"/"BD" = "AC"/"CE"` 

∴ `2/3 = 4/x`     ......[Given]

∴ x = `4 xx 3/2`

∴ x = 2 × 3

∴ x = 6

items.title: In fig., PM = 10 cm, A(ΔPQS) = 100 sq.cm, A(ΔQRS) = 110 sq.cm, then NR?ΔPQS and ΔQRS having seg QS common base. Areas of two triangles whose base is common are in proportion of their corresponding [_ - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: In fig., PM = 10 cm, A(ΔPQS) = 100 sq.cm, A(ΔQRS) = 110 sq.cm, then NR? ΔPQS and ΔQRS having seg QS common base. Areas of two triangles whose base is common are in proportion of their corresponding [______] `("A"("PQS"))/("A"("QRS")) = (["______"])/"NR"`, `100/110 = (["______"])/"NR"`, NR = [ ______ ] cm
items.solution_text: ΔPQS and ΔQRS having seg QS common base. Areas of two triangles whose base is common are in proportion of their corresponding heights. `("A"("ΔPQS"))/("A"("ΔQRS"))` = `"PM"/"NR"` , `100/110` = `10/"NR"` , ∴ NR = `(110 xx 10)/100` ∴ NR = 11 cm.
items.solution_html:
ΔPQS and ΔQRS having seg QS common base.

Areas of two triangles whose base is common are in proportion of their corresponding heights.

`("A"("ΔPQS"))/("A"("ΔQRS"))` = `"PM"/"NR"`,

`100/110` = `10/"NR"`,

∴ NR = `(110 xx 10)/100`

∴ NR = 11 cm.

items.title: In fig., PS = 2, SQ = 6, QR = 5, PT = x and TR = y. Then find the pair of value of x and y such that ST || side QR. - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: In fig., PS = 2, SQ = 6, QR = 5, PT = x and TR = y. Then find the pair of value of x and y such that ST || side QR.
items.solution_text: In ΔPQR, Line ST || side QR ...[Given] ∴ `"PS"/"SQ" = "PT"/"TR"` ...[Basic proportionality theorem] ∴ `2/6 = "PT"/"TR"` ...[Given] ∴ `1/3 = x/y` ∴ y = 3x When x = 1, y = 3(1) = 3 ∴ x = 1, y = 3 When x = 2, y = 3(2) = 6 ∴ x = 2, y = 6 When x = 3, y = 3(3) = 9 ∴ x = 3, y = 9 ∴ Some of the pairs of values of x and y are (1, 3), (2, 6), (3, 9).
items.solution_html:
In ΔPQR,

Line ST || side QR    ...[Given]

∴ `"PS"/"SQ" = "PT"/"TR"`   ...[Basic proportionality theorem]

∴ `2/6 = "PT"/"TR"`   ...[Given]

∴ `1/3 = x/y`

∴ y = 3x 

When x = 1, y = 3(1) = 3

∴ x = 1, y = 3 

When x = 2, y = 3(2) = 6

∴ x = 2, y = 6

When x = 3, y = 3(3) = 9

∴ x = 3, y = 9 

∴ Some of the pairs of values of x and y are (1, 3), (2, 6), (3, 9).
