items.title: In figure, chord EF || chord GH. Prove that, chord EG ≅ chord FH. Fill in the blanks and write the proof. Proof: Draw seg GF.∠EFG = ∠FGH ......□ .....(I) ∠EFG = □ ......[inscribed angle theorem] (II) - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: In figure, chord EF || chord GH. Prove that, chord EG ≅ chord FH. Fill in the blanks and write the proof. Proof: Draw seg GF. ∠EFG = ∠FGH ......`square` .....(I) ∠EFG = `square` ......[inscribed angle theorem] (II) ∠FGH = `square` ......[inscribed angle theorem] (III) ∴ m(arc EG) = `square` ......[By (I), (II), and (III)] chord EG ≅ chord FH ........[corresponding chords of congruent arcs]
items.solution_text: Proof: Draw seg GF. ∠EFG = ∠FGH ...... [Alternate angles] (I) ∠EFG = `1/2 ("arc EG")` ......[Inscribed angle theorem] (II) ∠FGH = `1/2 ("arc FH")` ......[Inscribed angle theorem] (III) ∴ m(arc EG) = m(arc FH) ......[By (I), (II), and (III)] chord EG ≅ chord FH ........[corresponding chords of congruent arcs]
items.solution_html:
Proof: Draw seg GF.

∠EFG = ∠FGH     ......[Alternate angles] (I)

∠EFG = `1/2 ("arc EG")`   ......[Inscribed angle theorem] (II)

∠FGH = `1/2 ("arc FH")`   ......[Inscribed angle theorem] (III)

∴ m(arc EG) = m(arc FH)  ......[By (I), (II), and (III)]

chord EG ≅ chord FH   ........[corresponding chords of congruent arcs]

items.title: In figure, chords AC and DE intersect at B. If ∠ABE = 108°, m(arc AE) = 95°, find m(arc DC). - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: In figure, chords AC and DE intersect at B. If ∠ABE = 108°, m(arc AE) = 95°, find m(arc DC).
items.solution_text: Chords AC and DE intersect internally at point B. ∴ ∠ABE = `1/2` [m(arc AE) + m(arc DC)] ∴ 108° = `1/2` [95° + m(arc DC)] ∴ 108° × 2 = 95° + m(arc DC) ∴ 95° + m(arc DC) = 216° ∴ m(arc DC) = 216° − 95° ∴ m(arc DC) = 121°
items.solution_html:
Chords AC and DE intersect internally at point B.

∴ ∠ABE = `1/2` [m(arc AE) + m(arc DC)]

∴ 108° = `1/2` [95° + m(arc DC)]

∴ 108° × 2 = 95° + m(arc DC)

∴ 95° + m(arc DC) = 216°

∴ m(arc DC) = 216° − 95°

∴ m(arc DC) = 121°

items.title: In figure, in a circle with centre O, length of chord AB is equal to the radius of the circle. Find measure of the following. ∠AOB - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: In figure, in a circle with center O, the length of chord AB is equal to the radius of the circle. Find the measure of the following. (i) ∠AOB (ii) ∠ACB (iii) arc AB.
items.solution_text: (i) seg OA = seg OB = radius ...(i) [Radii of the same circle] seg AB = radius ...(ii) [Given] ∴ seg OA = seg OB = seg AB ......[From (i) and (ii)] ∴ ∆OAB is an equilateral triangle. ∴ m∠AOB = 60° ...[Angle of an equilateral triangle] (ii) m∠ACB = `1/2` m∠AOB ...[Measure of an angle subtended by an arc at a point on the circle is half of the measure of the angle subtended by the arc at the centre] ∴ m∠ACB = `1/2 xx 60^circ` ∴ m∠ACB = 30° (iii) m(arc AB) = m∠AOB ...[Definition of measure of minor arc] ∴ m(arc AB) = 60°
items.solution_html:
(i) seg OA = seg OB = radius   ...(i) [Radii of the same circle]

seg AB = radius                        ...(ii) [Given]

∴ seg OA = seg OB = seg AB   ......[From (i) and (ii)]

∴ ∆OAB is an equilateral triangle.

∴ m∠AOB = 60°   ...[Angle of an equilateral triangle]

(ii) m∠ACB = `1/2` m∠AOB     ...[Measure of an angle subtended by an arc at a point on the circle is half of the measure of the angle subtended by the arc at the centre]

∴ m∠ACB = `1/2 xx 60^circ`

∴ m∠ACB = 30°

(iii) m(arc AB) = m∠AOB   ...[Definition of measure of minor arc]

∴ m(arc AB) = 60°

items.title: In figure, M is the centre of the circle and seg KL is a tangent segment. If MK = 12, KL = 63, then find (i) Radius of the circle.(ii) Measures of ∠K and ∠M. - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: In figure, M is the centre of the circle and seg KL is a tangent segment. If MK = 12, KL = `6sqrt(3)`, then find (i) Radius of the circle. (ii) Measures of ∠K and ∠M.
items.solution_text: (i) Line KL is the tangent to the circle at point L and seg ML is the radius. ......[Given] ∴ ∠MLK = 90° ......(i) [Tangent theorem] In ∆MLK, ∠MLK = 90° ∴ MK 2 = ML 2 + KL 2 .....[Pythagoras theorem] ∴ 12 2 = ML 2 + `(6sqrt(3))^2` ∴ 144 = ML 2 + 108 ∴ ML 2 = 144 − 108 ∴ ML 2 = 36 ∴ ML = `sqrt(36)` ∴ ML = 6 units ......[Taking square root of both sides] ∴ Radius of the circle is 6 units. (ii) We know that, ML = `1/2` MK, ∴ ∠K = 30° .....(ii) [Converse of 30°−60°−90° theorem] In ∆MLK, ∠L = 90° .....[From (i)] ∠K = 30° .....[From (ii)] ∴ ∠M = 60° ......[Remaining angle of ∆MLK]
items.solution_html:
(i)

Line KL is the tangent to the circle at point L and seg ML is the radius.                   ......[Given]

∴ ∠MLK = 90°      ......(i) [Tangent theorem]

In ∆MLK,

∠MLK = 90°

∴ MK2 = ML2 + KL2    .....[Pythagoras theorem]

∴ 122 = ML2 + `(6sqrt(3))^2`

∴ 144 = ML2 + 108

∴ ML2 = 144 − 108

∴ ML2 = 36

∴ ML = `sqrt(36)`

∴ ML = 6 units     ......[Taking square root of both sides]

∴ Radius of the circle is 6 units.

(ii)

We know that,

ML = `1/2` MK,

∴ ∠K = 30°     .....(ii) [Converse of 30°−60°−90° theorem]

In ∆MLK,

∠L = 90°     .....[From (i)]

∠K = 30°    .....[From (ii)]

∴ ∠M = 60°    ......[Remaining angle of ∆MLK]

items.title: In figure, O is the centre of a circle, chord PQ ≅ chord RS. If ∠POR = 70° and (arc RS) = 80°, find (i) m(arc PR) (ii) m(arc QS) (iii) m(arc QSR) - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: In figure, O is the centre of a circle, chord PQ ≅ chord RS. If ∠POR = 70° and (arc RS) = 80°, find (i) m(arc PR) (ii) m(arc QS) (iii) m(arc QSR)
items.solution_text: (i) m(arc PR) = m∠POR ...[Definition of measure of arc] ∴ m(arc PR) = 70° (ii) chord PQ ≅ chord RS .....[Given] ∴ m(arc PQ) = m(arc RS) = 80° .....[Corresponding arcs of congruent chords of a circle are congruent] Now, m(arc QS) + m(arc PQ) + m(arc PR) + m(arc RS) = 360° ...[Measure of a circle is 360°] ∴ m(arc QS) + 80° + 70° + 80° = 360° ∴ m(arc QS) + 230° = 360° ∴ m(arc QS) = 130° (iii) m(arc QSR) = m(arc QS) + m(arc SR) = 130° + 80° ...[Arc addition property] m(arc QSR) = 210°
items.solution_html:
(i) m(arc PR) = m∠POR     ...[Definition of measure of arc]

∴ m(arc PR) = 70°

(ii) 

chord PQ ≅ chord RS    .....[Given]

∴ m(arc PQ) = m(arc RS) = 80°    .....[Corresponding arcs of congruent chords of a circle are congruent]

Now,

m(arc QS) + m(arc PQ) + m(arc PR) + m(arc RS) = 360° ...[Measure of a circle is 360°]

∴ m(arc QS) + 80° + 70° + 80° = 360°

∴ m(arc QS) + 230° = 360°

∴ m(arc QS) = 130°

(iii) m(arc QSR) = m(arc QS) + m(arc SR) = 130° + 80°   ...[Arc addition property]

m(arc QSR) = 210°

items.title: In figure, points G, D, E, F are concyclic points of a circle with centre C∠ECF = 70°, m(arc DGF) = 200° Find m(arc DEF) by completing activity. ∴ m(arc EF) = ∠ECF ......[Definition of measure of arc - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: In figure, points G, D, E, F are concyclic points of a circle with centre C. ∠ECF = 70°, m(arc DGF) = 200° Find m(arc DEF) by completing activity. m(arc EF) = ∠ECF ......[Definition of measure of arc] ∴ m(arc EF) = `square` But; m(arc DE) + m(arc EF) + m(arc DGF) = `square` .....[Measure of a complete circle] ∴ m(arc DE) = `square` ∴ m(arc DEF) = m(arc DE) + m(arc EF) ∴ m(arc DEF) = `square`
items.solution_text: m(arc EF) = ∠ECF ...[Definition of measure of arc] ∴ m(arc EF) = 70° ...(i) But; m(arc DE)+ m(arc EF) + m(arc DGF) = 360° ...[Measure of a complete circle] ∴ m(arc DE) + 70° + 200° = 360° m(arc DE) = 360° – 270° ...[From (i) and given] ∴ m(arc DE) = 90° ...(ii) ∴ m(arc DEF) = m(arc DE) + m(arc EF) = 90° + 70° ...[From (i) and (ii)] ∴ m(arc DEF) = 160°
items.solution_html:
m(arc EF) = ∠ECF       ...[Definition of measure of arc]

∴ m(arc EF) = 70°      ...(i)

But; m(arc DE)+ m(arc EF) + m(arc DGF) = 360°    ...[Measure of a complete circle]

∴ m(arc DE) + 70° + 200° = 360°

m(arc DE) = 360° – 270°  ...[From (i) and given]

∴ m(arc DE) = 90°           ...(ii)

∴ m(arc DEF) = m(arc DE) + m(arc EF)

= 90° + 70°       ...[From (i) and (ii)]

∴ m(arc DEF) = 160°

items.title: In given fig., quadrilateral PQRS, side PQ || side SR, AR = 5 AP, then prove that, SR = 5PQ - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: In given fig., quadrilateral PQRS, side PQ || side SR, AR = 5 AP, then prove that, SR = 5PQ
items.solution_text: side PQ || side SR and seg SQ is their transversal. ...[Given] ∴ ∠QSR ≅ ∠SQP ...[Alternative angles] ∴ ∠ASR ≅ ∠AQP ......(i) [Q−A−S] In ΔASR and ΔAQP, ∠ASR ≅ ∠AQP ......[From (i)] ∠SAR ≅ ∠QAP ......[Vertically opposite angles] ∴ ΔASR ∼ ΔAQP ......[AA test of similarity] ∴ `"AR"/"AP" = "SR"/"PQ"` ......(ii)[Corresponding sides of similar triangles] But, AR = 5 AP ......[Given] ∴ `"AR"/"AP" = 5/1` ......(iii) ∴ `"SR"/"PQ" = 5/1` ......[From (ii) and (iii)] ∴ SR = 5PQ
items.solution_html:
side PQ || side SR and seg SQ is their transversal.    ...[Given]

∴ ∠QSR ≅ ∠SQP    ...[Alternative angles]

∴ ∠ASR ≅ ∠AQP    ......(i) [Q−A−S]

 In ΔASR and ΔAQP,

∠ASR ≅ ∠AQP     ......[From (i)]

∠SAR ≅ ∠QAP    ......[Vertically opposite angles]

∴ ΔASR ∼ ΔAQP    ......[AA test of similarity]

∴ `"AR"/"AP" = "SR"/"PQ"`   ......(ii)[Corresponding sides of similar triangles]

But, AR = 5 AP    ......[Given]

∴ `"AR"/"AP" = 5/1`    ......(iii)

∴ `"SR"/"PQ" = 5/1`    ......[From (ii) and (iii)]

∴ SR = 5PQ

items.title: In ∆LMN, l = 5, m = 13, n = 12 then complete the activity to show that whether the given triangle is right angled triangle or not.*(l, m, n are opposite sides of ∠L, ∠M, ∠N respectively) Activity: In - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: In ∆LMN, l = 5, m = 13, n = 12 then complete the activity to show that whether the given triangle is right angled triangle or not. *(l, m, n are opposite sides of ∠L, ∠M, ∠N respectively) Activity: In ∆LMN, l = 5, m = 13, n = `square` ∴ l 2 = `square`, m 2 = 169, n 2 = 144. ∴ l 2 + n 2 = 25 + 144 = `square` ∴ `square` + l 2 = m 2 ∴By Converse of Pythagoras theorem, ∆LMN is right angled triangle.
items.solution_text: In ∆LMN, l = 5, m = 13, n = 12 ∴ l 2 = 25 , m 2 = 169, n 2 = 144. ∴ l 2 + n 2 = 25 + 144 = 169 ∴ n 2 + l 2 = m 2 ∴By Converse of Pythagoras theorem, ∆LMN is right angled triangle.
items.solution_html:
In ∆LMN,

l = 5, m = 13, n = 12

∴ l2 = 25, m2 = 169, n2 = 144.

∴ l2 + n2 = 25 + 144 = 169

∴ n2 + l2 = m2 

∴By Converse of Pythagoras theorem, ∆LMN is right angled triangle.

items.title: In Quadrilateral ABCD, side AD || BC, diagonal AC and BD intersect in point P, then prove that APPDPCBPAPPD=PCBP - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: In Quadrilateral ABCD, side AD || BC, diagonal AC and BD intersect in point P, then prove that `"AP"/"PD" = "PC"/"BP"`
items.solution_text: Proof: seg AD || seg BC and BD is their transversal. ...[Given] ∴ ∠DBC ≅ ∠BDA ...[Alternate angles] ∴ ∠PBC ≅ ∠PDA ...(i)[D−P−B] In ΔPBC and ΔPDA, ∠PBC ≅ ∠PDA ...[From (i)] ∠BPC ≅ ∠DPA ...[Vertically opposite angles] ∴ ΔPBC ∼ ΔPDA ...[AA test of similarity] ∴ `"BP"/"PD" = "PC"/"AP"` ...[Corresponding sides of similar triangles] ∴ `"AP"/"PD" = "PC"/"BP"` ...[By alternendo]
items.solution_html:
Proof: seg AD || seg BC and BD is their transversal.    ...[Given]

∴ ∠DBC ≅ ∠BDA     ...[Alternate angles]

∴ ∠PBC ≅ ∠PDA      ...(i)[D−P−B]

In ΔPBC and ΔPDA,

∠PBC ≅ ∠PDA   ...[From (i)]

∠BPC ≅ ∠DPA   ...[Vertically opposite angles]

∴ ΔPBC ∼ ΔPDA    ...[AA test of similarity]

∴ `"BP"/"PD" = "PC"/"AP"`    ...[Corresponding sides of similar triangles]

∴ `"AP"/"PD" = "PC"/"BP"`   ...[By alternendo]

items.title: In the adjoining figure, circle with center D touches the sides of ∠ACB at A and B. If ∠ACB = 52°, find measure of ∠ADB. - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: In the adjoining figure, the circle with center D touches the sides of ∠ACB at A and B. If ∠ACB = 52°, find the measure of ∠ADB.
items.solution_text: In a figure, D is the centre of the circle. ADBC is a quadrilateral. ∴ ∠ACB + ∠CAD + ∠CBD + ∠ADB = 360° ......[The sum of all angles of a quadrilateral is 360°.] ∴ 52° + 90° + 90° + ∠ADB = 360° ......[Tangent theorem] ∴ ∠ADB + 232° = 360° ∴ ∠ADB = 360° – 232° ∴ ∠ADB = 128°
items.solution_html:
In a figure, D is the centre of the circle.

ADBC is a quadrilateral.

∴ ∠ACB + ∠CAD + ∠CBD + ∠ADB = 360°  ......[The sum of all angles of a quadrilateral is 360°.]

∴ 52° + 90° + 90° + ∠ADB = 360°  ......[Tangent theorem]

∴ ∠ADB + 232° = 360°

∴ ∠ADB = 360° – 232°

∴ ∠ADB = 128°

items.title: In the adjoining figure circle with Centre Q touches the sides of ∠MPN at M and N. If ∠MPN = 40°, find measure of ∠MQN. - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: In the adjoining figure circle with Centre, Q touches the sides of ∠MPN at M and N. If ∠MPN = 40°, find measure of ∠MQN.
items.solution_text: ∠MPN = 40° ....[Given] `{:(∠"QMP" = 90^circ),(∠"QNP" = 90^circ):}}` .....[Tangent theorem] In ▢MQNP, ∠MPN + ∠QMP + ∠QNP + ∠MQN = 360° ...[Sum of the measures of the angles of a quadrilateral is 360°] ∴ 40° + 90° + 90° + ∠MQN = 360° ∴ 220° + ∠MQN = 360° ∴ ∠MQN = 360° − 220° ∴ ∠MQN = 140°
items.solution_html:
∠MPN = 40°      ....[Given]

`{:(∠"QMP" = 90^circ),(∠"QNP" = 90^circ):}}` .....[Tangent theorem]

In ▢MQNP,

∠MPN + ∠QMP + ∠QNP + ∠MQN = 360°    ...[Sum of the measures of the angles of a quadrilateral is 360°]

∴ 40° + 90° + 90° + ∠MQN = 360°

∴ 220° + ∠MQN = 360°

∴ ∠MQN = 360° − 220°

∴ ∠MQN = 140°

items.title: In the Adjoining Figure Circles with Centres X and Y Touch Each Other at Point Z. a Secant Passing Through Z Intersects the Circles at Points a and B Respectively. Prove That, Radius Xa || Radius Yb. - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: In the adjoining figure circles with centres X and Y touch each other at point Z. A secant passing through Z intersects the circles at points A and B respectively. Prove that, radius XA || radius YB. Fill in the blanks and complete the proof. Construction: Draw segments XZ and YZ. Proof : By theorem of touching circles, points X, Z, Y are `square`. ∴ ∠XZA ≅ `square` ...(opposite angles) Let ∠XZA = ∠BZY = a ...(I) Now, seg XA ≅ seg XZ ...[Radii of the same circle] ∴∠XAZ = `square` = a ...[isosceles triangle theorem](II) Similarly, seg YB ≅ seg YZ ...[Radii of the same circle] ∴∠BZY = `square` = a ...[isosceles triangle theorem ] (III) ∴ from (I), (II), (III), ∠XAZ = `square` ∴ radius XA || radius YZ ...[`square`]
items.solution_text: Construction : Draw segments XZ and YZ. Proof : By theorem of touching circles, points X, Z, Y are collinear . ∴ ∠XZA ≅ ∠BZY ...(opposite angles) Let ∠XZA = ∠BZY = a .....(I) Now, seg XA ≅ seg XZ ...[Radii of the same circle] ∴∠XAZ = ∠XZA = a ....[isosceles triangle theorem](II) Similarly, seg YB ≅ seg YZ ....[Radii of the same circle] ∴∠BZY = ∠ZBY = a ...[isosceles triangle theorem](III) ∴ from (I), (II), (III), ∠XAZ = ∠ZBY ∴ radius XA || radius YB ...[Alternate angle test]
items.solution_html:
Construction: Draw segments XZ and YZ.
Proof:

By theorem of touching circles, points X, Z, Y are collinear.
∴ ∠XZA ≅ ∠BZY               ...(opposite angles)
Let ∠XZA = ∠BZY = a       .....(I)
Now, seg XA ≅ seg XZ      ...[Radii of the same circle]
∴∠XAZ = ∠XZA = a         ....[isosceles triangle theorem](II)
Similarly, seg YB ≅ seg YZ ....[Radii of the same circle]
∴∠BZY = ∠ZBY = a           ...[isosceles triangle theorem](III)
∴ from (I), (II), (III), 
∠XAZ = ∠ZBY
∴ radius XA || radius YB    ...[Alternate angle test]

appears_in.text: Balbharati Mathematics 2 [English] Standard 10 Maharashtra State Board

items.title: In the adjoining figure, O is the centre of the circle. From point R, seg RM and seg RN are tangent segments touching the circle at M and N. If (OR) = 10 cm and radius of the circle = 5 cm, then What - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: In the adjoining figure, O is the center of the circle. From point R, seg RM and seg RN are tangent segments touching the circle at M and N. If (OR) = 10 cm and radius of the circle = 5 cm, then (i) What is the length of each tangent segment? (ii) What is the measure of ∠MRO? (iii) What is the measure of ∠MRN?
items.solution_text: seg RM and seg RN are tangents to the circle with center O. ....[Given] ∴ ∠OMR = ∠ONR = 90° ...[Tangent theorem] (i) In ∆OMR, ∠OMR = 90° ∴ OR 2 = OM 2 + RM 2 ...[Pythagoras theorem] ∴ 10 2 = 5 2 + RM 2 ∴ 100 = 25 + RM 2 ∴ RM 2 = 75 ∴ RM = `sqrt(75)` ...[Taking square root of both sides] = `5sqrt(3)` cm ∴ RM = RN ......[Tangent segment theorem] ∴ Length of each tangent segment is `5sqrt(3)` cm. (ii) In ∆RMO, ∠OMR = 90° ...[Tangent theorem] OM = 5 cm and OR = 10 cm ∴ OM = `1/2` OR ∴ ∠MRO = 30° .....(i) [Converse of 30°–60°–90° theorem] Similarly, ∠NRO = 30° (iii) But, ∠MRN = ∠MRO + ∠NRO ...[Angle addition property] = 30° + 30° ...[From (i)] ∴ ∠MRN = 60°.
items.solution_html:
seg RM and seg RN are tangents to the circle with center O. ....[Given]

∴ ∠OMR = ∠ONR = 90°   ...[Tangent theorem]
(i) In ∆OMR,

∠OMR = 90°

∴ OR2 = OM2 + RM2      ...[Pythagoras theorem]

∴ 102 = 52 + RM2

∴ 100 = 25 + RM2

∴ RM2 = 75

∴ RM = `sqrt(75)`  ...[Taking square root of both sides]

= `5sqrt(3)` cm

∴ RM = RN   ......[Tangent segment theorem]

∴ Length of each tangent segment is `5sqrt(3)` cm.

(ii) In ∆RMO,

∠OMR = 90°   ...[Tangent theorem]

OM = 5 cm and OR = 10 cm

∴ OM = `1/2` OR

∴ ∠MRO = 30°  .....(i) [Converse of 30°–60°–90° theorem]

Similarly, ∠NRO = 30°

(iii) But, ∠MRN = ∠MRO + ∠NRO    ...[Angle addition property]

= 30° + 30°    ...[From (i)]

∴ ∠MRN = 60°.

items.title: In the adjoining figure, seg DE is the chord of the circle with center C. seg CF⊥ seg DE and DE = 16 cm, then find the length of DF? - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: In the adjoining figure, seg DE is the chord of the circle with center C. seg CF⊥ seg DE and DE = 16 cm, then find the length of DF?
items.solution_text: In given figure, seg CF ⊥ Chord DE ∴ DF ≅ DE ......[[A perpendicular drawn from the centre of a circle on its chord bisects the chord] ∴ DF = `1/2` DE = `1/2 xx 16` .....[Given] ∴ DF = 8 cm
items.solution_html:
In given figure, seg CF ⊥ Chord DE

∴ DF ≅ DE      ......[[A perpendicular drawn from the centre of a circle on its chord bisects the chord]

∴ DF = `1/2` DE

= `1/2 xx 16`   .....[Given]

∴ DF = 8 cm

items.title: In the adjoining figure, the line MN touches the circle with center A at point M. If AN = 13 and MN = 5, then find the radius of the circle? - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: In the adjoining figure, the line MN touches the circle with center A at point M. If AN = 13 and MN = 5, then find the radius of the circle?
items.solution_text: In given figure, AM is the radius of the circle. In ∆AMN, ∠AMN = 90° ......[Tangent theorem] ∴ AN 2 = AM 2 + MN 2 ......[Pythagoras theorem] ∴ 13 2 = AM 2 + 5 2 ......[Given] ∴ 169 = AM 2 + 25 ∴ AM 2 = 169 – 25 ∴ AM 2 = 144 ∴ AM = 12 ......[Taking square root of both sides] ∴ The radius of the circle is 12 units.
items.solution_html:
In given figure, AM is the radius of the circle.

In ∆AMN,

∠AMN = 90°                  ......[Tangent theorem]

∴ AN2 = AM2 + MN2    ......[Pythagoras theorem]

∴ 132 = AM2 + 52         ......[Given]

∴ 169 = AM2 + 25

∴ AM2 = 169 – 25

∴ AM2 = 144

∴ AM = 12                   ......[Taking square root of both sides]

∴ The radius of the circle is 12 units.

items.title: In the adjoining figure the radius of a circle with centre C is 6 cm, line AB is a tangent at A. What is the measure of ∠CAB? Why? - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: In the adjoining figure the radius of a circle with centre C is 6 cm, line AB is a tangent at A. What is the measure of ∠CAB? Why?
items.solution_text: Line AB is the tangent to the circle with centre C and radius AC. ......[Given] ∴ ∠CAB = 90° ......(i) [Tangent theorem]
items.solution_html:
Line AB is the tangent to the circle with centre C and radius AC.    ......[Given]

∴ ∠CAB = 90°   ......(i) [Tangent theorem]

items.title: In the figure, a circle touches all the sides of quadrilateral ABCD from the inside. The center of the circle is O. If AD⊥ DC and BC = 38, QB = 27, DC = 25, then find the radius of the circle. - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: In the figure, a circle touches all the sides of quadrilateral ABCD from the inside. The center of the circle is O. If AD⊥ DC and BC = 38, QB = 27, DC = 25, then find the radius of the circle.
items.solution_text: Given: AD ⊥ DC BC = 38, QB = 27, DC = 25 To find: Radius of the circle, i.e., OP. Solution: BC = 38 ......[Given] ∴ BQ + QC = 38 ......[B–Q–C] ∴ 27 + QC = 38 .......[Given] ∴ QC = 38 – 27 ∴ QC = 11 units ......(i) Now, QC = SC ......[Tangent segment theorem] ∴ SC = 11 units .....(ii) [From (i)] DC = 25 .......[Given] ∴ DS + SC = 25 ......[D–S–C] ∴ DS + 11 = 25 ......[From (ii)] ∴ DS = 25 – 11 ∴ DS = 14 units ......(iii) In ▢DSOP, ∠P = ∠S = 90° ......[Tangent theorem] ∠D = 90° ......[Given] ∴ ∠O = 90° ......[Remaning angle of ▢DSOP] ∴ ▢DSOP is a rectangle. Also, OP = OS ......[Radii of the same circle] ∴ ▢DSOP is a square .......`[("A rectangle is square if its"),("adjacent sides are congruent")]` ∴ OS = DS = DP = PO .....(iv) [Sides of the square] ∴ OP = 14 units ......[From (iii) and (iv)] ∴ The radius of the circle is 14 units.
items.solution_html:
Given: AD ⊥ DC

BC = 38, QB = 27, DC = 25

To find: Radius of the circle, i.e., OP.

Solution:

BC = 38      ......[Given]

∴ BQ + QC = 38    ......[B–Q–C]

∴ 27 + QC = 38    .......[Given]

∴ QC = 38 – 27

∴ QC = 11 units      ......(i)

Now, QC = SC    ......[Tangent segment theorem]

∴ SC = 11 units    .....(ii) [From (i)]

DC = 25     .......[Given]

∴ DS + SC = 25    ......[D–S–C]

∴ DS + 11 = 25   ......[From (ii)]

∴ DS = 25 – 11

∴ DS = 14 units   ......(iii)

In ▢DSOP,

∠P = ∠S = 90°    ......[Tangent theorem]

∠D = 90°    ......[Given]

∴ ∠O = 90°    ......[Remaning angle of ▢DSOP]

∴ ▢DSOP is a rectangle.

Also, OP = OS    ......[Radii of the same circle]

∴ ▢DSOP is a square    .......`[("A rectangle is square if its"),("adjacent sides are congruent")]`

∴ OS = DS = DP =  PO    .....(iv) [Sides of the square]

∴ OP = 14 units    ......[From (iii) and (iv)]

∴ The radius of the circle is 14 units.

items.title: In the figure, a circle with center C has m(arc AXB) = 100° then find central ∠ACB and measure m(arc AYB). - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: In the figure, a circle with center C has m(arc AXB) = 100° then find central ∠ACB and measure m(arc AYB).
items.solution_text: In the given figure, m(arc AXB) = ∠ACB ......[Definition of measure of minor arc] ∴ ∠ACB = 100° m(arc AXB) + m(arc AYB) = 360° ......[Measure of complete circle is 360°] ∴ 100° + m(arc AYB) = 360° ∴ m(arc AYB) = 360° – 100° ∴ m(arc AYB) = 260°
items.solution_html:
In the given figure, m(arc AXB) = ∠ACB  ......[Definition of measure of minor arc]

∴ ∠ACB = 100° 

m(arc AXB) + m(arc AYB) = 360°   ......[Measure of complete circle is 360°]

∴ 100° + m(arc AYB) = 360°

∴ m(arc AYB) = 360° – 100°

∴ m(arc AYB) = 260°

items.title: In the figure, a circle with center P touches the semicircle at points Q and C having center O. If diameter AB = 10, AC = 6, then find the radius x of the smaller circle. - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: In the figure, a circle with center P touches the semicircle at points Q and C having center O. If diameter AB = 10, AC = 6, then find the radius x of the smaller circle.
items.solution_text: Given: AB = 10 units, AC = 6 units, PC = PQ = x unit. To find: x Solution: Diameter AB = 10 ...[Given] ∴ Radius = `1/2 xx "AB" = 1/2 xx 10` = 5. ∴ OQ = AO = OB = 5 ......(i) AC = AO + OC .....[A–O–C] ∴ OC = AC – AO ∴ OC = 6 – 5 ......[Given and (i)] ∴ OC = 1 ......(ii) OQ = OP + PQ ......[O–P–Q] ∴ OP = OQ – PQ = 5 – x ......(iii) [From (i) and given] Note that seg AB is tangent to the given smaller circle at point C. ∴ ∠PCO = 90° ......[Tangent theorem] ∴ In ∆PCO, ∠PCO = 90° ∴ OP 2 = PC 2 + OC 2 ......[Pythagoras theorem] ∴ (5 – x) 2 = x 2 + (1) 2 ......[From (ii) and (iii)] ∴ 25 – 10x + x 2 = x 2 + 1 ∴ 10x = 24 ∴ x = `24/10` = 2.4 ∴ The radius x of the smaller circle is 2.4 units
items.solution_html:
Given: AB = 10 units, AC = 6 units, PC = PQ = x unit.

To find: x

Solution:

Diameter AB = 10                      ...[Given]

∴ Radius = `1/2 xx "AB"  = 1/2 xx 10` = 5.

∴ OQ = AO = OB = 5    ......(i)

AC = AO + OC    .....[A–O–C]

∴ OC = AC – AO

∴ OC = 6 – 5     ......[Given and (i)]

∴ OC = 1     ......(ii)

OQ = OP + PQ    ......[O–P–Q]

∴ OP = OQ – PQ

= 5 – x      ......(iii) [From (i) and given]

Note that seg AB is tangent to the given smaller circle at point C.

∴ ∠PCO = 90°     ......[Tangent theorem]

∴ In ∆PCO,

∠PCO = 90°

∴ OP2 = PC2 + OC2    ......[Pythagoras theorem]

∴ (5 – x)2 = x2 + (1)2   ......[From (ii) and (iii)]

∴ 25 – 10x + x2 = x2 + 1

∴ 10x = 24

∴ x = `24/10` = 2.4

∴ The radius x of the smaller circle is 2.4 units

items.title: In the figure, ΔABC is an equilateral triangle. The angle bisector of ∠B will intersect the circumcircle ΔABC at point P. Then prove that: CQ = CA. - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: In the figure, ΔABC is an equilateral triangle. The angle bisector of ∠B will intersect the circumcircle ΔABC at point P. Then prove that: CQ = CA.
items.solution_text: ∆ABC is an equilateral triangle. ∴ ∠ABC = ∠ACB = ∠BAC = 60° ......(i) [Angles of an equilateral triangle] ∠CBP = `1/2` ∠ABC ......[Ray BP bisects ∠B] ∴ ∠CBP = `1/2 xx 60^circ` ......[From (i)] ∴ ∠CBP = 30° ∠CBP = ∠CAP = 30° ......[Angles inscribed in the same arc] ∴ ∠CAQ = 30° .....(ii) [A−P−Q] In ∆ABQ, ∠BAQ = ∠BAC + ∠CAQ .....[Angle addition property] ∴ ∠BAQ = 60° + 30° .....[From (i) and (ii)] ∴ ∠BAQ = 90° Also, ∠ABQ = 60° ......[From (i) and B−C−Q] ∴ ∠BQA = 30° .....[Remaining angle of ∆ABQ] ∴ ∠CQA = 30° ......(iii) [B−C−Q] In ∆CQA, ∠CAQ = ∠CQA .......[From (ii) and (iii)] ∴ CQ = CA ......[Converse of isosceles triangle theorem]
items.solution_html:
∆ABC is an equilateral triangle.

∴ ∠ABC = ∠ACB = ∠BAC = 60°   ......(i) [Angles of an equilateral triangle]

∠CBP = `1/2` ∠ABC      ......[Ray BP bisects ∠B]

∴ ∠CBP = `1/2 xx 60^circ`   ......[From (i)]

∴ ∠CBP = 30°

∠CBP = ∠CAP = 30°    ......[Angles inscribed in the same arc]

∴ ∠CAQ = 30°    .....(ii) [A−P−Q]

In ∆ABQ,

∠BAQ = ∠BAC + ∠CAQ   .....[Angle addition property]

∴ ∠BAQ = 60° + 30°   .....[From (i) and (ii)]

∴ ∠BAQ = 90°

Also, ∠ABQ = 60°  ......[From (i) and B−C−Q]

∴ ∠BQA = 30°    .....[Remaining angle of ∆ABQ]

∴ ∠CQA = 30°   ......(iii) [B−C−Q]

In ∆CQA,

∠CAQ = ∠CQA    .......[From (ii) and (iii)]

∴ CQ = CA    ......[Converse of isosceles triangle theorem]

items.title: In the figure, ▢ABCD is a cyclic quadrilateral. If m(arc ABC) = 230°, then find ∠ABC, ∠CDA, ∠CBE. - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: In the figure, ▢ABCD is a cyclic quadrilateral. If m(arc ABC) = 230°, then find ∠ABC, ∠CDA, ∠CBE.
items.solution_text: m(arc ABC) = 230° .....(i) [Given] ∴ m(arc ADC) + m(arc ABC) = 360° .......[Degree measure of a circle is 360°] ∴ m(arc ADC) = 360° – m(arc ABC) ∴ m(arc ADC) = 360° – 230° .......[From (i)] ∴ m(arc ADC) = 130° ∠ABC = `1/2` m (arc ADC) ......[Inscribed angle theorem] = `1/2 xx 130^circ` = 65° Now, ∠CDA = `1/2` m (arc ABC) ......[Inscribed angle theorem] ∴ ∠CDA = `1/2 xx 230^circ` ∴ ∠CDA = 115° ......(ii) ∠CBE = ∠CDA ......(iiii) [The exterior angle of a cyclic quadrilateral is equal to the interior opposite angle] ∴ ∠CBE = 115° .....[From (ii) and (iii)] ∴ ∠ABC = 65°, ∠CDA = 115°, ∠CBE = 115°.
items.solution_html:
m(arc ABC) = 230°   .....(i) [Given]

∴ m(arc ADC) + m(arc ABC) = 360°   .......[Degree measure of a circle is 360°]

∴ m(arc ADC) = 360° – m(arc ABC)

∴ m(arc ADC) = 360° – 230°   .......[From (i)]

∴ m(arc ADC) = 130°

∠ABC = `1/2` m (arc ADC)   ......[Inscribed angle theorem]

= `1/2 xx 130^circ`

= 65°

Now, ∠CDA = `1/2` m (arc ABC)   ......[Inscribed angle theorem]

∴ ∠CDA = `1/2 xx 230^circ`

∴ ∠CDA = 115°    ......(ii)

∠CBE = ∠CDA    ......(iiii) [The exterior angle of a cyclic quadrilateral is equal to the interior opposite angle]

∴ ∠CBE = 115°     .....[From (ii) and (iii)]

∴ ∠ABC = 65°, ∠CDA = 115°, ∠CBE = 115°.

items.title: In the figure, chord LM ≅ chord LN, ∠L = 35°. Find (i) m(arc MN) (ii) m(arc LN) - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: In the figure, chord LM ≅ chord LN, ∠L = 35°. Find (i) m(arc MN) (ii) m(arc LN)
items.solution_text: (i) ∠L = `1/2` m(arc MN) ...[Inscribed angle theorem] ∴ 35° = `1/2` m(arc MN) ∴ 2 × 35° = m(arc MN) ∴ m(arc MN) = 70°. (ii) In ∆LMN, chord LM ≅ chord LN ∴ ∠M = ∠N ...[Isosceles triangle theorem] ∴ ∠L + ∠M + ∠N = 180° ...[Sum of the measures of angles of a triangle is 180°] ∴ 35° + ∠M + ∠M = 180° ∴ 2∠M = 180° – 35° = 145° ∴ ∠M = `145^circ/2` Now, m(arc LN) = 2 × ∠M ......[Inscribed angle theorem] = `2 xx 145^circ/2` = 145°
items.solution_html:
(i) ∠L = `1/2` m(arc MN)     ...[Inscribed angle theorem]

∴ 35° = `1/2` m(arc MN)

∴ 2 × 35° = m(arc MN)

∴ m(arc MN) = 70°.

(ii) In ∆LMN,

chord LM ≅ chord LN

∴ ∠M = ∠N                         ...[Isosceles triangle theorem]

∴ ∠L + ∠M + ∠N = 180°   ...[Sum of the measures of angles of a triangle is 180°]

∴ 35° + ∠M + ∠M = 180°

∴ 2∠M = 180° – 35° = 145°

∴ ∠M = `145^circ/2`

Now, m(arc LN) = 2 × ∠M   ......[Inscribed angle theorem]

= `2 xx 145^circ/2`

= 145°

items.title: In the figure, if ∠ABC = 35°, then find m(arc AXC)? - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: In the figure, if ∠ABC = 35°, then find m(arc AXC)?
items.solution_text: In the figure, arc AXC is intercepted by ∠ABC. ∴ ∠ABC = `1/2` m(arc AXC) ...[The measure of an inscribed angle is half of the measure of the arc intercepted by it] ∴ m(arc AXC) = 2∠ABC ∴ m(arc AXC) = 2 × 35° ∴ m(arc AXC) = 70°
items.solution_html:
In the figure, arc AXC is intercepted by ∠ABC.

∴ ∠ABC = `1/2` m(arc AXC)    ...[The measure of an inscribed angle is half of the measure of the arc intercepted by it]

∴ m(arc AXC) = 2∠ABC

∴ m(arc AXC) = 2 × 35°

∴ m(arc AXC) = 70°

items.title: In the figure, if O is the center of the circle and two chords of the circle EF and GH are parallel to each other. Show that ∠EOG ≅ ∠FOH - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: In the figure, if O is the center of the circle and two chords of the circle EF and GH are parallel to each other. Show that ∠EOG ≅ ∠FOH
items.solution_text: Construction: Draw seg EO, seg FO, seg GO, seg HO, seg GF. Proof: Chord EF || chord GH, GF is the transversal ∠EFG ≅ ∠HGF .....(i) [Alternate angles] `{:(∠"EFG" = 1/2 "m"("arc GE")),(∠"HGF" = 1/2 "m"("arc HF")):}}` `{:(......("ii")),(......["Inscribed angle theorem"]),(......("iii")):}` ∴ m(arc GE) = m(arc HF) .....(iv) [From (i), (ii) and (iii)] `{:("Also""," ∠"EOG" = "m"("arc GE")),(∠"FOH" = "m"("arc HF")):}}``{:(......("v") ["Definition of measure of arc"]),(.....("vi")):}` ∴ ∠EOG = ∠FOH .....[From (iv), (v) and (vi)]
items.solution_html:
Construction: Draw seg EO, seg FO, seg GO, seg HO, seg GF.

Proof:

Chord EF || chord GH, GF is the transversal

∠EFG ≅ ∠HGF    .....(i) [Alternate angles]

`{:(∠"EFG" = 1/2 "m"("arc GE")),(∠"HGF" = 1/2 "m"("arc HF")):}}` `{:(......("ii")),(......["Inscribed angle theorem"]),(......("iii")):}`

∴ m(arc GE) = m(arc HF)    .....(iv) [From (i), (ii) and (iii)]

`{:("Also"","  ∠"EOG" = "m"("arc GE")),(∠"FOH" = "m"("arc HF")):}}``{:(......("v") ["Definition of measure of arc"]),(.....("vi")):}`

∴ ∠EOG = ∠FOH     .....[From (iv), (v) and (vi)]

items.title: In the figure if ∠PQR = 50°, then find ∠PSR. - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: In the figure if ∠PQR = 50°, then find ∠PSR.
items.solution_text: In given figure, ∠PQR ≅ ∠PSR ......[Angles inscribed in the same arc are congruent] ∴ ∠PSR = 50°
items.solution_html:
In given figure,

∠PQR ≅ ∠PSR     ......[Angles inscribed in the same arc are congruent]

∴ ∠PSR = 50°

items.title: In the figure, if the chord PQ and chord RS intersect at point T, prove that: m∠STQ = 12 [m(arc PR) + m(arc SQ)] for any measure of ∠STQ by filling out the boxesProof: m∠STQ = m∠SPQ + □ .....[Theorem - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: In the figure, if the chord PQ and chord RS intersect at point T, prove that: m∠STQ = `1/2` [m(arc PR) + m(arc SQ)] for any measure of ∠STQ by filling out the boxes Proof: m∠STQ = m∠SPQ + `square` .....[Theorem of the external angle of a triangle] = `1/2` m(arc SQ) + `square` .....[Inscribed angle theorem] = `1/2 [square + square]`
items.solution_text: m∠STQ = m∠SPQ + m∠PSR .....[Theorem of the external angle of a triangle] = `1/2` m(arc SQ) + `1/2 "m"("arc PR")` .....[Inscribed angle theorem] = `1/2 ["m"("arc PR") + "m"("arc SQ")]`
items.solution_html:
m∠STQ = m∠SPQ + m∠PSR  .....[Theorem of the external angle of a triangle]

 = `1/2` m(arc SQ) + `1/2 "m"("arc PR")`   .....[Inscribed angle theorem]

= `1/2 ["m"("arc PR") + "m"("arc SQ")]`

items.title: In the figure, line l touches the circle with centre O at point P. Q is the midpoint of radius OP. RS is a chord through Q such that chords RS || line l. If RS = 12, find the radius of the circle. - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: In the figure, line l touches the circle with center O at point P. Q is the midpoint of radius OP. RS is a chord through Q such that chords RS || line l. If RS = 12, find the radius of the circle.
items.solution_text: Let the radius of the circle be r. line l is the tangent to the circle and seg OP is the radius. ...[Given] ∴ seg OP ⊥ line l ...[Tangent theorem] chord RS || line l ...[Given] ∴ seg OP ⊥ chord RS ∴ QS = `1/2` RS ...[Perpendicular drawn from the center of the circle to the chord bisects the chord] ∴ QS = `1/2 xx 12` ∴ QR = QS = 6 cm Also, OQ = `1/2` OP ......[Q is the midpoint of OP] ∴ OQ = `1/2` r In ∆OQS, ∠OQS = 90° ....[seg OP ⊥ chord RS ] ∴ OS 2 = OQ 2 + QS 2 ...[Pythagoras theorem] ∴ r 2 = `(1/2 "r")^2 + 6^2` ∴ r 2 = `1/4 "r"^2` + 36 ∴ r 2 - `1/4 "r"^2` = 36 ∴ `3/4 "r"^2` = 36 ∴ r 2 = `(36 xx 4)/3` ∴ r 2 = 48 ∴ r = `sqrt(48)` ∴ r = `4sqrt(3)` cm ....[Taking square root of both sides] ∴ The radius of the given circle is `4sqrt(3)` cm.
items.solution_html:
Let the radius of the circle be r.

line l is the tangent to the circle and
seg OP is the radius.    ...[Given]

∴ seg OP ⊥ line l         ...[Tangent theorem]

chord RS || line l          ...[Given]

∴ seg OP ⊥ chord RS

∴ QS = `1/2` RS          ...[Perpendicular drawn from the center of the circle to the chord bisects the chord]

∴ QS = `1/2 xx 12`

∴ QR = QS = 6 cm

Also,

OQ = `1/2` OP       ......[Q is the midpoint of OP]

∴ OQ = `1/2` r

In ∆OQS,

∠OQS = 90°                   ....[seg OP ⊥ chord RS ]

∴ OS2 = OQ2 + QS2      ...[Pythagoras theorem]

∴ r2 = `(1/2 "r")^2 + 6^2`

∴ r2  = `1/4 "r"^2` + 36

∴ r2  - `1/4 "r"^2` = 36

∴ `3/4 "r"^2` = 36

∴ r2 = `(36 xx 4)/3`

∴ r2 = 48

∴ r = `sqrt(48)`

∴ r = `4sqrt(3)` cm        ....[Taking square root of both sides]

∴ The radius of the given circle is `4sqrt(3)` cm.

appears_in.text: Balbharati Mathematics 2 [English] Standard 10 Maharashtra State Board

items.title: In the figure, O is the center of the circle. Line AQ is a tangent. If OP = 3, m(arc PM) = 120°, then find the length of AP. - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: In the figure, O is the center of the circle. Line AQ is a tangent. If OP = 3, m(arc PM) = 120°, then find the length of AP.
items.solution_text: Given: line AQ is a tangent. OP = 3, m(arc PM) = 120° To find: AP In the given figure, arc PMQ is a semicircle. ∴ m(arc PMQ) = 180° ......[Measure of semicircular arc is 180°] ∴ m(arc PM) + m(arc MQ) = 180° .....[Arc addition property] ∴ 120° + m(arc MQ) = 180° ......[Given] ∴ m(arc MQ) = 180° – 120° ∴ m(arc MQ) = 60° .......(i) ∠MPQ = `1/2` m(arc MQ) .....[Inscribed angle theorem] ∴ ∠MPQ = `1/2 xx 60^circ` ......[From (i)] ∴ ∠MPQ = 30° i.e., ∠APQ = 30° ......(ii) [A – M – P] In ∆PQA, ∠PQA = 90° ......[Tangent theorem] ∠ APQ = 30° ......[From (ii)] ∴ ∠PAQ = 60° ......[Remaning angle of ∆PQA] ∴ ∆PAQ is 30° – 60° – 90° triangle. ∴ PQ = `sqrt(3)/2` AP ......[Side opposite to 60°] ∴ (PO + OQ) = `sqrt(3)/2` AP ......[P – O – Q] ∴ (3 + 3) = `sqrt(3)/2` AP ......[Radii of same circle and op = 3] ∴ AP = `(6 xx 2)/sqrt(3)` ∴ AP = `(6 xx 2 xx sqrt(3))/(sqrt(3) xx sqrt(3))` ......[Multiply and divide by `sqrt(3)`] ∴ AP = `(6 xx 2 xx sqrt(3))/3` ∴ AP = `2 xx 2sqrt(3)` ∴ AP = `4sqrt(3)` units
items.solution_html:
Given: line AQ is a tangent.

OP = 3, m(arc PM) = 120°

To find: AP

In the given figure, arc PMQ is a semicircle.

∴ m(arc PMQ) = 180°     ......[Measure of semicircular arc is 180°]

∴ m(arc PM) + m(arc MQ) = 180°    .....[Arc addition property]

∴ 120° + m(arc MQ) = 180°   ......[Given]

∴ m(arc MQ) = 180° – 120°

∴ m(arc MQ) = 60°    .......(i)

∠MPQ = `1/2` m(arc MQ)    .....[Inscribed angle theorem]

∴ ∠MPQ = `1/2 xx 60^circ`     ......[From (i)]

∴ ∠MPQ = 30°

i.e., ∠APQ = 30°    ......(ii) [A – M – P]

In ∆PQA, ∠PQA = 90°    ......[Tangent theorem]

∠ APQ = 30°     ......[From (ii)]

∴ ∠PAQ = 60°    ......[Remaning angle of ∆PQA]

∴ ∆PAQ is 30° – 60° – 90° triangle.

∴ PQ = `sqrt(3)/2` AP    ......[Side opposite to 60°]

∴ (PO + OQ) = `sqrt(3)/2` AP    ......[P – O – Q]

∴ (3 + 3) = `sqrt(3)/2` AP     ......[Radii of same circle and op = 3]

∴ AP = `(6 xx 2)/sqrt(3)`

∴ AP = `(6 xx 2 xx sqrt(3))/(sqrt(3) xx sqrt(3))`  ......[Multiply and divide by `sqrt(3)`]

∴ AP = `(6 xx 2 xx sqrt(3))/3`

∴ AP = `2 xx 2sqrt(3)`

∴ AP = `4sqrt(3)` units

items.title: In the figure, O is the centre of the circle and ∠AOB = 90°, ∠ABC = 30°. Then find ∠CAB. - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: In the figure, O is the centre of the circle, and ∠AOB = 90°, ∠ABC = 30°. Then find ∠CAB.
items.solution_text: Given: ∠AOB = 90°, ∠ABC = 30° To find: ∠CAB Solution: In given figure, ∠AOB = m (arc AB) ......[Definition of measure of minor arc] ∴ m(arc AB) = 90° ......(i) Also, ∠ACB = `1/2` m(arc AB) .....[Inscribed angle theorem] ∴ ∠ACB = `1/2 xx 90^circ` ......[From (i)] ∴ ∠ACB = 45° .....(ii) In ∆ACB, ∠CAB + ∠ABC + ∠ACB = 180° ......`[("Sum of the measures of"),("angles of a triangle is" 180^circ)]` ∴ ∠CAB + 30° + 45° = 180° ......[From (ii)] ∴ ∠CAB + 75° = 180° ∴ ∠CAB = 180° – 75° ∠CAB = 105°
items.solution_html:
Given: ∠AOB = 90°, ∠ABC = 30°

To find: ∠CAB

Solution:

In given figure, 

∠AOB = m (arc AB)   ......[Definition of measure of minor arc]

∴ m(arc AB) = 90°     ......(i)

Also, ∠ACB = `1/2` m(arc AB)    .....[Inscribed angle theorem]

∴ ∠ACB = `1/2 xx 90^circ`    ......[From (i)]

∴ ∠ACB = 45°    .....(ii)

In ∆ACB,

∠CAB + ∠ABC + ∠ACB = 180°    ......`[("Sum of the measures of"),("angles of a triangle is"  180^circ)]`

∴ ∠CAB + 30° + 45° = 180°    ......[From (ii)]

∴ ∠CAB + 75° = 180°

∴ ∠CAB = 180° – 75°

∠CAB = 105°

items.title: In the figure, PQRS is cyclic, side PQ ≅ side RQ, ∠PSR = 110°. Find (i) measure of ∠PQR (ii) m(arc PQR) (iii) m(arc QR) - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: In the figure, PQRS is cyclic, side PQ ≅ side RQ, ∠PSR = 110°. Find (i) measure of ∠PQR (ii) m(arc PQR) (iii) m(arc QR)
items.solution_text: (i) ▢PQRS is a cyclic quadrilateral. ...[Given] ∴ ∠PSR + ∠PQR = 180° ...[Opposite angles of a cyclic quadrilateral are supplementary] ∴ 110° + ∠PQR = 180° ∴ ∠PQR = 180° − 110° ∴ m∠PQR = 70° (ii) ∠PSR = `1/2` m(arc PQR) .....[Inscribed angle theorem] ∴ 110° = `1/2` m(arc PQR) ∴ m(arc PQR) = 220° (iii) In ∆PQR, Side PQ ≅ side RQ ...[Given] ∴ ∠PRQ ≅ ∠QPR ...[Isosceles triangle theorem] Let ∠PRQ = ∠QPR = x Now, ∠PQR + ∠QPR + ∠PRQ = 180° ...[Sum of the measures of angles of a triangle is 180°] ∴ ∠PQR + x + x = 180° ∴ 70° + 2x = 180° ∴ 2x = 180° − 70° ∴ 2x = 110° ∴ x = `110^circ/2` ∴ x = 55° ∴ ∠PRQ = ∠QPR = 55° ......(i) But, ∠QPR = `1/2` m(arc QR) .....[Inscribed angle theorem] ∴ 55° = `1/2` m(arc QR) ∴ m(arc QR) = 110°
items.solution_html:
(i) ▢PQRS is a cyclic quadrilateral.    ...[Given]

∴ ∠PSR + ∠PQR = 180°    ...[Opposite angles of a cyclic quadrilateral are supplementary]

∴ 110° + ∠PQR = 180°

∴ ∠PQR = 180° − 110°

∴ m∠PQR = 70°

(ii) ∠PSR = `1/2` m(arc PQR)     .....[Inscribed angle theorem]

∴ 110° = `1/2` m(arc PQR)

∴ m(arc PQR) = 220°

(iii) In ∆PQR,

Side PQ ≅ side RQ     ...[Given]

∴ ∠PRQ ≅ ∠QPR     ...[Isosceles triangle theorem]

Let ∠PRQ = ∠QPR = x

Now, ∠PQR + ∠QPR + ∠PRQ = 180°    ...[Sum of the measures of angles of a triangle is 180°]

∴ ∠PQR + x + x = 180°

∴ 70° + 2x = 180°

∴ 2x = 180° − 70°

∴ 2x = 110°

∴ x = `110^circ/2`

∴ x = 55°

∴ ∠PRQ = ∠QPR = 55°    ......(i)

But, ∠QPR = `1/2` m(arc QR)   .....[Inscribed angle theorem]

∴ 55° = `1/2` m(arc QR)

∴ m(arc QR) = 110°

items.title: In the figure, quadrilateral ABCD is a cyclic. If ∠DAB = 75°, then find measure of ∠DCB. - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: In the figure, quadrilateral ABCD is a cyclic. If ∠DAB = 75°, then find measure of ∠DCB.
items.solution_text: In ▢ABCD, ∠DAB = 75° .....[Given] ∠DAB + ∠DCB = 180° ......[Opposite angle of cyclic quadrilateral are supplementary] ∴ 75° + ∠ DCB = 180° ∴ ∠ DCB = 180° – 75° ∴ ∠ DCB = 105°
items.solution_html:
In ▢ABCD,

∠DAB = 75°    .....[Given]

∠DAB + ∠DCB = 180°  ......[Opposite angle of cyclic quadrilateral are supplementary]

∴ 75° + ∠ DCB = 180°

∴ ∠ DCB = 180° – 75°

∴ ∠ DCB = 105°

items.title: In the figure, quadrilateral ABCD is cyclic. If m(arc BC) = 90° and ∠DBC = 55°, then find the measure of ∠BCD. - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: In the figure, quadrilateral ABCD is cyclic. If m(arc BC) = 90° and ∠DBC = 55°, then find the measure of ∠BCD.
items.solution_text: Given: m(arc BC) = 90°, ∠DBC = 55° To find: ∠BCD Solution: ∠BDC = `1/2` m(arc BC) ......[Inscribed angle theorem] ∴ ∠BDC = `1/2 xx 90^circ` ......[Given] ∴ ∠BDC = 45° ......(i) In ∆BCD, ∠BDC + ∠DBC + ∠BCD = 180° ......[Sum of the measures of all angles of a triangle is 180°] ∴ 45° + 55° + ∠BCD = 180° .....[From (i) and given] ∴ 100° + ∠BCD = 180° ∴ ∠BCD = 180° – 100° ∴ ∠BCD = 80° ∴ The measure of ∠BCD is 80°.
items.solution_html:
Given: m(arc BC) = 90°, ∠DBC = 55°

To find: ∠BCD

Solution:

∠BDC = `1/2` m(arc BC)    ......[Inscribed angle theorem]

∴ ∠BDC = `1/2 xx 90^circ`    ......[Given]

∴ ∠BDC = 45°    ......(i)

In ∆BCD,

∠BDC + ∠DBC + ∠BCD = 180°   ......[Sum of the measures of all angles of a triangle is 180°]

∴ 45° + 55° + ∠BCD = 180°   .....[From (i) and given]

∴ 100° + ∠BCD = 180°

∴ ∠BCD = 180° – 100°

∴ ∠BCD = 80°

∴ The measure of ∠BCD is 80°.

items.title: In the figure, seg AB is a diameter of a circle with centre O. The bisector of ∠ACB intersects the circle at point D. Prove that, seg AD ≅ seg BD. - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: In the figure, seg AB is a diameter of a circle with centre O. The bisector of ∠ACB intersects the circle at point D. Prove that, seg AD ≅ seg BD. Complete the following proof by filling in the blanks. Proof: Draw seg OD. ∠ACB = `square` ......[Angle inscribed in semicircle] ∠DCB = `square` ......[CD is the bisector of ∠C] m(arc DB) = `square` ......[Inscribed angle theorem] ∠DOB = `square` ......[Definition of measure of an arc](i) seg OA ≅ seg OB ...... `square` (ii) ∴ Line OD is `square` of seg AB ......[From (i) and (ii)] ∴ seg AD ≅ seg BD
items.solution_text: Proof: ∠ACB = 90° ......[Angle inscribed in semicircle] ∠DCB = ∠DCA = 45° ......[CD is the bisector of ∠C] m(arc DB) = 2∠DCB = 90° ......[Inscribed angle theorem] ∠DOB = m(arc DB) = 90° ......[Definition of measure of an arc](i) seg OA ≅ seg OB ...... [Radii of the same circle] (ii) ∴ Line OD is perpendicular bisector of seg AB ......[From (i) and (ii)] ∴ seg AD ≅ seg BD
items.solution_html:
Proof: 

∠ACB = 90°     ......[Angle inscribed in semicircle]

∠DCB = ∠DCA = 45°    ......[CD is the bisector of ∠C]

m(arc DB) = 2∠DCB = 90°    ......[Inscribed angle theorem]

∠DOB = m(arc DB) = 90°   ......[Definition of measure of an arc](i)

seg OA ≅ seg OB     ......[Radii of the same circle] (ii)

∴ Line OD is perpendicular bisector of seg AB    ......[From (i) and (ii)]

∴ seg AD ≅ seg BD

items.title: In the figure, segment PQ is the diameter of the circle with center O. The tangent to the tangent circle drawn from point C on it, intersects the tangents drawn from points P and Q at points A and B - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: In the figure, segment PQ is the diameter of the circle with center O. The tangent to the tangent circle drawn from point C on it, intersects the tangents drawn from points P and Q at points A and B respectively, prove that ∠AOB = 90°
items.solution_text: Given: PQ is the diameter of the circle. Point P, Q, C are points of contact of the respective tangents. To prove: ∠AOB = 90° Construction: Draw seg OC Proof: In ∆OPA and ∆OCA, side OP ≅ side OC ......[Radii of the same circle] side OA ≅ side OA ......[Common side] side PA ≅ side CA ......[Tangent segment theorem] ∴ ∆OPA ≅ ∠OCA .....[[SSS test of congruency] ∴ ∠AOP ≅ ∠AOC ......[C.A.C.T.] Let m∠AOP = m∠AOC = x ......(i) Similarly, we can prove that ∠BOC ≅ ∠BOQ. Let m∠BOC = m∠BOQ = y ......(ii) m∠AOP + m∠AOC + m∠BOC + m∠BOQ = 180° .....[Linear angles] ∴ x + x + y + y = 180° ......[From (i) and (ii)] ∴ 2x + 2y = 180° ∴ 2(x + y) = 180° ∴ x + y = 90° ......(iii) Now ∠AOB = ∠AOC + ∠BOC = x + y ......[From (i) and (ii)] ∴ ∠AOB = ∠AOC + ∠BOC = x + y ∴ ∠AOB = 90° .....[From (iii)]
items.solution_html:
Given: PQ is the diameter of the circle.

Point P, Q, C are points of contact of the respective tangents.

To prove: ∠AOB = 90°

Construction: Draw seg OC

Proof:
In ∆OPA and ∆OCA,

side OP ≅ side OC    ......[Radii of the same circle]

side OA ≅ side OA   ......[Common side]

side PA ≅ side CA   ......[Tangent segment theorem]

∴ ∆OPA ≅ ∠OCA   .....[[SSS test of congruency]

∴ ∠AOP ≅ ∠AOC   ......[C.A.C.T.]

Let m∠AOP = m∠AOC = x   ......(i)

Similarly, we can prove that ∠BOC ≅ ∠BOQ.

Let m∠BOC = m∠BOQ = y   ......(ii)

m∠AOP + m∠AOC + m∠BOC + m∠BOQ = 180°  .....[Linear angles]

∴ x + x + y + y = 180°    ......[From (i) and (ii)]

∴ 2x + 2y = 180°

∴ 2(x + y) = 180°

∴ x + y = 90°  ......(iii)

Now ∠AOB = ∠AOC + ∠BOC

= x + y   ......[From (i) and (ii)]

∴ ∠AOB = ∠AOC + ∠BOC

= x + y    

∴ ∠AOB = 90°   .....[From (iii)]

items.title: In the given figure, chord AB ≅ chord CD, Prove that, arc AC ≅ arc BD. - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: In the given figure, chord AB ≅ chord CD, Prove that, arc AC ≅ arc BD.
items.solution_text: Chord AB ≅ Chord CD ...(Given) ∴ Arc ACB ≅ Arc CBD ...(Arcs corresponding to congruent chords.) ∴ m(arc ACB) = m(arc CBD) ...(1) But m(arc ACB) = m(arc AC) + m(arc CB) ...(2) and m(arc CBD) = m(arc CB) + m(arc BD) ...(3) From (1), (2), and (3), we get, m(arc AC) + m(arc CB) = m(arc CB) + m(arc BD) ∴ m(arc AC) = m(arc BD) ∴ arc AC ≅ arc BD Hence, proved.
items.solution_html:
Chord AB ≅ Chord CD   ...(Given)

∴ Arc ACB ≅ Arc CBD   ...(Arcs corresponding to congruent chords.)

∴ m(arc ACB) = m(arc CBD)   ...(1)

But m(arc ACB) = m(arc AC) + m(arc CB)   ...(2)

and m(arc CBD) = m(arc CB) + m(arc BD)   ...(3)

From (1), (2), and (3), we get,

m(arc AC) + m(arc CB) = m(arc CB) + m(arc BD)

∴ m(arc AC) = m(arc BD)

∴ arc AC ≅ arc BD

Hence, proved.

items.title: In the given figure, m(arc NS) = 125°, m(arc EF) = 37°, find the measure ∠NMS. - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: In the given figure, m(arc NS) = 125°, m(arc EF) = 37°, find the measure ∠NMS.
items.solution_text: m(arc NS) = 125° m(arc EF) = 37° Chords EN and FS intersect externally at point M. ∴ m∠NMS = `1/2 ["m"("arc NS") - "m"("arc EF")]` = `1/2 (125^circ - 37^circ)` = `1/2 xx 88^circ` ∴ m∠NMS = 44°
items.solution_html:
m(arc NS) = 125°

m(arc EF) = 37°

Chords EN and FS intersect externally at point M.

∴ m∠NMS = `1/2 ["m"("arc NS") - "m"("arc EF")]`

= `1/2 (125^circ - 37^circ)`

= `1/2 xx 88^circ`

∴ m∠NMS = 44°

items.title: In the given figure, O is the centre of the circle. Seg AB, seg AC are tangent segments. Radius of the circle is r and l(AB) = r, Prove that, ▢ABOC is a square. Proof: Draw segment OB and O - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: In the given figure, O is the centre of the circle. Seg AB, seg AC are tangent segments. Radius of the circle is r and l(AB) = r, Prove that ▢ABOC is a square. Proof: Draw segment OB and OC. l(AB) = r ......[Given] (I) AB = AC ......[`square`] (II) But OB = OC = r ......[`square`] (III) From (i), (ii) and (iii) AB = `square` = OB = OC = r ∴ Quadrilateral ABOC is `square` Similarly, ∠OBA = `square` ......[Tangent Theorem] If one angle of `square` is right angle, then it is a square. ∴ Quadrilateral ABOC is a square.
items.solution_text: Proof: Draw segment OB and OC. l(AB) = r ......[Given] (I) AB = AC ......[ Tangent segment theorem ] (II) But OB = OC = r ...... [Radii of the same circle ] (III) From (I), (II) and (III) AB = AC = OB = OC = r ∴ Quadrilateral ABOC is rhombus Similarly, ∠OBA = 90° ......[Tangent Theorem] If one angle of rhombus is right angle, then it is a square. ∴ Quadrilateral ABOC is a square.
items.solution_html:
Proof: Draw segment OB and OC.

l(AB) = r      ......[Given] (I)

AB = AC    ......[Tangent segment theorem] (II)

But OB = OC = r    ......[Radii of the same circle] (III)

From (I), (II) and (III)

AB = AC = OB = OC = r

∴ Quadrilateral ABOC is rhombus

Similarly, ∠OBA = 90°      ......[Tangent Theorem]

If one angle of rhombus is right angle, then it is a square.

∴ Quadrilateral ABOC is a square.

items.title: In triangle ABC point D is on side BC (B−D−C) such that ∠BAC = ∠ADC then prove that CA2 = CB × CD - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: In triangle ABC point D is on side BC (B−D−C) such that ∠BAC = ∠ADC then prove that CA 2 = CB × CD
items.solution_text: In ΔBAC and ΔADC, ∠BAC ≅ ∠ADC ......[Given] ∠BCA ≅ ∠ACD ......[Common angle] ∴ ΔBAC ∼ ΔADC .....[AA test of similarity] ∴ `"CA"/"CD" = "CB"/"CA"` ......[Corresponding sides of similar triangles] ∴ CA × CA = CB × CD ∴ CA 2 = CB × CD
items.solution_html:
In ΔBAC and ΔADC,

∠BAC ≅ ∠ADC      ......[Given]

∠BCA ≅ ∠ACD    ......[Common angle]

∴ ΔBAC ∼ ΔADC  .....[AA test of similarity]

∴ `"CA"/"CD" = "CB"/"CA"`  ......[Corresponding sides of similar triangles]

∴ CA × CA = CB × CD

∴ CA2 = CB × CD

items.title: Length of a tangent segment drawn from a point which is at a distance 15 cm from the centre of a circle is 12 cm, find the diameter of the circle? - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: Length of a tangent segment drawn from a point which is at a distance 15 cm from the centre of a circle is 12 cm, find the diameter of the circle?
items.solution_text: Let seg BC be the tangent to the circle with centre A from point C. ∴ AC = 15 cm, BC = 12 cm ......[Given] AB is the radius of the circle. ∴ In ∆ABC, ∠ABC = 90° ......[Tangent theorem] ∴ AC 2 = AB 2 + BC 2 ∴ (15) 2 = AB 2 + (12) 2 ∴ 225 = AB 2 + 144 ∴ AB 2 = 225 – 144 ∴ AB 2 = 81 ∴ AB = 9 .....[Taking square root of both sides] i.e., radius of the circle is 9 cm. ∴ The diameter of the circle is 18 cm.
items.solution_html:
Let seg BC be the tangent to the circle with centre A from point C.

∴ AC = 15 cm, BC = 12 cm     ......[Given]

AB is the radius of the circle.

∴ In ∆ABC,

∠ABC = 90°                         ......[Tangent theorem]

∴ AC2 = AB2 + BC2

∴ (15)2 = AB2 + (12)2

∴ 225 = AB2 + 144

∴ AB2 = 225 – 144

∴ AB2 = 81

∴ AB = 9                          .....[Taking square root of both sides]

i.e., radius of the circle is 9 cm.

∴ The diameter of the circle is 18 cm.

items.title: Line ℓ touches a circle with centre O at point P. If radius of the circle is 9 cm, answer the following. If d(O, Q) = 8 cm, where does the point Q lie? - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: Line ℓ touches a circle with center O at point P. If the radius of the circle is 9 cm, answer the following. If d(O, Q) = 8 cm, where does the point Q lie?
items.solution_text: Here, 8 cm < 9 cm ∴ d(O, Q) < d(O, P) ∴ d(O, Q) < radius ∴ Point Q lies in the interior of the circle.
items.solution_html:
Here, 8 cm < 9 cm

∴ d(O, Q) < d(O, P)

∴ d(O, Q) < radius

∴ Point Q lies in the interior of the circle.

items.title: Line ℓ touches a circle with centre O at point P. If radius of the circle is 9 cm, answer the following. If d(O, R) = 15 cm, how many locations of point R are line on line l? At what distance will ea - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: Line ℓ touches a circle with centre O at point P. If radius of the circle is 9 cm, answer the following. If d(O, R) = 15 cm, how many locations of point R are line on line l? At what distance will each of them be from point P?
items.solution_text: There can be two locations of point R on line l. d(O, R) = 15 cm Now, in ∆OPR, ∠OPR = 90° ......[Tangent theorem] ∴ OR 2 = OP 2 + PR 2 ......[Pythagoras theorem] ∴ 15 2 = 9 2 + PR 2 ∴ 225 = 81 + PR 2 ∴ PR 2 = 225 – 81 = 144 ∴ PR = `sqrt(144)` = 12 cm ∴ Each location of point R will be at a distance of 12 cm from point P. ......[Taking square root of both sides]
items.solution_html:
There can be two locations of point R on line l.

d(O, R) = 15 cm

Now, in ∆OPR, ∠OPR = 90°    ......[Tangent theorem]

∴ OR2 = OP2 + PR2    ......[Pythagoras theorem]

∴ 152 = 92 + PR2

∴ 225 = 81 + PR2

∴ PR2 = 225 – 81 = 144

∴ PR = `sqrt(144)`

= 12 cm

∴ Each location of point R will be at a distance of 12 cm from point P.    ......[Taking square root of both sides]

items.title: MRPN is cyclic, ∠R = (5x – 13)°, ∠N = (4x + 4)°. Find measures of ∠R and ∠N, by completing the following activity. Solution:  MRPN is cyclic The apposite angles of a cyclic square are □ ∠R + ∠N = □ - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: MRPN is cyclic, ∠R = (5x – 13)°, ∠N = (4x + 4)°. Find measures of ∠R and ∠N, by completing the following activity. Solution: MRPN is cyclic The opposite angles of a cyclic square are `square` ∠R + ∠N = `square` ∴ (5x – 13)° + (4x + 4)° = `square` ∴ 9x = 189° ∴ x = `square` ∴ ∠R = (5x – 13)° = `square` ∴ ∠N = (4x + 4)° = `square`
items.solution_text: MRPN is a cyclic quadrilateral. ......[Given] Opposite angles of a cyclic quadrilateral are supplementary. ∴ ∠R + ∠N = 180° ∴ (5x – 13)° + (4x + 4) ° = 180° ∴ 9x − 9 = 180 ∴ 9x = 189 ∴ x = `189/9` ∴ x = 21 ∴ ∠R = (5x – 13)° ∴ ∠R = (5 × 21 − 13)° ∴ ∠R = (105 − 13)° ∴ ∠R = 92° ∴ ∠N = (4x + 4)° ∴ ∠N = (4 × 21 + 4)° ∴ ∠N = (84 + 4)° ∴ ∠N = 88° ∴ m∠R = 92° and m ∠N = 88°
items.solution_html:
MRPN is a cyclic quadrilateral.    ......[Given]

Opposite angles of a cyclic quadrilateral are supplementary.

∴ ∠R + ∠N = 180°

∴ (5x – 13)° + (4x + 4) ° = 180°

∴ 9x − 9 = 180

∴ 9x = 189

∴ x = `189/9`

∴ x = 21

∴ ∠R = (5x – 13)°

∴ ∠R = (5 × 21 − 13)°

∴ ∠R = (105 − 13)°

∴ ∠R = 92°

∴ ∠N = (4x + 4)°

∴ ∠N = (4 × 21 + 4)°

∴ ∠N = (84 + 4)°

∴ ∠N = 88°

∴ m∠R = 92° and m ∠N = 88°

items.title: Point P is at a distance of 6 cm from line AB. Draw a circle of radius 4 cm passing through point P so that line AB is the tangent to the circle - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: Point P is at a distance of 6 cm from line AB. Draw a circle of radius 4 cm passing through point P so that line AB is the tangent to the circle
items.solution_text: Analysis: As shown in the figure, A circle of radius 4 cm has center O. Perpendicular distance of P from line AB = 6 cm. ∴ PY = 6 cm and ∠PYA = 90° ......(i) seg OX is a perpendicular from point O to seg PY at point X. Line AB is a tangent to the circle at point Z. ∴ OZ is radius of the circle. In ▢XOZY, ∠X = 90° ......[∵ OX ⊥ PY] ∠Y = 90° ......[From (i)] ∠Z = 90° ......[Tangent theorem] ∴ ∠O = 90° ......[Remaning angle of ▢XOZY] ∴ ▢XOZY is a rectangle. . ∴ seg ZY = seg OX and seg XO = seg YZ ......[Opposite sides of a rectangle] But OZ = 4 cm ......…[Radius of the circle] ∴ XY = 4 cm Steps of construction: Draw line AB. From any point Q on line AB, draw a, perpendicular PQ of 6 cm. Draw point X on seg PQ such that seg XQ = 4 cm. Draw ray XY interior of the circle such that ∠QXY = 90° Taking distance of 4 cm in compass, draw an arc with centre P on ray XY and name the point of intersection as O. With centre O, draw a circle of radius 4 cm. Which is the required circle.
items.solution_html:
Analysis: As shown in the figure, A circle of radius 4 cm has center O.

Perpendicular distance of P from line AB = 6 cm.

∴ PY = 6 cm and ∠PYA = 90°   ......(i)

seg OX is a perpendicular from point O to seg PY at point X.

Line AB is a tangent to the circle at point Z.

∴ OZ is radius of the circle.

In ▢XOZY,

∠X = 90°    ......[∵ OX ⊥ PY]

∠Y = 90°   ......[From (i)]

∠Z = 90°    ......[Tangent theorem]

∴ ∠O = 90°   ......[Remaning angle of ▢XOZY]

∴ ▢XOZY is a rectangle.

. ∴ seg ZY = seg OX and seg XO = seg YZ   ......[Opposite sides of a rectangle]

But OZ = 4 cm     ......…[Radius of the circle]

∴ XY = 4 cm

Steps of construction:

  1. Draw line AB.
  2. From any point Q on line AB, draw a, perpendicular PQ of 6 cm.
  3. Draw point X on seg PQ such that seg XQ = 4 cm.
  4. Draw ray XY interior of the circle such that ∠QXY = 90°
  5. Taking distance of 4 cm in compass, draw an arc with centre P on ray XY and name the point of intersection as O.
  6. With centre O, draw a circle of radius 4 cm. Which is the required circle.

items.title: Point P is midpoint of segment AB where A(– 4, 2) and B(6, 2), then the coordinates of P are ______ - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: Point P is midpoint of segment AB where A(– 4, 2) and B(6, 2), then the coordinates of P are ______
items.solution_html:
(1, 2)

A(x1, y1) = A( –4, 2), B(x2, y2) = B(6, 2)

Here, x1 = – 4, y1 = 2, x2 = 6, y2 = 2

∴ Co-ordinates of the midpoint of seg AB

= `((x_1 + x_2)/2, (y_1 + y_2)/2)`

= `((-4 + 6)/2, (2 + 2)/2)`

= `(2/2, 4/2)`

= (1, 2)

items.title: ΔPQR ~ ΔABC. In ΔPQR, PQ = 3.6cm, QR = 4 cm, PR = 4.2 cm. Ratio of the corresponding sides of triangle is 3 : 4, then construct ΔPQR and ΔABC - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: ΔPQR ~ ΔABC. In ΔPQR, PQ = 3.6cm, QR = 4 cm, PR = 4.2 cm. Ratio of the corresponding sides of triangle is 3 : 4, then construct ΔPQR and ΔABC
items.solution_text: Analysis: ∆PQR ∼ ∆ABC ∴ `"PQ"/"AB" = "QR"/"BC" = "PR"/"AC"` .....[Corresponding sides of similar triangles] ∴ `3.6/"AB" = 4/"BC" =4.2/"AC" = 3/4` ......[Given] ∴ `3.6/"AB" = 3/4` ∴ AB = `(3.6 xx 4)/3` ∴ AB = 1.2 × 4 ∴ AB = 4.8 cm `4/"BC" = 3/4` ∴ BC = `(4 xx 4)/3` ∴ BC = `16/3` ∴ BC = 5.3 cm (approx) `4.2/"AC" = 3/4` ∴ AC = `(4.2 xx 4)/3` ∴ AC = 1.4 × 4 ∴ AC = 5.6 cm Steps of Construction: ∆PQR ∆ABC i. Draw seg QR of 4 cm Draw seg BC of 5.3 cm ii. Taking 3.6 cm and 4.2 cm distances on compass draw two arcs from Q and R respectively Taking 4.8 cm and 5.6 cm distance on compass draw two arcs from point B and C respectively. iii. Name the point of intersection as P. Name the point of intersection as A.
items.solution_html:
Analysis: ∆PQR ∼ ∆ABC

∴ `"PQ"/"AB" = "QR"/"BC" = "PR"/"AC"`  .....[Corresponding sides of similar triangles]

∴ `3.6/"AB" = 4/"BC" =4.2/"AC" = 3/4`   ......[Given]

∴ `3.6/"AB" = 3/4`

∴ AB = `(3.6 xx 4)/3`

∴ AB = 1.2 × 4

∴ AB = 4.8 cm 

`4/"BC" = 3/4`

∴ BC = `(4 xx 4)/3`

∴ BC = `16/3`

∴ BC = 5.3 cm (approx)

`4.2/"AC" = 3/4`

∴ AC = `(4.2 xx 4)/3`

∴ AC = 1.4 × 4

∴ AC = 5.6 cm


Steps of Construction:

  ∆PQR ∆ABC
i. Draw seg QR of 4 cm Draw seg BC of 5.3 cm
ii. Taking 3.6 cm and 4.2 cm distances on compass draw two arcs from Q and R respectively Taking 4.8 cm and 5.6 cm distance on compass draw two arcs from point B and C respectively.
iii. Name the point of intersection as P. Name the point of intersection as A.

items.title: Prove that (1 – cos2A) . sec2B + tan2B(1 – sin2A) = sin2A + tan2B - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: Prove that (1 – cos 2 A) . sec 2 B + tan 2 B(1 – sin 2 A) = sin 2 A + tan 2 B
items.solution_text: L.H.S = (1 – cos 2 A) . sec 2 B + tan 2 B(1 – sin 2 A) = `sin^2"A"* 1/(cos^2"B") + (sin^2"B")/(cos^2"B") (1 - sin^2"A")` ......`[(because sin^2"A" + cos^2"A" = 1),(therefore 1 - cos^2"A" = sin^2"A")]` = `(sin^2"A")/(cos^2"B") + (sin^2"B")/(cos^2"B") - (sin^2"A"sin^2"B")/(cos^2"B")` = `(sin^2"A")/(cos^2"B") - (sin^2"A"sin^2"B")/(cos^2"B") + (sin^2"B")/(cos^2"B")` = `(sin^2"A")/(cos^2"B") (1 - sin^2"B") + tan^2"B"` = `(sin^2"A")/(cos^2"B") (cos^2"B") + tan^2"B"` = sin 2 A + tan 2 B = R.H.S ∴ (1 – cos 2 A) . sec 2 B + tan 2 B (1 – sin 2 A) = sin 2 A + tan 2 B
items.solution_html:
L.H.S = (1 – cos2A) . sec2B + tan2B(1 – sin2A)

= `sin^2"A"* 1/(cos^2"B") + (sin^2"B")/(cos^2"B") (1 - sin^2"A")`     ......`[(because sin^2"A" + cos^2"A" = 1),(therefore 1 - cos^2"A" = sin^2"A")]`

= `(sin^2"A")/(cos^2"B") + (sin^2"B")/(cos^2"B") - (sin^2"A"sin^2"B")/(cos^2"B")`

= `(sin^2"A")/(cos^2"B") - (sin^2"A"sin^2"B")/(cos^2"B") + (sin^2"B")/(cos^2"B")`

= `(sin^2"A")/(cos^2"B") (1 - sin^2"B") + tan^2"B"`

= `(sin^2"A")/(cos^2"B") (cos^2"B") + tan^2"B"`

= sin2A + tan2B

= R.H.S

∴ (1 – cos2A) . sec2B + tan2B (1 – sin2A) = sin2A + tan2B

items.title: Prove that 1+cosA1-cosA = cosec A + cot A - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: Prove that `sqrt((1 + cos "A")/(1 - cos"A"))` = cosec A + cot A
items.solution_text: L.H.S = `sqrt((1 + cos "A")/(1 - cos"A"))` = `sqrt((1 + cos "A")/(1 - cos "A") xx (1 + cos "A")/(1 + cos "A"))` ......[On rationalising the denominator] = `sqrt((1 + cos "A")^2/(1 - cos^2 "A"))` = `sqrt((1 + cos "A")^2/(sin^2 "A")` ......`[(because sin^2"A" + cos^2"A" = 1),(therefore 1 - cos^2"A" = sin^2"A")]` = `(1 + cos"A")/"sin A"` = `1/"sin A" + "cos A"/"sin A"` = cosec A + cot A = R.H.S ∴ `sqrt((1 + cos "A")/(1 - cos"A"))` = cosec A + cot A
items.solution_html:
L.H.S = `sqrt((1 + cos "A")/(1 - cos"A"))`

= `sqrt((1 + cos "A")/(1 - cos "A") xx (1 + cos "A")/(1 + cos "A"))`   ......[On rationalising the denominator]

= `sqrt((1 + cos "A")^2/(1 - cos^2 "A"))`

= `sqrt((1 + cos "A")^2/(sin^2 "A")`    ......`[(because sin^2"A" + cos^2"A" = 1),(therefore 1 - cos^2"A" = sin^2"A")]`

= `(1 + cos"A")/"sin A"`

= `1/"sin A" + "cos A"/"sin A"`

= cosec A + cot A

= R.H.S

∴ `sqrt((1 + cos "A")/(1 - cos"A"))` = cosec A + cot A

items.title: Prove that 1+secAsec A=sin2A1-cosA - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: Prove that `(1 + sec "A")/"sec A" = (sin^2"A")/(1 - cos"A")`
items.solution_text: L.H.S = `(1 + sec "A")/"sec A"` = `1/"sec A" + "sec A"/"sec A"` = cos A + 1 = `(1 + cos "A") xx (1 - cos"A")/(1 - cos"A")` = `(1 - cos^2"A")/(1 - cos"A")` = `(sin^2"A")/(1 - cos"A")` .......`[(because sin^2"A" + cos^2"A" = 1),(therefore 1 - cos^2"A" = sin^2"A")]` = R.H.S ∴ `(1 + sec "A")/"sec A" = (sin^2"A")/(1 - cos"A")`
items.solution_html:
L.H.S = `(1 + sec "A")/"sec A"`

= `1/"sec A" + "sec A"/"sec A"`

= cos A + 1

= `(1 + cos "A") xx (1 -  cos"A")/(1 - cos"A")`

= `(1 - cos^2"A")/(1 - cos"A")`

= `(sin^2"A")/(1 - cos"A")`     .......`[(because sin^2"A" + cos^2"A" = 1),(therefore 1 - cos^2"A" = sin^2"A")]`

= R.H.S

∴ `(1 + sec "A")/"sec A" = (sin^2"A")/(1 - cos"A")`

items.title: Prove that 1+sinθ1-sinθ = (sec θ + tan θ)2 - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: Prove that `(1 + sintheta)/(1 - sin theta)` = (sec θ + tan θ) 2
items.solution_text: L.H.S = `(1 + sintheta)/(1 - sin theta)` = `((1 + sintheta)/(costheta))/((1 - sintheta)/(costheta))` ......[Dividing numerator and denominator by cos θ] = `(1/costheta + (sintheta)/(costheta))/(1/costheta - (sintheta)/(costheta)` = `(sectheta + tantheta)/(sectheta - tantheta)` = `(sectheta + tantheta)/(sectheta - tantheta) xx (sectheta + tantheta)/(sectheta + tantheta)` ......[On rationalising the denominator] = `(sectheta + tantheta)^2/(sec^2theta - tan^2theta)` = `(sectheta + tantheta)^2/1` ......`[(because 1 + tan^2theta = sec^2theta),(therefore sec^2theta - tan^2theta = 1)]` = (sec θ + tan θ) 2 = R.H.S ∴ `(1 + sintheta)/(1 - sin theta)` = (sec θ + tan θ) 2
items.solution_html:
L.H.S = `(1 + sintheta)/(1 - sin theta)`

= `((1 + sintheta)/(costheta))/((1 - sintheta)/(costheta))`  ......[Dividing numerator and denominator by cos θ]

= `(1/costheta + (sintheta)/(costheta))/(1/costheta - (sintheta)/(costheta)`

= `(sectheta + tantheta)/(sectheta - tantheta)`

= `(sectheta + tantheta)/(sectheta - tantheta) xx (sectheta + tantheta)/(sectheta + tantheta)` ......[On rationalising the denominator]

= `(sectheta + tantheta)^2/(sec^2theta - tan^2theta)`

= `(sectheta + tantheta)^2/1`   ......`[(because 1 + tan^2theta = sec^2theta),(therefore sec^2theta - tan^2theta = 1)]`

= (sec θ + tan θ)2

= R.H.S

∴ `(1 + sintheta)/(1 - sin theta)` = (sec θ + tan θ)2

items.title: Prove that 1+sinBcos B+cos B1+sinB = 2 sec B - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: Prove that `(1 + sin "B")/"cos B" + "cos B"/(1 + sin "B")` = 2 sec B
items.solution_html:
L.H.S = `(1 + sin "B")/"cos B" + "cos B"/(1 + sin "B")`

= `((1 +sin "B")^2 + cos^2"B")/(cos "B"(1 + sin "B"))`

= `(1 +2sin"B" + sin^2"B" + cos^2"B")/(cos"B"(1 + sin"B"))`    ......[∵ (a + b)2 = a2 + 2ab + b2]

= `(1 + 2sin"B" + 1)/(cos"B"(1+ sin"B"))`   .....[∵ sin2B + cos2B = 1]

= `(2 + 2sin"B")/(cos"B"(1 + sin"B"))`

= `(2(1 + sin"B"))/(cos"B"(1 + sin"B"))`

= `2/"cos B"`

= 2 sec B

= R.H.S

∴ `(1 + sin "B")/"cos B" + "cos B"/(1 + sin "B")` = 2 sec B

items.title: Prove that 1cosec θ-cotθ = cosec θ + cot θ - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: Prove that `1/("cosec" theta - cot theta)` = cosec θ + cot θ
items.solution_text: L.H.S = `1/("cosec" theta - cot theta)` = `1/("cosec" theta - cot theta) xx ("cosec"theta + cottheta)/("cosec"theta + cottheta)` ......[On rationalising the denominator] = `("cosec"theta + cottheta)/("cosec"^2theta - cot^2theta)` ......[∵ (a – b)(a + b) = a 2 – b 2 ] = `("cosec"theta cottheta)/1` ......`[(∵ 1 + cot^2θ = "cosec"^2θ),(∴ "cosec"^2θ - cot^2θ = 1)]` = cosecθ + cotθ = R.H.S ∴ `1/("cosec" theta - cot theta)` = cosec θ + cot θ
items.solution_html:
L.H.S = `1/("cosec"  theta - cot theta)`

= `1/("cosec"  theta - cot theta) xx ("cosec"theta + cottheta)/("cosec"theta + cottheta)`    ......[On rationalising the denominator]

= `("cosec"theta + cottheta)/("cosec"^2theta - cot^2theta)`   ......[∵ (a – b)(a + b) = a2 – b2]

= `("cosec"theta  cottheta)/1`    ......`[(∵ 1 + cot^2θ = "cosec"^2θ),(∴ "cosec"^2θ - cot^2θ = 1)]`

= cosecθ + cotθ

= R.H.S

∴ `1/("cosec"  theta - cot theta)` = cosec θ + cot θ

items.title: Prove that 2(sin6A + cos6A) – 3(sin4A + cos4A) + 1 = 0 - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: Prove that 2(sin 6 A + cos 6 A) – 3(sin 4 A + cos 4 A) + 1 = 0
items.solution_text: sin 6 A + cos 6 A = (sin 2 A) 3 + (cos 2 A) 3 = (1 – cos 2 A) 3 + (cos 2 A) 3 ......`[(because sin^2"A" + cos^2"A" = 1),(therefore 1 - cos^2"A" = sin^2"A")]` = 1 – 3 cos 2 A + 3(cos 2 A) 2 – (cos 2 A) 3 + cos 6 A ......[∵ (a – b) 3 = a 3 – 3a 2 b + 3ab 2 – b 3 ] = 1 – 3 cos 2 A(1 – cos 2 A) – cos 6 A + cos 6 A = 1 – 3 cos 2 A sin 2 A sin 4 A + cos 4 A = (sin 2 A) 2 + (cos 2 A) 2 = (1 – cos 2 A) 2 + (cos 2 A) 2 = 1 – 2 cos 2 A + (cos 2 A) 2 + (cos 2 A) 2 ......[∵ (a – b) 2 = a 2 – 2ab + b 2 ] = 1 – 2 cos 2 A + 2 cos 4 A = 1 – 2 cos 2 A(1 – cos 2 A) = 1 – 2 cos 2 A sin 2 A L.H.S = 2(sin 6 A + cos 6 A) – 3(sin 4 A + cos 4 A) + 1 = 2(1 – 3 cos 2 A sin 2 A) – 3(1 – 2 cos 2 A sin 2 A) + 1 = 2 – 6 cos 2 A sin 2 A – 3 + 6 cos 2 A sin 2 A + 1 = 0 = R.H.S ∴ 2(sin 6 A + cos 6 A) – 3(sin 4 A + cos 4 A) + 1 = 0
items.solution_html:
sin6A + cos6A = (sin2A)3 + (cos2A)3

 = (1 – cos2A)3 + (cos2A)3   ......`[(because sin^2"A" + cos^2"A" = 1),(therefore 1 - cos^2"A" = sin^2"A")]`

= 1 – 3 cos2A + 3(cos2A)2 – (cos2A)3 + cos6A   ......[∵ (a – b)3 = a3 – 3a2b + 3ab2 – b3]

= 1 – 3 cos2A(1 – cos2A) – cos6A + cos6A

= 1 – 3 cos2A sin2A

sin4A + cos4A = (sin2A)2 + (cos2A)2

= (1 – cos2A)2 + (cos2A)2

= 1 – 2 cos2A + (cos2A)2 + (cos2A)2   ......[∵ (a – b)2 = a2 – 2ab + b2]

= 1 – 2 cos2A + 2 cos4A

= 1 – 2 cos2A(1 – cos2A)

= 1 – 2 cos2A sin2A

L.H.S = 2(sin6A + cos6A) – 3(sin4A + cos4A) + 1

= 2(1 – 3 cos2A sin2A) – 3(1 – 2 cos2A sin2A) + 1

= 2 – 6 cos2A sin2A – 3 + 6 cos2A sin2A + 1

= 0

= R.H.S

∴ 2(sin6A + cos6A) – 3(sin4A + cos4A) + 1 = 0

items.title: Prove that angles inscribed in the same arc are congruent.Given: In a circle with center C, ∠PQR and ∠PSR are inscribed in same arc PQR. Arc PTR is intercepted by the angles. To prove: ∠PQR ≅ ∠PSR. P - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: Prove that angles inscribed in the same arc are congruent. Given: In a circle with center C, ∠PQR and ∠PSR are inscribed in same arc PQR. Arc PTR is intercepted by the angles. To prove: ∠PQR ≅ ∠PSR. Proof: m∠PQR = `1/2 xx ["m"("arc PTR")]` ......(i) `square` m∠`square` = `1/2 xx ["m"("arc PTR")]` ......(ii) `square` m∠`square` = m∠PSR .....[By (i) and (ii)] ∴ ∠PQR ≅ ∠PSR
items.solution_text: Proof: m∠PQR = `1/2 xx ["m"("arc PTR")]` ......(i) [Inscribed angle theorem] m∠ PSR = `1/2 xx ["m"("arc PTR")]` ......(ii) [Inscribed angle theorem] m∠ PQR = m∠PSR .....[By (i) and (ii)] ∴ ∠PQR ≅ ∠PSR
items.solution_html:
Proof:

m∠PQR = `1/2 xx ["m"("arc PTR")]`   ......(i) [Inscribed angle theorem]

m∠PSR = `1/2 xx ["m"("arc PTR")]`   ......(ii) [Inscribed angle theorem]

m∠PQR = m∠PSR      .....[By (i) and (ii)]

∴ ∠PQR ≅ ∠PSR

items.title: Prove that, any rectangle is a cyclic quadrilateral - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: Prove that, any rectangle is a cyclic quadrilateral
items.solution_text: Given: ▢ABCD is a rectangle. To prove: ▢ABCD is a cyclic quadrilateral Proof: ▢ABCD is a rectangle. ....[Given] ∴ ∠A = ∠B = ∠C = ∠D = 90° ....[Angles of a rectangle] Now, ∠A + ∠C = 90° + 90° ∴ ∠A + ∠C = 180° We know, if a pair of opposite angles of a quadrilateral is supplementary, then the quadrilateral is cyclic. ∴ ▢ABCD is a cyclic quadrilateral ......[Converse of cyclic quadrilateral theorem] So, any rectangle is a cyclic quadrilateral.
items.solution_html:
Given: ▢ABCD is a rectangle.

To prove: ▢ABCD is a cyclic quadrilateral

Proof:

▢ABCD is a rectangle.                ....[Given]

∴ ∠A = ∠B = ∠C = ∠D = 90°     ....[Angles of a rectangle]

Now, ∠A + ∠C = 90° + 90° 

∴ ∠A + ∠C = 180°

We know, if a pair of opposite angles of a quadrilateral is supplementary, then the quadrilateral is cyclic.

∴ ▢ABCD is a cyclic quadrilateral   ......[Converse of cyclic quadrilateral theorem]

So, any rectangle is a cyclic quadrilateral.
