items.title: Prove that cosθ1+sinθ=1-sinθcosθ - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: Prove that `costheta/(1 + sintheta) = (1 - sintheta)/(costheta)`
items.solution_text: L.H.S = `costheta/(1 + sintheta)` = `costheta/(1 + sintheta) xx (1 - sintheta)/(1 - sintheta)` ......[On rationalising the denominator] = `(costheta(1 - sintheta))/(1 - sin^2theta)` = `(costheta(1 - sintheta))/(cos^2theta)` ......`[(because sin^2theta +cos^2theta = 1),(therefore 1 -sin^2theta = cos^2theta)]` = `(1 - sintheta)/costheta` = R.H.S ∴ `costheta/(1 + sintheta) = (1 - sintheta)/(costheta)`
items.solution_html:
L.H.S = `costheta/(1 + sintheta)`

= `costheta/(1 + sintheta) xx (1 - sintheta)/(1 -  sintheta)`   ......[On rationalising the denominator]

= `(costheta(1 - sintheta))/(1 - sin^2theta)`

= `(costheta(1 - sintheta))/(cos^2theta)`    ......`[(because sin^2theta +cos^2theta = 1),(therefore 1 -sin^2theta = cos^2theta)]`

= `(1 - sintheta)/costheta`

= R.H.S

∴ `costheta/(1 + sintheta) = (1 - sintheta)/(costheta)`

items.title: Prove that cos(90-A)sinA=sin(90-A)cosA - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: Prove that `(cos(90 - "A"))/(sin "A") = (sin(90 - "A"))/(cos "A")`
items.solution_text: L.H.S = `(cos(90 - "A"))/(sin "A")` = `"sin A"/"sin A"` = 1 R.H.S = `(sin(90 - "A"))/(cos "A")` = `"cos A"/"cos A"` = 1 ∴ L.H.S = R.H.S
items.solution_html:
L.H.S = `(cos(90 - "A"))/(sin "A")`

= `"sin A"/"sin A"`

= 1

R.H.S = `(sin(90 - "A"))/(cos "A")`

= `"cos A"/"cos A"`

= 1

∴ L.H.S = R.H.S

items.title: Prove that cos2θ . (1 + tan2θ) = 1. Complete the activity given below. Activity: L.H.S = □ = cos2θ×□ .....[1+tan2θ=□] = (cosθ×□)2 = 12 = 1 = R.H.S - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: Prove that cos 2 θ . (1 + tan 2 θ) = 1. Complete the activity given below. Activity: L.H.S = `square` = `cos^2theta xx square .....[1 + tan^2theta = square]` = `(cos theta xx square)^2` = 1 2 = 1 = R.H.S
items.solution_text: L.H.S. = `cos^2theta*(1 + tan^2theta)` = `cos^2theta xx sec^2theta` .....`[1 + tan^2theta = sec^2theta]` = `(cos theta xx sectheta)^2` = 1 2 = 1 = R.H.S
items.solution_html:
L.H.S. = `cos^2theta*(1 + tan^2theta)`

= `cos^2theta xx sec^2theta`    .....`[1 + tan^2theta = sec^2theta]`

= `(cos theta xx sectheta)^2`

= 12

= 1

= R.H.S

items.title: Prove that cos2θsinθ+sinθ = cosec θ - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: Prove that `(cos^2theta)/(sintheta) + sintheta` = cosec θ
items.solution_text: L.H.S = `(cos^2theta)/(sintheta) + sintheta` = `(cos^2theta + sin^2theta)/sintheta` = `1/sintheta` .......[∵ sin 2 θ + cos 2 θ = 1] = cosec θ = R.H.S ∴ `(cos^2theta)/(sintheta) + sintheta` = cosec θ
items.solution_html:
L.H.S = `(cos^2theta)/(sintheta) + sintheta` 

= `(cos^2theta + sin^2theta)/sintheta`

= `1/sintheta`   .......[∵ sin2θ + cos2θ = 1]

= cosec θ

= R.H.S

∴ `(cos^2theta)/(sintheta) + sintheta` = cosec θ

items.title: Prove that θcosec θ×1-cos2θ = 1 - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: Prove that `"cosec" θ xx sqrt(1 - cos^2theta)` = 1
items.solution_text: L.H.S = `"cosec" θ xx sqrt(1 - cos^2theta)` = `"cosec" θ xx sqrt(sin^2theta)` ......`[(because sin^2theta + cos^2theta = 1),(therefore 1 - cos^2theta = sin^2theta)]` = cosec θ × sin θ = 1 ......[∵ sin θ × cosec θ = 1] = R.H.S
items.solution_html:
L.H.S = `"cosec"  θ xx sqrt(1 - cos^2theta)`

= `"cosec"  θ xx sqrt(sin^2theta)`   ......`[(because sin^2theta + cos^2theta = 1),(therefore 1 - cos^2theta = sin^2theta)]`

= cosec θ × sin θ

= 1     ......[∵ sin θ × cosec θ = 1]

= R.H.S

items.title: Prove that cosec θ – cot θ = sinθ1+cosθ - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: Prove that cosec θ – cot θ = `sin theta/(1 + cos theta)`
items.solution_text: L.H.S = cosec θ – cot θ = `1/sintheta - costheta/sintheta` = `(1 -costheta)/sintheta` = `(1 - costheta)/sintheta xx (1 + costheta)/(1 +costheta)` .....[On rationalising the numerator] = `(1 - cos^2theta)/(sintheta(1 +costheta))` = `(sin^2theta)/(sintheta(1 + costheta))` .....`[(because sin^2theta + cos^2theta = 1),(therefore 1 - cos^2theta = sin^2theta)]` = `sintheta/(1 + costheta)` = R.H.S ∴ cosec θ – cot θ = `sin theta/(1 + cos theta)`
items.solution_html:
L.H.S = cosec θ – cot θ

= `1/sintheta - costheta/sintheta`

= `(1 -costheta)/sintheta`

= `(1 - costheta)/sintheta xx (1 + costheta)/(1 +costheta)`    .....[On rationalising the numerator]

= `(1 - cos^2theta)/(sintheta(1 +costheta))`

= `(sin^2theta)/(sintheta(1 + costheta))`   .....`[(because sin^2theta + cos^2theta = 1),(therefore 1 - cos^2theta = sin^2theta)]`

= `sintheta/(1 + costheta)`

= R.H.S

∴ cosec θ – cot θ = `sin theta/(1 + cos theta)`

items.title: Prove that cot A1-cotA+tan A1-tanA = – 1 - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: Prove that `"cot A"/(1 - cot"A") + "tan A"/(1 - tan "A")` = – 1
items.solution_html:
L.H.S = `"cot A"/(1 - cot"A") + "tan A"/(1 - tan "A")`

= `"cot A"/(1 - 1/(tan"A")) + "tan A"/(1 - tan "A")`

= `"cot A"/((tan "A" - 1)/(tan "A")) + "tan A"/(1 - tan "A")`

= `"cot A tan A"/(tan "A" - 1) + "tan A"/(1 - tan "A")`

= `1/(tan "A" - 1) + "tan A"/(1 - tan "A")`   ......[∵ cot A tan A = 1]

= `- 1/(1 - tan "A") + "tan A"/(1 - tan "A")`

= `- (1/(1 -tan "A") - "tan A"/(1- tan "A"))`

= `-((1 - tan "A")/(1 - tan "A"))`

= – 1

= R.H.S

∴ `"cot A"/(1 - cot"A") + "tan A"/(1 - tan "A")` = – 1

items.title: Prove that cot A1-tanA+tan A1-cotA = 1 + tan A + cot A = sec A . cosec A + 1 - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: Prove that `"cot A"/(1 - tan "A") + "tan A"/(1 - cot"A")` = 1 + tan A + cot A = sec A . cosec A + 1
items.solution_text: `"cot A"/(1 - tan "A") + "tan A"/(1 - cot"A")` = `((cos "A")/(sin "A"))/(1 - (sin "A")/(cos "A")) + ((sin "A")/(cos "A"))/(1 - (cos "A")/(sin "A"))` = `((cos "A")/(sin "A"))/((cos "A" - sin "A")/(cos "A")) + ((sin "A")/(cos "A"))/((sin "A" - cos "A")/(sin "A"))` = `"cos A"/"sin A" xx "cos A"/(cos "A" - sin "A") + "sin A"/"cos A" xx "sin A"/(sin "A" - cos "A")` = `(cos^2"A")/(sin "A"(cos "A" - sin "A")) + (sin^2"A")/(cos"A"(sin"A" - cos"A"))` = `1/(sin "A" - cos "A") ((-cos^3"A" + sin^3"A")/(sin"A" cos"A"))` = `1/(sin"A" - cos"A")((sin^3"A" - cos^3"A")/(sin"A" cos"A"))` = `1/(sin"A" - cos"A")xx ((sin"A" - cos"A")(sin^2"A" + sin"A" cos"A" + cos^2"A"))/(sin"A" cos"A")` ......[∵ a 3 – b 3 = (a – b)(a 2 + ab + b 2 )] = `(sin^2"A" +sin"A" cos"A" + cos^2"A")/(sin"A" cos"A"` ......(i) = `(1 + sin"A" cos"A")/(sin"A" cos"A")` .....[∵ sin 2 A + cos 2 A = 1] = `1/(sin"A" cos"A") + (sin"A" cos"A")/(sin"A" cos"A")` = cosec A sec A + 1 .....(ii) `"cot A"/(1 - tan "A") + "tan A"/(1 - cot "A")` = `(sin^2"A" + sin"A" cos"A" + cos^2"A")/(sin"A" cos"A")` ......[From (i)] = `(sin^2"A")/(sin"A" cos"A") + "sin A cos A"/"sin A cos A" + (cos^2"A")/"sin A cos A"` = `"sin A"/"cos A" + 1 + "cos A"/"sin A"` = tan A + 1 + cot A ......(iii) From (ii) and (iii), we get `"cot A"/(1 - tan "A") + "tan A"/(1 - cot "A")` = 1 + tan A + cot A = sec A . cosec A + 1
items.solution_html:
`"cot A"/(1 - tan "A") + "tan A"/(1 - cot"A")`

= `((cos "A")/(sin "A"))/(1 - (sin "A")/(cos "A")) + ((sin "A")/(cos "A"))/(1 - (cos "A")/(sin "A"))`

= `((cos "A")/(sin "A"))/((cos "A" -  sin "A")/(cos "A")) + ((sin "A")/(cos "A"))/((sin "A" -  cos "A")/(sin "A"))`

= `"cos A"/"sin A" xx "cos A"/(cos "A" - sin "A") + "sin A"/"cos A" xx "sin A"/(sin "A" - cos "A")`

= `(cos^2"A")/(sin "A"(cos "A" - sin "A")) + (sin^2"A")/(cos"A"(sin"A" - cos"A"))`

= `1/(sin "A" - cos "A") ((-cos^3"A" + sin^3"A")/(sin"A" cos"A"))`

= `1/(sin"A" - cos"A")((sin^3"A" - cos^3"A")/(sin"A" cos"A"))`

= `1/(sin"A" - cos"A")xx ((sin"A" - cos"A")(sin^2"A" + sin"A" cos"A" + cos^2"A"))/(sin"A" cos"A")`  ......[∵ a3 – b3 = (a – b)(a2 + ab + b2)]

= `(sin^2"A" +sin"A" cos"A" + cos^2"A")/(sin"A" cos"A"`  ......(i)

= `(1 + sin"A" cos"A")/(sin"A" cos"A")`   .....[∵ sin2A + cos2A = 1]

= `1/(sin"A" cos"A") + (sin"A" cos"A")/(sin"A" cos"A")`

= cosec A sec A + 1  .....(ii)

`"cot A"/(1 - tan "A") + "tan A"/(1 - cot "A")`

= `(sin^2"A" + sin"A" cos"A" + cos^2"A")/(sin"A" cos"A")`     ......[From (i)]

= `(sin^2"A")/(sin"A" cos"A") + "sin A cos A"/"sin A cos A" + (cos^2"A")/"sin A cos A"`

= `"sin A"/"cos A" + 1 + "cos A"/"sin A"`

= tan A + 1 + cot A    ......(iii)

From (ii) and (iii), we get

`"cot  A"/(1  - tan "A") + "tan A"/(1 -  cot "A")` = 1 + tan A + cot A = sec A . cosec A + 1

items.title: Prove that cot2θ × sec2θ = cot2θ + 1 - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: Prove that cot 2 θ × sec 2 θ = cot 2 θ + 1
items.solution_text: L.H.S = cot 2 θ × sec 2 θ = `(cos^2theta)/(sin^2theta) xx 1/(cos^2theta)` = `1/(sin^2theta)` = cosec 2 θ = 1 + cot 2 θ ......[∵ 1 + cot 2 θ = cosec 2 θ] = R.H.S ∴ cot 2 θ × sec 2 θ = cot 2 θ + 1
items.solution_html:
L.H.S = cot2θ × sec2θ

= `(cos^2theta)/(sin^2theta) xx 1/(cos^2theta)`

= `1/(sin^2theta)`

= cosec2θ

= 1 + cot2θ    ......[∵ 1 + cot2θ = cosec2θ]

= R.H.S

∴ cot2θ × sec2θ = cot2θ + 1

items.title: Prove that cot2θ – tan2θ = cosec2θ – sec2θ - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: Prove that cot 2 θ – tan 2 θ = cosec 2 θ – sec 2 θ
items.solution_text: L.H.S = cot 2 θ – tan 2 θ = (cosec 2 θ − 1) − (sec 2 θ − 1) ......`[(because tan^2theta = sec^2theta - 1),(cot^2theta = "cosec"^2 theta - 1)]` = cosec 2 θ − 1 − sec 2 θ + 1 = cosec 2 θ − sec 2 θ = R.H.S ∴ cot 2 θ – tan 2 θ = cosec 2 θ – sec 2 θ
items.solution_html:
L.H.S = cot2θ – tan2θ

= (cosec2θ − 1) − (sec2θ − 1)    ......`[(because tan^2theta = sec^2theta - 1),(cot^2theta = "cosec"^2 theta - 1)]`

= cosec2θ − 1 − sec2θ + 1

= cosec2θ − sec2θ

= R.H.S

∴ cot2θ – tan2θ = cosec2θ – sec2θ

items.title: Prove that cotA+cosec A-1cotA-cosec A+1=1+cosAsin A - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: Prove that `(cot "A" + "cosec A" - 1)/(cot"A" - "cosec A" + 1) = (1 + cos "A")/"sin A"`
items.solution_text: L.H.S = `(cot "A" + "cosec A" - 1)/(cot"A" - "cosec A" + 1)` = `(cot"A" + "cosec A" - ("cosec"^2"A" - cot^2"A"))/(cot"A" - "cosec A" + 1)` .....`[(because 1 + cot^2"A" = "cosec"^2"A"),(therefore "cosec"^2"A" - cot^2"A" = 1)]` = `(cot"A" + "cosec A" - ("cosec A" + cot"A")("cosec A" - cot"A"))/(cot"A" - "cosec A" + 1)` .....[∵ a 2 – b 2 = (a + b) (a – b)] = `((cot"A" + "cosec A")(1 - "cosec A" + cot "A"))/(cot"A" - "cosec A" + 1)` = cot A + cosec A = `"cos A"/"sin A" + 1/"sin A"` = `(cos "A" + 1)/"sin A"` = R.H.S ∴ `(cot "A" + "cosec A" - 1)/(cot"A" - "cosec A" + 1) = (1 + cos "A")/"sin A"`
items.solution_html:
L.H.S = `(cot "A" + "cosec A" - 1)/(cot"A" - "cosec A" + 1)`

= `(cot"A" + "cosec A" - ("cosec"^2"A" - cot^2"A"))/(cot"A" - "cosec A" + 1)`   .....`[(because 1 + cot^2"A" = "cosec"^2"A"),(therefore "cosec"^2"A" - cot^2"A" = 1)]`

= `(cot"A" + "cosec A" - ("cosec A" + cot"A")("cosec A" - cot"A"))/(cot"A" - "cosec A" + 1)`   .....[∵ a2 – b2 = (a + b) (a – b)]

= `((cot"A" + "cosec A")(1 - "cosec A" + cot "A"))/(cot"A" - "cosec A" + 1)`

= cot A + cosec A

= `"cos A"/"sin A" + 1/"sin A"`

= `(cos "A" + 1)/"sin A"`

= R.H.S

∴ `(cot "A" + "cosec  A" - 1)/(cot"A" - "cosec  A" + 1) = (1 + cos "A")/"sin A"`

items.title: Prove that sec2θ − cos2θ = tan2θ + sin2θ - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: Prove that sec 2 θ − cos 2 θ = tan 2 θ + sin 2 θ
items.solution_text: L.H.S = sec 2 θ − cos 2 θ = 1 + tan 2 θ – cos 2 θ .......[∵ 1 + tan 2 θ = sec 2 θ] = tan 2 θ + (1 – cos 2 θ) = tan 2 θ + sin 2 θ ......`[(because sin^2theta +cos^2theta = 1),(therefore 1 - cos^2theta = sin^2theta)]` = R.H.S ∴ sec 2 θ − cos 2 θ = tan 2 θ + sin 2 θ
items.solution_html:
L.H.S = sec2θ − cos2θ

= 1 + tan2θ – cos2θ     .......[∵ 1 + tan2θ = sec2θ]

= tan2θ + (1 – cos2θ)

= tan2θ + sin2θ       ......`[(because sin^2theta +cos^2theta = 1),(therefore 1 - cos^2theta = sin^2theta)]`

= R.H.S

∴ sec2θ − cos2θ = tan2θ + sin2θ

items.title: Prove that sec2θ – cos2θ = tan2θ + sin2θ - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: Prove that sec 2 θ – cos 2 θ = tan 2 θ + sin 2 θ
items.solution_text: L.H.S = sec 2 θ – cos 2 θ = sec 2 θ – (1 – sin 2 θ) ......`[(because sin^2theta + cos^2theta = 1),(therefore 1 - sin^2theta = cos^2theta)]` = sec 2 θ – 1 + sin 2 θ = tan 2 θ + sin 2 θ ......`[(because 1 + tan^2theta = sec^2theta),(therefore tan^2theta = sec^2theta - 1)]` = R.H.S ∴ sec 2 θ – cos 2 θ = tan 2 θ + sin 2 θ
items.solution_html:
L.H.S = sec2θ – cos2θ

= sec2θ – (1 – sin2θ)    ......`[(because sin^2theta + cos^2theta = 1),(therefore 1 - sin^2theta = cos^2theta)]`

= sec2θ – 1 + sin2θ

= tan2θ + sin2θ     ......`[(because 1 + tan^2theta = sec^2theta),(therefore tan^2theta = sec^2theta - 1)]`

= R.H.S

∴ sec2θ – cos2θ = tan2θ + sin2θ

items.title: Prove that sec2θ + cosec2θ = sec2θ × cosec2θ - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: Prove that sec 2 θ + cosec 2 θ = sec 2 θ × cosec 2 θ
items.solution_text: L.H.S = sec 2 θ + cosec 2 θ = `1/(cos^2theta) + 1/(sin^2theta)` = `(sin^2theta + cos^2theta)/(cos^2theta*sin^2theta)` = `1/(cos^2theta*sin^2theta)` ......[∵ sin 2 θ + cos 2 θ = 1] = `1/(cos^2theta) xx 1/(sin^2theta)` = sec 2 θ × cosec 2 θ = R.H.S ∴ sec 2 θ + cosec 2 θ = sec 2 θ × cosec 2 θ
items.solution_html:
L.H.S = sec2θ + cosec2θ

= `1/(cos^2theta) + 1/(sin^2theta)`

= `(sin^2theta + cos^2theta)/(cos^2theta*sin^2theta)`

= `1/(cos^2theta*sin^2theta)`   ......[∵ sin2θ + cos2θ = 1]

= `1/(cos^2theta) xx 1/(sin^2theta)`

= sec2θ × cosec2θ

= R.H.S

∴ sec2θ + cosec2θ = sec2θ × cosec2θ

items.title: Prove that sec2A – cosec2A = AAA2sin2A-1sin2A⋅cos2A - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: Prove that sec 2 A – cosec 2 A = `(2sin^2"A" - 1)/(sin^2"A"*cos^2"A")`
items.solution_text: L.H.S = sec 2 A – cosec 2 A = `1/(cos^2"A") - 1/(sin^2"A")` = `(sin^2"A" - cos^2"A")/(cos^2"A"*sin^2"A")` = `(sin^2"A" - (1 - sin^2"A"))/(sin^2"A"*cos^2"A")` .....`[(because sin^2"A" + cos^2"A" = 1),(therefore 1 - sin^2"A" = cos^2"A")]` = `(sin^2"A" - 1 + sin^2"A")/(sin^2"A"*cos^2"A")` = `(2sin^2"A" - 1)/(sin^2"A"*cos^2"A")` = R.H.S ∴ sec 2 A – cosec 2 A = `(2sin^2"A" - 1)/(sin^2"A"*cos^2"A")`
items.solution_html:
L.H.S = sec2A – cosec2A

= `1/(cos^2"A") - 1/(sin^2"A")`

= `(sin^2"A" - cos^2"A")/(cos^2"A"*sin^2"A")`

= `(sin^2"A" - (1 - sin^2"A"))/(sin^2"A"*cos^2"A")` .....`[(because sin^2"A" + cos^2"A" = 1),(therefore 1 - sin^2"A" = cos^2"A")]`

= `(sin^2"A" - 1 + sin^2"A")/(sin^2"A"*cos^2"A")`

= `(2sin^2"A" - 1)/(sin^2"A"*cos^2"A")`

= R.H.S

∴ sec2A – cosec2A = `(2sin^2"A" - 1)/(sin^2"A"*cos^2"A")`

items.title: Prove that secAtanA+cotA = sin A - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: Prove that `sec"A"/(tan "A" + cot "A")` = sin A
items.solution_text: L.H.S = `sec"A"/(tan "A" + cot "A")` = `sec"A"/((sin"A")/(cos"A") + (cos"A")/(sin"A"))` = `sec"A"/((sin^2"A" + cos^2"A")/(cos"A" sin"A"))` = `sec"A"/(1/(cos"A" sin"A"))` ......[∵ sin 2 A + cos 2 A = 1] = sec A cos A sin A = `1/cos"A" xx cos "A" sin "A"` = sin A = R.H.S. ∴ `sec"A"/(tan "A" + cot "A")` = sin A
items.solution_html:
L.H.S = `sec"A"/(tan "A" + cot "A")`

= `sec"A"/((sin"A")/(cos"A") + (cos"A")/(sin"A"))`

= `sec"A"/((sin^2"A" + cos^2"A")/(cos"A" sin"A"))`

= `sec"A"/(1/(cos"A" sin"A"))`   ......[∵ sin2A + cos2A = 1]

= sec A cos A sin A

= `1/cos"A" xx cos "A" sin "A"`

= sin A

= R.H.S.

∴ `sec"A"/(tan "A" + cot "A")` = sin A

items.title: Prove that sin θ (1 – tan θ) – cos θ (1 – cot θ) = cosec θ – sec θ - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: Prove that sin θ (1 – tan θ) – cos θ (1 – cot θ) = cosec θ – sec θ
items.solution_text: L.H.S = sin θ (1 – tan θ) – cos θ (1 – cot θ) = `sintheta (1 - (sintheta)/(costheta)) - costheta (1 - (costheta)/(sintheta))` = `sintheta - (sin^2theta)/costheta - costheta + (cos^2theta)/sintheta` = `sintheta + (cos^2theta)/sintheta - (sin^2theta)/costheta - costheta` = `(sin^2theta + cos^2theta)/sintheta - ((sin^2theta + cos^2theta)/costheta)` = `1/sintheta - 1/costheta` ......[∵ sin 2 θ + cos 2 θ = 1] = cosec θ – sec θ = R.H.S ∴ sin θ (1 – tan θ) – cos θ (1 – cot θ) = cosec θ – sec θ
items.solution_html:
L.H.S = sin θ (1 – tan θ) – cos θ (1 – cot θ)

= `sintheta (1 - (sintheta)/(costheta)) - costheta (1 - (costheta)/(sintheta))`

= `sintheta - (sin^2theta)/costheta - costheta + (cos^2theta)/sintheta`

= `sintheta + (cos^2theta)/sintheta - (sin^2theta)/costheta - costheta`

= `(sin^2theta + cos^2theta)/sintheta - ((sin^2theta + cos^2theta)/costheta)`

= `1/sintheta - 1/costheta`   ......[∵ sin2θ + cos2θ = 1]

= cosec θ – sec θ

= R.H.S

∴ sin θ (1 – tan θ) – cos θ (1 – cot θ) = cosec θ – sec θ

items.title: Prove that sinθ+cosec θsinθ = 2 + cot2θ - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: Prove that `(sintheta + "cosec" theta)/sin theta` = 2 + cot 2 θ
items.solution_text: L.H.S = `(sintheta + "cosec" theta)/sin theta` = `sintheta/sintheta + ("cosec"theta)/sintheta` = 1 + cosec θ × cosec θ ......`[∵ "cosec" theta = 1/sin theta]` = 1 + cosec 2 θ = 1 + 1 + cot 2 θ .......[∵ 1 + cot 2 θ = cosec 2 θ] = 2 + cot 2 θ = R.H.S ∴ `(sintheta + "cosec" theta)/sin theta` = 2 + cot 2 θ
items.solution_html:
L.H.S = `(sintheta + "cosec"  theta)/sin theta`

= `sintheta/sintheta + ("cosec"theta)/sintheta`

= 1 + cosec θ × cosec θ   ......`[∵ "cosec"  theta = 1/sin theta]`

= 1 + cosec2θ

= 1 + 1 + cot2θ      .......[∵ 1 + cot2θ = cosec2θ]

= 2 + cot2θ

= R.H.S

∴ `(sintheta + "cosec"  theta)/sin theta` = 2 + cot2θ

items.title: Prove that sinθsecθ+1+sinθsecθ-1 = 2 cot θ - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: Prove that `sintheta/(sectheta+ 1) +sintheta/(sectheta - 1)` = 2 cot θ
items.solution_text: L.H.S = `sintheta/(sectheta+ 1) +sintheta/(sectheta - 1)` = `sintheta/(1/costheta + 1) + sintheta/(1/costheta - 1` = `sintheta/((1 + costheta)/costheta) + sintheta/((1 - costheta)/(costheta))` = `(sintheta costheta)/(1 + costheta) + (sintheta costheta)/(1 - costheta)` = `sin theta costheta (1 /(1 + costheta) + 1/(1 - costheta))` = `sintheta costheta [(1 - costheta + 1 + costheta)/((1 + costheta)(1 - costheta))]` = `sintheta costheta (2/(1 - cos^2theta))` ......[∵ (a + b)(a – b) = a 2 – b 2 ] = `sintheta costheta xx 2/(sin^2theta)` .....`[(because sin^2theta + cos^2theta = 1),(therefore 1 - cos^2theta = sin^2theta)]` = `2 xx (costheta)/(sintheta)` = 2cot θ = R.H.S ∴ `sintheta/(sectheta+ 1) +sintheta/(sectheta - 1)` = 2 cot θ
items.solution_html:
L.H.S = `sintheta/(sectheta+ 1) +sintheta/(sectheta - 1)` 

= `sintheta/(1/costheta + 1) + sintheta/(1/costheta - 1`

= `sintheta/((1 + costheta)/costheta) + sintheta/((1 - costheta)/(costheta))`

= `(sintheta costheta)/(1 + costheta) + (sintheta costheta)/(1 - costheta)`

= `sin theta costheta (1 /(1 + costheta) + 1/(1 -  costheta))`

= `sintheta costheta [(1 - costheta + 1 + costheta)/((1 + costheta)(1 - costheta))]`

= `sintheta costheta (2/(1 - cos^2theta))`   ......[∵ (a + b)(a – b) = a2 – b2]

= `sintheta costheta xx 2/(sin^2theta)`   .....`[(because sin^2theta + cos^2theta = 1),(therefore 1 - cos^2theta = sin^2theta)]`

= `2 xx (costheta)/(sintheta)`

= 2cot θ

= R.H.S

∴ `sintheta/(sectheta+ 1) +sintheta/(sectheta - 1)` = 2 cot θ

items.title: Prove that sinθ+tanθcothη = tan θ(1 + sec θ) - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: Prove that `(sintheta + tantheta)/cos theta` = tan θ(1 + sec θ)
items.solution_text: L.H.S = `(sintheta + tantheta)/cos theta` = `sintheta/costheta + tantheta/costheta` = tan θ + tan θ sec θ = tan θ(1 + sec θ) = R.H.S ∴ `(sintheta + tantheta)/cos theta` = tan θ(1 + sec θ)
items.solution_html:
L.H.S = `(sintheta + tantheta)/cos theta`

= `sintheta/costheta + tantheta/costheta`

= tan θ + tan θ sec θ

= tan θ(1 + sec θ)

= R.H.S

∴ `(sintheta + tantheta)/cos theta` = tan θ(1 + sec θ)

items.title: Prove that sin2θcosθ+cosθ = sec θ - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: Prove that `(sin^2theta)/(cos theta) + cos theta` = sec θ
items.solution_text: L.H.S = `(sin^2theta)/(cos theta) + cos theta` = `(sin^2theta + cos^2theta)/costheta` = `1/costheta` ......[∵ sin 2 θ + cos 2 θ = 1] = sec θ = R.H.S ∴ `(sin^2theta)/(cos theta) + cos theta` = sec θ
items.solution_html:
L.H.S = `(sin^2theta)/(cos theta) + cos theta`

= `(sin^2theta + cos^2theta)/costheta`

= `1/costheta`     ......[∵ sin2θ + cos2θ = 1]

= sec θ

= R.H.S

∴ `(sin^2theta)/(cos theta) + cos theta` = sec θ

items.title: Prove that sin2A . tan A + cos2A . cot A + 2 sin A . cos A = tan A + cot A - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: Prove that sin 2 A . tan A + cos 2 A . cot A + 2 sin A . cos A = tan A + cot A
items.solution_text: L.H.S = sin 2 A . tan A + cos 2 A . cot A + 2 sin A . cos A = `sin^2"A"* (sin "A")/(cos "A") + cos^2"A"* (cos"A")/(sin"A") + 2sin"A" *cos"A"` = `(sin^3"A")/"cosA" + (cos^3"A")/"sinA" + 2sin"A"*cos"A"` = `(sin^4"A" + cos^4"A" + 2sin^2"A"cos^2"A")/(sin"A"cos"A")` = `(sin^2"A" + cos^2"A")^2/(sin"A"cos"A")` .....[∵ a 2 + b 2 + 2ab = (a + b) 2 ] = `1^2/(sin"A"cos"A")` ......[∵ sin 2 A + cos 2 A = 1] = `1/(sin"A"cos"A")` = `(sin^2"A"+ cos^2"A")/(sin"A"cos"A")` ......[∵ 1 = sin 2 A + cos 2 A] = `(sin^2"A")/(sin"A"cos"A") + (cos^2"A")/(sin"A"cos"A")` = `"sin A"/"cos A" + "cos A"/"sin A"` = tan A + cot A = R.H.S ∴ sin 2 A . tan A + cos 2 A . cot A + 2 sin A . cos A = tan A + cot A
items.solution_html:
L.H.S = sin2A . tan A + cos2A . cot A + 2 sin A . cos A

= `sin^2"A"* (sin "A")/(cos "A")  + cos^2"A"* (cos"A")/(sin"A") + 2sin"A" *cos"A"`

= `(sin^3"A")/"cosA" + (cos^3"A")/"sinA" + 2sin"A"*cos"A"`

= `(sin^4"A" + cos^4"A" + 2sin^2"A"cos^2"A")/(sin"A"cos"A")`

= `(sin^2"A" + cos^2"A")^2/(sin"A"cos"A")` .....[∵ a2 + b2 + 2ab = (a + b)2]

= `1^2/(sin"A"cos"A")`    ......[∵ sin2A + cos2A = 1]

=  `1/(sin"A"cos"A")`  

= `(sin^2"A"+ cos^2"A")/(sin"A"cos"A")`  ......[∵ 1 = sin2A + cos2A]

= `(sin^2"A")/(sin"A"cos"A") + (cos^2"A")/(sin"A"cos"A")`

= `"sin A"/"cos A" + "cos A"/"sin A"`

= tan A + cot A

= R.H.S

∴ sin2A . tan A + cos2A . cot A + 2 sin A . cos A = tan A + cot A

items.title: Prove that sin4A – cos4A = 1 – 2cos2A - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: Prove that sin 4 A – cos 4 A = 1 – 2cos 2 A
items.solution_text: L.H.S = sin 4 A – cos 4 A = (sin 2 A) 2 – (cos 2 A) 2 = (sin 2 A + cos 2 A)(sin 2 A – cos 2 A) .....[∵ a 2 – b 2 = (a + b)(a – b)] = (1)(sin 2 A – cos 2 A) ......[∵ sin 2 A + cos 2 A = 1] = sin 2 A – cos 2 A = (1 – cos 2 A) – cos 2 A ......`[(because sin^2"A" + cos^2"A" = 1),(therefore 1 - cos^2"" = sin^2"A")]` = 1 – 2cos 2 A = R.H.S ∴ sin 4 A – cos 4 A = 1 – 2cos 2 A
items.solution_html:
L.H.S = sin4A – cos4A 

= (sin2A)2 – (cos2A)2

= (sin2A + cos2A)(sin2A – cos2A)   .....[∵  a2 – b2 = (a + b)(a – b)]

= (1)(sin2A – cos2A)     ......[∵ sin2A + cos2A = 1]

= sin2A – cos2A

= (1 – cos2A) – cos2A   ......`[(because sin^2"A" + cos^2"A" = 1),(therefore 1 -  cos^2"" = sin^2"A")]`

= 1 – 2cos2A

= R.H.S

∴ sin4A – cos4A = 1 – 2cos2A

items.title: Prove that sin6A + cos6A = 1 – 3sin2A . cos2A - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: Prove that sin 6 A + cos 6 A = 1 – 3sin 2 A . cos 2 A
items.solution_text: L.H.S = sin 6 A + cos 6 A = (sin 2 A) 3 + (cos 2 A) 3 = (1 – cos 2 A) 3 + (cos 2 A) 3 ......`[(because sin^2"A" + cos^2"A" = 1),(therefore 1 - cos^2"A" = sin^2A")]` = 1 – 3cos 2 A + 3(cos 2 A) 2 – (cos 2 A) 3 + cos 6 A ......[∵ (a – b) 3 = a 3 – 3a 2 b + 3ab 2 – b 3 ] = 1 – 3 cos 2 A(1 – cos 2 A) – cos 6 A + cos 6 A = 1 – 3 cos 2 A sin 2 A = R.H.S ∴ sin 6 A + cos 6 A = 1 – 3sin 2 A . cos 2 A
items.solution_html:
L.H.S = sin6A + cos6A

= (sin2A)3 + (cos2A)3   

= (1 – cos2A)3 + (cos2A)3    ......`[(because sin^2"A" + cos^2"A" = 1),(therefore 1 - cos^2"A" = sin^2A")]`

= 1 – 3cos2A + 3(cos2A)2 – (cos2A)3 + cos6A   ......[∵ (a – b)3 = a3 – 3a2b + 3ab2 – b3]

= 1 – 3 cos2A(1 – cos2A) – cos6A + cos6A

= 1 – 3 cos2A sin2A

= R.H.S

∴ sin6A + cos6A = 1 – 3sin2A . cos2A

items.title: Prove that tan(90-θ)+cot(90-θ)cosecθ = sec θ - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: Prove that `(tan(90 - theta) + cot(90 - theta))/("cosec" theta)` = sec θ
items.solution_text: L.H.S = `(tan(90 - theta) + cot(90 - theta))/("cosec" theta)` = `1/("cosec" theta)(cottheta + tantheta)` .....`[(because tan(90 - theta) = cot theta),(cot(90 - theta) = tantheta)]` = sin θ (cot θ + tan θ) = `sintheta ((costheta)/(sintheta) + (sintheta)/(costheta))` = `sintheta ((cos^2theta + sin^2theta)/(sintheta costheta))` = `sintheta (1/(sintheta costheta))` ......[∵ sin 2 θ + cos 2 θ = 1] = `1/costheta` = sec θ = R.H.S ∴ `(tan(90 - theta) + cot(90 - theta))/("cosec" theta)` = sec θ
items.solution_html:
L.H.S = `(tan(90 - theta) + cot(90 - theta))/("cosec"  theta)`

= `1/("cosec"  theta)(cottheta + tantheta)`  .....`[(because tan(90 - theta) = cot theta),(cot(90 - theta) = tantheta)]`

= sin θ (cot θ + tan θ)

= `sintheta ((costheta)/(sintheta) + (sintheta)/(costheta))`

= `sintheta ((cos^2theta + sin^2theta)/(sintheta costheta))`

= `sintheta (1/(sintheta costheta))`   ......[∵ sin2θ  + cos2θ = 1]

= `1/costheta`

= sec θ

= R.H.S

∴ `(tan(90 - theta) + cot(90 - theta))/("cosec"  theta)` = sec θ

items.title: Prove that tan Acot A=sec2Acosec2A - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: Prove that `"tan A"/"cot A" = (sec^2"A")/("cosec"^2"A")`
items.solution_html:
R.H.S = `(sec^2"A")/("cosec"^2"A")`

= `(1 + tan^2"A")/(1 + cot^2"A")`    .....`[(because 1 + tan^2"A" = sec^2"A"),(1 + cot^2"A" = "cosec"^2"A")]`

= `(1 + (sin^2"A")/(cos^2"A"))/(1 + (cos^2"A")/(sin^2"A"))`

= `((cos^2"A" + sin^2"A")/(cos^2"A"))/((sin^2"A" + cos^2"A")/(sin^2"A"))`

= `(1/(cos^2"A"))/(1/(sin^2"A"))`     .......[∵ sin2A + cos2A = 1]

= `(sin^2"A")/(cos^2"A")`

= tan2A

= tan A . tan A

= `"tan A"/"cot A"`

= L.H.S

∴ `"tan A"/"cot A" = (sec^2"A")/("cosec"^2"A")`

items.title: Prove the following theorem: Angles inscribed in the same arc are congruent. - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: Prove the following theorem: Angles inscribed in the same arc are congruent.
items.solution_text: Given: C is the circle's center. In the same arc PTR, ∠PQR and ∠PSR are written. To prove: ∠PQR ≅ ∠PSR Proof: arc PTR is intercepted by ∠PQR. arc PTR is intercepted by ∠PSR. ∠PQR = `1/2` m(arc PTR) ......(i) [Inscribed angle theorem] and ∠PSR = `1/2` m(arc PTR) ...(ii) [Inscribed angle theorem] ∴ ∠PQR ≅ ∠PSR ...[From (i) and (ii)]
items.solution_html:
Given: C is the circle's center. In the same arc PTR, ∠PQR and ∠PSR are written.

To prove: ∠PQR ≅ ∠PSR

Proof:

arc PTR is intercepted by ∠PQR.

arc PTR is intercepted by ∠PSR.

∠PQR = `1/2` m(arc PTR) ......(i) [Inscribed angle theorem]

and 

∠PSR = `1/2` m(arc PTR)   ...(ii) [Inscribed angle theorem]

∴ ∠PQR ≅ ∠PSR    ...[From (i) and (ii)]

items.title: Prove the following theorem: Tangent segments drawn from an external point to the circle are congruent. - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: Prove the following theorem: Tangent segments drawn from an external point to the circle are congruent.
items.solution_text: Given: A is the center of the circle. Tangents through external point D touch the circle at the points P and Q. To prove: seg DP ≅ seg DQ Construction: Draw seg AP, seg AD, and seg AQ. Proof: In ΔPAD and ΔQAD, seg PA ≅ seg QA ...[Radii of the same circle] seg AD ≅ seg AD ...[Common side] ∠APD = ∠AQD = 90° ...[Tangent theorem] ∴ ΔPAD ≅ ΔQAD ...[By Hypotenuse side test] ∴ seg DP ≅ seg DQ ...[Corresponding sides of congruent triangles]
items.solution_html:
Given: A is the center of the circle. Tangents through external point D touch the circle at the points P and Q.

To prove: seg DP ≅ seg DQ

Construction: Draw seg AP, seg AD, and seg AQ.

Proof: In ΔPAD and ΔQAD,

seg PA ≅ seg QA    ...[Radii of the same circle]

seg AD ≅ seg AD    ...[Common side]

∠APD = ∠AQD = 90°    ...[Tangent theorem]

∴ ΔPAD ≅ ΔQAD    ...[By Hypotenuse side test]

∴ seg DP ≅ seg DQ  ...[Corresponding sides of congruent triangles]

items.title: Prove the following theorems: Opposite angles of a cyclic quadrilateral are supplementary. - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: Prove the following theorems: Opposite angles of a cyclic quadrilateral are supplementary.
items.solution_text: arc ABC is intercepted by the inscribed angle ∠ADC. ∴ ∠ADC = `1/2` m(arc ABC) ......(i) [Inscribed angle theorem] Similarly, ∠ABC is an inscribed angle. It intercepts arc ADC. ∴ ∠ABC =`1/2` m(arc ADC) ......(ii) [Inscribed angle theorem] ∴ ∠ADC + ∠ABC = `1/2` m(arc ABC) + `1/2` m(arc ADC) ....[Adding (i) and (ii)] ∴ ∠D + ∠B = `1/2` m (arc ABC) + m(arc ADC)] ∴ ∠B + ∠D = `1/2 xx 360^circ` ......[arc ABC and arc ADC constitute a complete circle] = 180° ∴ ∠B + ∠D = 180° Similarly we can prove, ∠A + ∠C = 180°
items.solution_html:
arc ABC is intercepted by the inscribed angle ∠ADC.

∴ ∠ADC = `1/2` m(arc ABC)    ......(i) [Inscribed angle theorem]

Similarly, ∠ABC is an inscribed angle.

It intercepts arc ADC.

∴ ∠ABC =`1/2` m(arc ADC)    ......(ii) [Inscribed angle theorem]

∴ ∠ADC + ∠ABC = `1/2` m(arc ABC) + `1/2` m(arc ADC)  ....[Adding (i) and (ii)]

∴ ∠D + ∠B = `1/2` m (arc ABC) + m(arc ADC)]

∴ ∠B + ∠D = `1/2 xx 360^circ`    ......[arc ABC and arc ADC constitute a complete circle]

= 180°

∴ ∠B + ∠D = 180°

Similarly we can prove,

∠A + ∠C = 180°

items.title: Ratio of areas of two similar triangles is 9 : 25. _______ is the ratio of their corresponding sides. - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: Ratio of areas of two similar triangles is 9 : 25. _______ is the ratio of their corresponding sides.
items.solution_text: 3: 5 Let ΔABC and ΔPQR be two similar triangles. According to the given condition, `"A(ΔABC)"/"A(ΔPQR)" = 9/25` ...(Given) But `"A(ΔABC)"/"A(ΔPQR)" = "AB"^2/"PQ"^2` ...(By the theorem of areas of similar triangles) ∴ `"AB"^2/"PQ"^2 = 9/25` ∴ `"AB"/"PQ" = 3/5` ∴ 3: 5 is the ratio of their corresponding sides.
items.solution_html:
3: 5

Let ΔABC and ΔPQR be two similar triangles.  

According to the given condition,

`"A(ΔABC)"/"A(ΔPQR)" = 9/25`      ...(Given)

But `"A(ΔABC)"/"A(ΔPQR)" = "AB"^2/"PQ"^2` ...(By the theorem of areas of similar triangles)

∴ `"AB"^2/"PQ"^2 = 9/25`

∴ `"AB"/"PQ" = 3/5`

∴ 3: 5 is the ratio of their corresponding sides.

items.title: Ratio of corresponding sides of two similar triangles is 4:7, then find the ratio of their areas = ? - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: Ratio of corresponding sides of two similar triangles is 4:7, then find the ratio of their areas = ?
items.solution_text: Let the corresponding sides of similar triangles be s 1 and s 2 . Let A 1 and A 2 be their corresponding areas. s 1 : s 2 = 4 : 7 ......[Given] ∴ `"s"_1/"s"_2= 4/7` ......(i) by theorem of areas of similar triangles, `"A"_1/"A"_2 = "s"_1^2/"s"_2^2` `"A"_1/"A"_2 = ("s"_1/"s"_2)^2` `"A"_1/"A"_2 = (4/7)^2` ......[From (i)] `"A"_1/"A"_2 = 16/49` ∴ Ratio of areas of similar triangles = 16 : 49
items.solution_html:
Let the corresponding sides of similar triangles be s1 and s2.

Let A1 and A2 be their corresponding areas.

s1 : s2 = 4 : 7        ......[Given]

∴ `"s"_1/"s"_2= 4/7`     ......(i)

by theorem of areas of similar triangles,

`"A"_1/"A"_2 = "s"_1^2/"s"_2^2`  

`"A"_1/"A"_2 = ("s"_1/"s"_2)^2`

`"A"_1/"A"_2 = (4/7)^2`    ......[From (i)]

`"A"_1/"A"_2 = 16/49`

∴ Ratio of areas of similar triangles = 16 : 49

items.title: ΔRHP ~ ΔNED, In ΔNED, NE = 7 cm, ∠D = 30°, ∠N = 20° and HPED=45. Then construct ΔRHP and ΔNED - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: ΔRHP ~ ΔNED, In ΔNED, NE = 7 cm, ∠D = 30°, ∠N = 20° and `"HP"/"ED" = 4/5`. Then construct ΔRHP and ΔNED
items.solution_text: In ∆NED, ∠D = 30° and ∠N = 20° ......(i) [Given] ∴ ∠E = 130° .....(ii) [Remaining angle of a triangle] ∆RHP ∼ ∆NED ∴ `"RH"/"NE" = "HP"/"ED" = "PR"/"DN"` .....[Corresponding sides of similar triangles] ∴ `"RH"/7 = 4/5` ......[Given] ∴ RH = `(4 xx 7)/5` = 5.6 cm Also, ∠R = ∠N, ∠H = ∠E, ∠P = ∠D ......(iii) [Corresponding angles of similar triangles] ∴ ∠R = 20°, ∠H = 130°, ∠P = 30° ......[From (i),(ii) and (iii)] Steps of Construction: ∆NED ∆RHP i. Draw seg NE of 7 cm Draw seg RH of 5.6 cm ii. Draw a ray NA and EB such that ∠ANE = 20° and ∠BEN = 130°. Draw a ray RC and HD such that ∠CRH = 20° and ∠DHR = 130°. iii. Name the point of intersection of rays D. Name the point of intersection of rays P.
items.solution_html:
In ∆NED, ∠D = 30°

and ∠N = 20°   ......(i) [Given]

∴ ∠E = 130°    .....(ii) [Remaining angle of a triangle]

∆RHP ∼ ∆NED

∴ `"RH"/"NE" = "HP"/"ED" = "PR"/"DN"`  .....[Corresponding sides of similar triangles]

∴ `"RH"/7 = 4/5`   ......[Given]

∴ RH = `(4 xx 7)/5` = 5.6 cm

Also, ∠R = ∠N, ∠H = ∠E, ∠P = ∠D  ......(iii) [Corresponding angles of similar triangles]

∴ ∠R = 20°, ∠H = 130°, ∠P = 30°   ......[From (i),(ii) and (iii)]



Steps of Construction:

  ∆NED ∆RHP
i. Draw seg NE of 7 cm Draw seg RH of 5.6 cm
ii. Draw a ray NA and EB such that ∠ANE = 20° and ∠BEN = 130°. Draw a ray RC and HD such that ∠CRH = 20° and ∠DHR = 130°.
iii. Name the point of intersection of rays D. Name the point of intersection of rays P.

items.title: ΔRHP ~ ΔNED, In ΔNED, NE = 7 cm. ∠D = 30°, ∠N = 20°, HPEDHPED=45, then construct ΔRHP and ∆NED - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: ΔRHP ~ ΔNED, In ΔNED, NE = 7 cm. ∠D = 30°, ∠N = 20°, `"HP"/"ED" = 4/5`, then construct ΔRHP and ∆NED
items.solution_text: Analysis: In ∆NED, ∠D = 30° and ∠N = 20° ......[Given] ∴ ∠E = 130° ......(ii) [Remaining angle of a triangle] ∆RHP ∼ ∆NED ∴ `"RH"/"NE" = "HP"/"ED" = "PR"/"DN"` ......[Corresponding sides of similar triangles] ∴ `"RH"/7 = 4/5` ......[Given] ∴ RH = `(4 xx 7)/5` = 5.6 cm Also, ∠R = ∠N, ∠H = ∠E, ∠P = ∠D ......(iiii) [Corresponding angles of similar triangles] ∴ ∠R = 20°, ∠H = 130°, ∠P = 30° ......[From (i), (ii) and (iii)] Steps of construction: ∆NED ∆RHP i. Draw seg NE of 7 cm Draw seg RH of 5.6 cm ii. Draw a ray NA and EB such that ∠ANE = 20° and ∠BEN = 130°. Draw a ray RC and HD such that ∠CRH = 20° and ∠DHR = 130°. iii. Name the point of intersection of rays D. Name the point of intersection of rays P.
items.solution_html:
Analysis:

In ∆NED, ∠D = 30° and ∠N = 20°   ......[Given]

∴ ∠E = 130°    ......(ii) [Remaining angle of a triangle]

∆RHP ∼ ∆NED

∴ `"RH"/"NE" = "HP"/"ED" = "PR"/"DN"`  ......[Corresponding sides of similar triangles]

∴ `"RH"/7 = 4/5`    ......[Given]

∴ RH = `(4 xx 7)/5` = 5.6 cm

Also, ∠R = ∠N, ∠H = ∠E, ∠P = ∠D   ......(iiii) [Corresponding angles of similar triangles]

∴ ∠R = 20°, ∠H = 130°, ∠P = 30°    ......[From (i), (ii) and (iii)]



Steps of construction:

  ∆NED ∆RHP
i. Draw seg NE of 7 cm Draw seg RH of 5.6 cm
ii. Draw a ray NA and EB such that ∠ANE = 20° and ∠BEN = 130°. Draw a ray RC and HD such that ∠CRH = 20° and ∠DHR = 130°.
iii. Name the point of intersection of rays D. Name the point of intersection of rays P.

items.title: (sec θ + tan θ) . (sec θ – tan θ) = ? - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: (sec θ + tan θ) . (sec θ – tan θ) = ?
items.solution_text: (sec θ + tan θ)(sec θ – tan θ) = sec 2 θ – tan 2 θ ......[∵ (a + b)(a – b) = a 2 – b 2 ] = 1 ......`[(because 1 + tan^2theta = sec^2theta),(therefore sec^2theta - tan^2theta = 1)]`
items.solution_html:
(sec θ + tan θ)(sec θ – tan θ)

= sec2θ – tan2θ  ......[∵ (a + b)(a – b) = a2 – b2]

= 1       ......`[(because 1 + tan^2theta = sec^2theta),(therefore sec^2theta - tan^2theta = 1)]`

items.title: Seg OA is the radius of a circle with centre O. The coordinates of point A is (0, 2) then decide whether the point B(1, 2) is on the circle? - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: Seg OA is the radius of a circle with centre O. The coordinates of point A is (0, 2) then decide whether the point B(1, 2) is on the circle?
items.solution_text: Co-ordinates of A = (0, 2) Co-ordinates of O = (0, 0) Co-ordinates of B = (1, 2) Distance between two points = `sqrt((x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2` By distance formula, d(O, A) = `sqrt((0 - 0)^2 + (0 - 2)^2` = `sqrt((0)^2 + (-2)^2` = `sqrt(0 + 4)` = 2 ......(i) d(O, B) = `sqrt((0 - 1)^2 + (0 - 2)^2` `sqrt((-1)^2 + (-2)^2` = `sqrt(1 + 4)` = `sqrt(5)` ......(ii) ∴ From (i) and (ii), d(O, B) > d(O, A) ∴ d(O, B) > Radius of circle ∴ Point B(1, 2) does not lie on the circle but lies outside the circle.
items.solution_html:
Co-ordinates of A = (0, 2)

Co-ordinates of O = (0, 0)

Co-ordinates of B = (1, 2)

Distance between two points = `sqrt((x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2`

By distance formula,

d(O, A) = `sqrt((0 - 0)^2 + (0 - 2)^2`

= `sqrt((0)^2 + (-2)^2`

= `sqrt(0 + 4)`

= 2    ......(i)

d(O, B) = `sqrt((0 - 1)^2 + (0 - 2)^2`

`sqrt((-1)^2 + (-2)^2`

= `sqrt(1 + 4)`

= `sqrt(5)`    ......(ii)

∴ From (i) and (ii),

d(O, B) > d(O, A)

∴ d(O, B) > Radius of circle

∴ Point B(1, 2) does not lie on the circle but lies outside the circle.

items.title: Seg RM and seg RN are tangent segments of a circle with centre O. Prove that seg OR bisects ∠MRN as well as ∠MON with the help of activity. - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: Seg RM and seg RN are tangent segments of a circle with centre O. Prove that seg OR bisects ∠MRN as well as ∠MON with the help of activity. Proof: In ∆RMO and ∆RNO, ∠RMO ≅ ∠RNO = 90° ......[`square`] hypt OR ≅ hypt OR ......[`square`] seg OM ≅ seg `square` ......[Radii of the same circle] ∴ ∆RMO ≅ ∆RNO ......[`square`] ∠MOR ≅ ∠NOR Similairy ∠MRO ≅ `square` ......[`square`]
items.solution_text: Proof: In ∆RMO and ∆RNO, ∠RMO ≅ ∠RNO = 90° ......[ Tangent theorem ] hypt OR ≅ hypt OR ......[ Common side ] seg OM ≅ seg ON ......[Radii of the same circle] ∴ ∆RMO ≅ ∆RNO ......[ By Hypotenuse side test ] ∠MOR ≅ ∠NOR Similarly ∠MRO ≅ ∠NRO ......[ Corresponding angles of congruent triangles ]
items.solution_html:
Proof: In ∆RMO and ∆RNO,

∠RMO ≅ ∠RNO = 90°   ......[Tangent theorem]

hypt OR ≅ hypt OR    ......[Common side]

seg OM ≅ seg ON    ......[Radii of the same circle]

∴ ∆RMO ≅ ∆RNO      ......[By Hypotenuse side test]

∠MOR ≅ ∠NOR

Similarly ∠MRO ≅ ∠NRO    ......[Corresponding angles of congruent triangles]

items.title: Segment DP and segment DQ are tangent segments to the circle with center A. If DP = 7 cm. So find the length of the segment DQ. - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: Segment DP and segment DQ are tangent segments to the circle with center A. If DP = 7 cm. So find the length of the segment DQ.
items.solution_text: seg DP and seg DQ are the tangents to the circle. ∴ DP = DQ ......[Tangent segment theorem] ∴ DQ = 7 cm ......[Given]
items.solution_html:
seg DP and seg DQ are the tangents to the circle.

∴ DP = DQ     ......[Tangent segment theorem]

∴ DQ = 7 cm   ......[Given]

items.title: Show that A(1, 2), (1, 6), C(1 + 2 3, 4) are vertices of a equilateral triangle - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: Show that A(1, 2), (1, 6), C(1 + 2 `sqrt(3)`, 4) are vertices of a equilateral triangle
items.solution_text: Distance between two points = `sqrt((x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2` By distance formula, AB = `sqrt((1- 1)^2 + (6 - 2)^2` = `sqrt(0^2 + 4^2)` = `sqrt(4^2)` = 4 ......(i) BC = `sqrt((1 + 2sqrt(3) - 1)^2 + (4 - 6)^2` = `sqrt((2sqrt(3))^2 + (-2)^2` = `sqrt(12 + 4)` = `sqrt(16)` = 4 .....(ii) AC = `sqrt((1 + 2sqrt(3) - 1)^2 + (4 -2)^2` = `sqrt((2sqrt(3))^2 + 2^2` = `sqrt(12 + 4)` = `sqrt(16)` = 4 ......(iii) ∴ AB = BC = AC ......[From (i), (ii) and (iii)] ∴ ∆ABC is an equilateral triangle. ∴ Points A, B and C are the vertices of an equilateral triangle.
items.solution_html:
Distance between two points = `sqrt((x_2 -  x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2`

By distance formula,

AB = `sqrt((1- 1)^2 + (6 - 2)^2`

= `sqrt(0^2 + 4^2)`

= `sqrt(4^2)`

= 4    ......(i)

BC = `sqrt((1 + 2sqrt(3) - 1)^2 + (4 - 6)^2`

= `sqrt((2sqrt(3))^2 + (-2)^2`

= `sqrt(12 + 4)`

= `sqrt(16)`

= 4    .....(ii)

AC = `sqrt((1 + 2sqrt(3) - 1)^2 + (4 -2)^2`

= `sqrt((2sqrt(3))^2 + 2^2`

= `sqrt(12 + 4)`

= `sqrt(16)`

= 4   ......(iii)

∴ AB = BC = AC   ......[From (i), (ii) and (iii)]

∴ ∆ABC is an equilateral triangle.

∴ Points A, B and C are the vertices of an equilateral triangle.

items.title: Show that P(– 2, 2), Q(2, 2) and R(2, 7) are vertices of a right angled triangle - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: Show that P(– 2, 2), Q(2, 2) and R(2, 7) are vertices of a right angled triangle
items.solution_text: Distance between two points = `sqrt((x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2` By distance formula, PQ = `sqrt([2 - (-2)]^2 + (2 - 2)^2` = `sqrt((2 + 2)^2 + (0)^2` = `sqrt((4)^2` = 4 .....(i) QR = `sqrt((2 - 2)^2 + (7 - 2)^2` = `sqrt((0)^2 + (5)^2` = `sqrt((5)^2` = 5 ......(ii) PR = `sqrt([2 -(-2)]^2 + (7 - 2)^2` = `sqrt((2 + 2)^2 + (5)^2` = `sqrt((4)^2 + (5)^2` = `sqrt(16 + 25)` = `sqrt(41)` Now, PR 2 = `(sqrt(41))^2` = 41 ......(iii) Consider, PQ 2 + QR 2 = 4 2 + 5 2 = 16 + 25 = 41 ......[From (i) and (ii)] ∴ PR 2 = PQ 2 + QR 2 ......[From (iii)] ∴ ∆PQR is a right angled triangle. ......[Converse of Pythagoras theorem] ∴ Points P, Q, and R are the vertices of a right angled triangle.
items.solution_html:
Distance between two points = `sqrt((x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2`

By distance formula,

PQ = `sqrt([2 - (-2)]^2 + (2 - 2)^2`

= `sqrt((2 + 2)^2 + (0)^2`

= `sqrt((4)^2`

= 4    .....(i)

QR = `sqrt((2 - 2)^2 + (7 - 2)^2`

= `sqrt((0)^2 + (5)^2`

= `sqrt((5)^2`

= 5   ......(ii)

PR = `sqrt([2 -(-2)]^2 + (7 - 2)^2`

= `sqrt((2 + 2)^2 + (5)^2`

= `sqrt((4)^2 + (5)^2`

= `sqrt(16 + 25)`

= `sqrt(41)`

Now, PR2 = `(sqrt(41))^2` = 41   ......(iii)

Consider, PQ2 + QR2

= 42 + 52

= 16 + 25

= 41    ......[From (i) and (ii)]

∴ PR2 = PQ2 + QR2   ......[From (iii)]

∴ ∆PQR is a right angled triangle.   ......[Converse of Pythagoras theorem]

∴ Points P, Q, and R are the vertices of a right angled triangle.

items.title: Show that points A(– 4, –7), B(–1, 2), C(8, 5) and D(5, – 4) are the vertices of a parallelogram ABCD - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: Show that points A(– 4, –7), B(–1, 2), C(8, 5) and D(5, – 4) are the vertices of a parallelogram ABCD
items.solution_text: We know that, slope of line = `(y_2 - y_1)/(x_2 - x_1)` Slope of side AB = `(2 - (-7))/(-1 - (-4))` = `(2 + 7)/(-1 + 4)` = `9/3` = 3 ......(i) Slope of side BC = `(5 - 2)/(8 - (-1))` = `3/(8 + 1)` = `3/9` = `1/3` ......(ii) Slope of side CD = `(-4 - 5)/(5 - 8)` = `(-9)/(-3)` = 3 .....(iii) Slope of side AD = `(-4 - (-7))/(5 - (-4))` = `(-4 + 7)/(5 + 4)` =`3/9` = `1/3` ......(iv) ∴ Slope of side AB = Slope of side CD ......[From (i) and (iii)] ∴ Side AB || side CD ∴ Slope of side BC = Slope of side AD .......[From (ii) and (iv)] ∴ Side BC || side AD Both the pairs of opposite sides of ABCD are parallel ∴ ▢ABCD is a parallelogram. ∴ Points A(– 4, – 7), B(– 1, 2), C(8, 5) and D(5, – 4) are the vertices of a parallelogram.
items.solution_html:
We know that, slope of line = `(y_2 - y_1)/(x_2 - x_1)`

Slope of side AB = `(2 - (-7))/(-1 - (-4))`

= `(2 + 7)/(-1 + 4)`

= `9/3`

= 3    ......(i)

Slope of side BC = `(5 - 2)/(8 - (-1))`

= `3/(8 + 1)`

= `3/9`

= `1/3`  ......(ii)

Slope of side CD = `(-4 - 5)/(5 - 8)`

= `(-9)/(-3)`

= 3       .....(iii)

Slope of side AD = `(-4 - (-7))/(5 - (-4))`

= `(-4 + 7)/(5 + 4)`

=`3/9`

= `1/3`   ......(iv)

∴ Slope of side AB = Slope of side CD   ......[From (i) and (iii)]

∴ Side AB || side CD

∴ Slope of side BC = Slope of side AD   .......[From (ii) and (iv)]

∴ Side BC || side AD

Both the pairs of opposite sides of ABCD are parallel

∴ ▢ABCD is a parallelogram.

∴ Points A(– 4, – 7), B(– 1, 2), C(8, 5) and D(5, – 4) are the vertices of a parallelogram.

items.title: Show that tan 7° × tan 23° × tan 60° × tan 67° × tan 83° = 3 - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: Show that tan 7° × tan 23° × tan 60° × tan 67° × tan 83° = `sqrt(3)`
items.solution_html:
L.H.S = tan 7° × tan 23° × tan 60° × tan 67° × tan 83°

= tan 7° × tan 23° × `sqrt(3)` × tan(90° – 23°) × tan(90° – 7°)

= `sqrt(3)` × [tan 7° × tan(90° – 7°)] × [tan 23° × tan(90° – 23°)]

= `sqrt(3) xx 1 xx 1`    ......[∵ tan θ × tan(90° – θ) = 1]

= `sqrt(3)`

= R.H.S

∴ tan 7° × tan 23° × tan 60° × tan 67° × tan 83° = `sqrt(3)`

items.title: Show that the point (0, 9) is equidistant from the points (– 4, 1) and (4, 1) - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: Show that the point (0, 9) is equidistant from the points (– 4, 1) and (4, 1)
items.solution_html:
Let P(x1, y1) = P(0, 9), Q(x2, y2) = Q(– 4, 1), R(x3, y3) = R(4, 1)

By distance formula,

d(P, Q) = `sqrt((x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2`

= `sqrt([(-4) - 0]^2 + (1 - 9)^2`

= `sqrt((-4)^2 + (-8)^2`

= `sqrt(16 + 64)`

= `sqrt(80)`

= `4sqrt(5)`

And

d(P, R) = `sqrt((x_3 - x_1)^2 + (y_3 - y_1)^2`

= `sqrt((4 - 0)^2 + (1 - 9)^2`

= `sqrt(4^2 + (-8)^2`

= `sqrt(16 + 64)`

= `sqrt(80)`

= `4sqrt(5)`

Here, d(P, Q) = d(P, R)

∴ The point (0, 9) is equidistant from (– 4, 1) and (4, 1).

items.title: Show that the points (0, –1), (8, 3), (6, 7) and (– 2, 3) are vertices of a rectangle. - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: Show that the points (0, –1), (8, 3), (6, 7) and (– 2, 3) are vertices of a rectangle.
items.solution_text: Let the points be P(0, –1), Q(8, 3), R(6, 7), S(–2, 3) Distance between two points= `sqrt((x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2` ∴ By distance formula, d(P, Q) = `sqrt((8 - 0)^2 + [3 - (-1)]^2` = `sqrt((8 - 0)^2 + (3 + 1)^2` = `sqrt(8^2 + 4^2)` = `sqrt(64 + 16)` = `sqrt(80)` ......(i) d(Q, R) = `sqrt((6 - 8)^2 + (7 - 3)^2` = `sqrt((-2)^2 + (4)^2` = `sqrt(4 + 16)` = `sqrt(20)` ......(ii) d(R, S) = `sqrt([(-2) - 6]^2 + (3 - 7)^2` = `sqrt((-8)^2 + (-4)^2` = `sqrt(64 + 16)` =`sqrt(80)` ......(iii) d(P, S) = `sqrt([(-2) - 0]^2 + [3 - (-1)^2]` = `sqrt((-2)^2+ (3+ 1)^2` = `sqrt((-2)^2 + 4^2` = `sqrt(4 + 16)` = `sqrt(20)` ......(iv) In ▢PQRS, ∴ side PQ = side RS .......[From (i) and (iii)] side QR = side PS ......[From (ii) and (iv)] ∴ ▢PQRS is a parallelogram ......[A quadrilateral is a parallelogram, if both the pairs of its opposite sides are congruent] d(P, R) = `sqrt((6 - 0)^2 + [7 - (-1)]^2` = `sqrt((6 - 0)^2 + (7 + 1)^2` = `sqrt(6^2 + 8^2)` = `sqrt(36 + 64)` = `sqrt(100)` = 10 ......(iv) d(Q, S) = `sqrt([(-2) - 8]^2 + [3 - 3]^2` = `sqrt((-10)^2 + (0)^2` = `sqrt(100 + 0)` = `sqrt(100)` = 10 ......(vi) In parallelogram PQRS, PR = QS .......[From (v) and (vi)] ∴ ▢PQRS is a rectangle. .......[A parallelogram is a rectangle if its diagonals are equal]
items.solution_html:
Let the points be P(0, –1), Q(8, 3), R(6, 7), S(–2, 3)

Distance between two points= `sqrt((x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2`

∴ By distance formula,

d(P, Q) = `sqrt((8 - 0)^2 + [3 - (-1)]^2`

= `sqrt((8 - 0)^2 + (3 + 1)^2`

= `sqrt(8^2 + 4^2)`

= `sqrt(64 + 16)`

= `sqrt(80)`              ......(i)

d(Q, R) = `sqrt((6 - 8)^2 + (7 - 3)^2`

= `sqrt((-2)^2 + (4)^2`

= `sqrt(4 + 16)`

= `sqrt(20)`          ......(ii)

d(R, S) = `sqrt([(-2) - 6]^2 + (3 - 7)^2`

= `sqrt((-8)^2 + (-4)^2`

= `sqrt(64 + 16)`

=`sqrt(80)`            ......(iii)

d(P, S) = `sqrt([(-2) - 0]^2 + [3 - (-1)^2]`

= `sqrt((-2)^2+ (3+ 1)^2`

= `sqrt((-2)^2 + 4^2`

= `sqrt(4 + 16)`

= `sqrt(20)`           ......(iv)

In ▢PQRS,

∴ side PQ = side RS              .......[From (i) and (iii)]

side QR = side PS              ......[From (ii) and (iv)]

∴ ▢PQRS is a parallelogram            ......[A quadrilateral is a parallelogram, if both the pairs of its opposite sides are congruent]

d(P, R) = `sqrt((6 - 0)^2 + [7 - (-1)]^2`

= `sqrt((6 - 0)^2 + (7 + 1)^2`

= `sqrt(6^2 + 8^2)`

= `sqrt(36 + 64)`

= `sqrt(100)`

= 10                 ......(iv)

d(Q, S) = `sqrt([(-2) - 8]^2 + [3 - 3]^2`

= `sqrt((-10)^2 + (0)^2`

= `sqrt(100 + 0)`

= `sqrt(100)`

= 10                ......(vi)

In parallelogram PQRS,

PR = QS              .......[From (v) and (vi)]

∴ ▢PQRS is a rectangle.           .......[A parallelogram is a rectangle if its diagonals are equal]

items.title: Show that the points (2, 0), (– 2, 0) and (0, 2) are vertices of a triangle. State the type of triangle with reason - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: Show that the points (2, 0), (– 2, 0) and (0, 2) are vertices of a triangle. State the type of triangle with reason
items.solution_text: Let the points be P(2, 0), Q(– 2, 0) and R(0, 2) Distance between two points = `sqrt((x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2` By distance formula, d(P, Q) = `sqrt([(-2) - 2]^2 + (0 - 0)^2` = `sqrt((-4)^2 + (0)^2` = `sqrt(16 + 0)` = 4 .....(i) d(Q, R) = `sqrt([0 - (-2)]^2 + (2 - 0)^2` = `sqrt((2)^2 + (2)^2` = `sqrt(4 + 4)` = `sqrt(8)` ......(ii) d (P, R) = `sqrt((0 -2)^2 + (2 - 0)^2` = `sqrt((- 2)^2 + (2)^2` = `sqrt(4 + 4)` = `sqrt(8)` ......(iii) On adding (ii) and (iii), d(P, Q) + d(Q, R) = `4 + sqrt(8)` `4 + sqrt(8) > sqrt(8)` ∴ d(P, Q) + d(Q, R) > d(P, R) ∴ Points P, Q, R are non colinear points. We can construct a triangle through 3 non collinear points. ∴ The segment joining the given points form a triangle. Since P(Q, R) = P(P, R) ∴ ∆PQR is an isosceles triangle. ∴ The segment joining the points (2, 0), (– 2, 0) and (0, 2) will form an isosceles triangle.
items.solution_html:
Let the points be P(2, 0), Q(– 2, 0) and R(0, 2)

Distance between two points = `sqrt((x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2`

By distance formula,

d(P, Q) = `sqrt([(-2) - 2]^2 + (0 - 0)^2`

= `sqrt((-4)^2 + (0)^2`

= `sqrt(16 + 0)`

= 4    .....(i)

d(Q, R) = `sqrt([0 - (-2)]^2 + (2 - 0)^2`

= `sqrt((2)^2 + (2)^2`

= `sqrt(4 + 4)`

= `sqrt(8)`  ......(ii)

d (P, R) = `sqrt((0 -2)^2 + (2 - 0)^2`

= `sqrt((- 2)^2 + (2)^2`

= `sqrt(4 + 4)`

= `sqrt(8)`  ......(iii)

On adding (ii) and (iii),

d(P, Q) + d(Q, R) = `4 + sqrt(8)`

`4 + sqrt(8) > sqrt(8)`

∴ d(P, Q) + d(Q, R) > d(P, R)

∴ Points P, Q, R are non colinear points.

We can construct a triangle through 3 non collinear points.

∴ The segment joining the given points form a triangle.

Since P(Q, R) = P(P, R)

∴ ∆PQR is an isosceles triangle.

∴ The segment joining the points (2, 0), (– 2, 0) and (0, 2) will form an isosceles triangle.

items.title: Side of equilateral triangle PQR is 8 cm then find the area of triangle whose side is half of the side of triangle PQR. - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: Side of equilateral triangle PQR is 8 cm then find the area of triangle whose side is half of the side of triangle PQR.
items.solution_text: Given: ΔPQR is an equilateral triangle with PQ = QR = PR = 8 cm and ΔABC is an equilateral triangle with AB = BC = AC = 4 cm To find: A(ΔABC) Construction: Draw seg AD ⊥ BC, B−D−C In ΔABD, ∠ADB = 90° ...[Construction] ∠ABD = 60° ...[Angle of an equilateral triangle] ∠BAD = 30° ...[Remaining angle of a triangle] ∴ ΔABD is a 30°–60°–90° triangle. ∴ AD = `sqrt(3)/2` AB ...[Side opposite to 60°] ∴ AD = `sqrt(3)/2 xx 4` ∴ AD = `2sqrt(3)` ...(i) Area of triangle = `1/2 xx "height" xx "base" ` ∴ Area of ΔABC = `1/2 xx "AD" xx "BC"` = `1/2 xx 2sqrt(3) xx 4` = `2 xx 2sqrt(3)` = `4sqrt(3)` ......[From (i)] ∴ Area of the triangle whose side is half of the side of ΔPQR is `4sqrt(3)` sq.cm
items.solution_html:
Given: ΔPQR is an equilateral triangle with PQ = QR = PR = 8 cm and ΔABC is an equilateral triangle with AB = BC = AC = 4 cm

To find: A(ΔABC)

Construction: Draw seg AD ⊥ BC, B−D−C

In ΔABD,

∠ADB = 90°       ...[Construction]

∠ABD = 60°       ...[Angle of an equilateral triangle]

∠BAD = 30°      ...[Remaining angle of a triangle]

∴ ΔABD is a 30°–60°–90° triangle.

∴ AD = `sqrt(3)/2` AB     ...[Side opposite to 60°]

∴ AD = `sqrt(3)/2 xx 4`

∴ AD = `2sqrt(3)`    ...(i)

Area of triangle = `1/2 xx "height" xx "base"  `

∴ Area of ΔABC = `1/2 xx "AD" xx "BC"`

= `1/2 xx 2sqrt(3) xx 4`

= `2 xx 2sqrt(3)`

= `4sqrt(3)`     ......[From (i)]

∴ Area of the triangle whose side is half of the side of ΔPQR is `4sqrt(3)` sq.cm

items.title: Sin2θ + sin2(90 – θ) = ? - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: sin 2 θ + sin 2 (90 – θ) = ?
items.solution_text: 1 Explanation: (sin (90 – θ)) 2 = (cosθ) 2 sin 2 (90 – θ) = cos 2 θ ...(1) sin 2 θ + cos 2 θ = 1 ∴ sin 2 θ + sin 2 (90 – θ) = 1 ...From (1)
items.solution_html:
1

Explanation:

(sin (90 – θ))2 = (cosθ)2 

sin2 (90 – θ) = cos2θ     ...(1)

sin2θ + cos2θ = 1      

∴ sin2θ + sin2(90 – θ) = 1     ...From (1)

items.title: Sin4A – cos4A = 1 – 2cos2A. For proof of this complete the activity given below. Activity: L.H.S = □ = (sin2A + cos2A) (□) = 1(□) .....[sin2A+□=1] = □ – cos2A .....[sin2A = 1 – cos2A] = □ = R.H.S - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: sin 4 A – cos 4 A = 1 – 2cos 2 A. For proof of this complete the activity given below. Activity: L.H.S = `square` = (sin 2 A + cos 2 A) `(square)` = `1 (square)` .....`[sin^2"A" + square = 1]` = `square` – cos 2 A .....[sin 2 A = 1 – cos 2 A] = `square` = R.H.S
items.solution_text: L.H.S = sin 4 A – cos 4 A = (sin 2 A) 2 – (cos 2 A) 2 = (sin 2 A + cos 2 A) ( sin 2 A – cos 2 A ) .....[∵ a 2 – b 2 = (a + b)(a – b)] = 1( sin 2 A – cos 2 A ) .....[∵ sin 2 A + cos 2 A = 1] = sin 2 A – cos 2 A = 1 – cos 2 A – cos 2 A .....[sin 2 A = 1 – cos 2 A] = 1 – 2cos 2 A = R.H.S
items.solution_html:
L.H.S = sin4A – cos4A 

= (sin2A)2 – (cos2A)2

 = (sin2A + cos2A) (sin2A – cos2A)    .....[∵ a2 – b2 = (a + b)(a – b)]

= 1(sin2A – cos2A)       .....[∵ sin2A + cos2A = 1]

= sin2A – cos2A

= 1 – cos2A – cos2A    .....[sin2A = 1 – cos2A]

= 1 – 2cos2A

= R.H.S

items.title: Sin75∘cos15∘ = ? - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: `(sin 75^circ)/(cos 15^circ)` = ?
items.solution_text: `(sin 75^circ)/(cos 15^circ)` = `(sin(90^circ - 15^circ))/(cos 15^circ)` = `(cos 15^circ)/(cos 15^circ)` .....[∵ sin(90° – θ) = cos θ] = 1
items.solution_html:
`(sin 75^circ)/(cos 15^circ)` = `(sin(90^circ - 15^circ))/(cos 15^circ)`

= `(cos 15^circ)/(cos 15^circ)`    .....[∵ sin(90° – θ) = cos θ]

= 1

items.title: State whether the following triangles are similar or not: If yes, then write the test of similarity. ∠P = 35°, ∠X = 35° and ∠Q = 60°, ∠Y = 60° - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: State whether the following triangles are similar or not: If yes, then write the test of similarity. ∠P = 35°, ∠X = 35° and ∠Q = 60°, ∠Y = 60°
items.solution_text: In ΔPQR and ΔXYZ, ∠P = 35°, ∠X = 35°, ∠Q = 60° and ∠Y = 60° ......[Given] ∴ ∠P ≅ ∠X and ∠Q ≅ ∠Y ∴ ΔPQR ~ ΔXYZ ...[AA test of similarity] ∴ The triangles in the figure are similar by AA test of similarity.
items.solution_html:
In ΔPQR and ΔXYZ,

∠P = 35°, ∠X = 35°, ∠Q = 60° and ∠Y = 60°   ......[Given]

∴ ∠P ≅ ∠X and ∠Q ≅ ∠Y  

∴ ΔPQR ~ ΔXYZ        ...[AA test of similarity]

∴ The triangles in the figure are similar by AA test of similarity.

items.title: Take point P and Q and draw a circle passing through them. Draw a tangent AB to the circle without using the centre of the circle - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: Take point P and Q and draw a circle passing through them. Draw a tangent AB to the circle without using the centre of the circle.
items.solution_text: Analysis: Centre of the circle passing through P and Q must be equidistant from points P and Q. ∴ It must lie of perpendicular bisector of seg PQ. Steps of construction: Take any two point P, Q and join them. Draw a perpendicular bisector of PQ. Take any point O on the perpendicular bisector and draw a circle with centre O and radius OP. Take any point A on the major arc of the circle and draw ∆PQA. By taking P as centre and any convenient distance on compass draw an arc intersecting the arms of ∠QPA in points T and R. With A as centre and the same distance in the compass, draw an arc intersecting the chord QA at point S. Taking radius equal to TR and S as centre, draw an arc intersecting the previously drawn arc. Name the point of intersection as B. Draw line AB. Line AB is the required tangent to the circle.
items.solution_html:
Analysis:

Centre of the circle passing through P and Q must be equidistant from points P and Q.

∴ It must lie of perpendicular bisector of seg PQ.

Steps of construction:

  1. Take any two point P, Q and join them.
  2. Draw a perpendicular bisector of PQ.
  3. Take any point O on the perpendicular bisector and draw a circle with centre O and radius OP.
  4. Take any point A on the major arc of the circle and draw ∆PQA.
  5. By taking P as centre and any convenient distance on compass draw an arc intersecting the arms of ∠QPA in points T and R.
  6. With A as centre and the same distance in the compass, draw an arc intersecting the chord QA at point S.
  7. Taking radius equal to TR and S as centre, draw an arc intersecting the previously drawn arc. Name the point of intersection as B.
  8. Draw line AB. Line AB is the required tangent to the circle.

items.title: Tan2θ – sin2θ = tan2θ × sin2θ. For proof of this complete the activity given below. Activity: L.H.S = □ = □(1-sin2θtan2θ) = tan2θ(1-□sin2θcos2θ) = tan2θ(1 sin2θ1×cos2θ□) = tan2θ(1-□) = tan2θ×□ ..... - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: tan 2 θ – sin 2 θ = tan 2 θ × sin 2 θ. For proof of this complete the activity given below. Activity: L.H.S = `square` = `square (1 - (sin^2theta)/(tan^2theta))` = `tan^2theta (1 - square/((sin^2theta)/(cos^2theta)))` = `tan^2theta (1 - (sin^2theta)/1 xx (cos^2theta)/square)` = `tan^2theta (1 - square)` = `tan^2theta xx square` .....[1 – cos 2 θ = sin 2 θ] = R.H.S
items.solution_text: L.H.S = tan 2 θ – sin 2 θ = `tan^2theta (1 - (sin^2theta)/(tan^2theta))` = `tan^2theta (1 - (sin^2theta)/((sin^2theta)/(cos^2theta)))` = `tan^2theta (1 - (sin^2theta)/1 xx (cos^2theta)/sin^2theta)` = `tan^2theta (1 - cos^2theta)` = tan 2 θ × sin 2 θ .....[1 – cos 2 θ = sin 2 θ] = R.H.S
items.solution_html:
L.H.S = tan2θ – sin2θ 

= `tan^2theta (1 - (sin^2theta)/(tan^2theta))`

= `tan^2theta (1 - (sin^2theta)/((sin^2theta)/(cos^2theta)))`

= `tan^2theta (1 - (sin^2theta)/1 xx (cos^2theta)/sin^2theta)`

= `tan^2theta (1 - cos^2theta)`

= tan2θ × sin2θ     .....[1 – cos2θ = sin2θ]

= R.H.S

items.title: Tangent segments drawn from an external point to a circle are congruent, prove this theorem. Complete the following activity.Given: □ To Prove: □ Proof: Draw radius AP and radius AQ and complete - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: Tangent segments drawn from an external point to a circle are congruent, prove this theorem. Complete the following activity. Given: `square` To Prove: `square` Proof: Draw radius AP and radius AQ and complete the following proof of the theorem. In ∆PAD and ∆QAD, seg PA ≅ `square` .....[Radii of the same circle] seg AD ≅ seg AD ......[`square`] ∠APD ≅ ∠AQD = 90° .....[Tangent theorem] ∴ ∆PAD ≅ ∆QAD ....[`square`] ∴ seg DP ≅ seg DQ .....[`square`]
items.solution_text: Given: A is the centre of the circle. Tangents through external point D Touch the circle at the points P and Q. To Prove: seg DP ≅ seg DQ Proof: In ∆PAD and ∆QAD, seg PA ≅ seg QA .....[Radii of the same circle] seg AD ≅ seg AD ......[ Common side ] ∠APD = ∠AQD = 90° .....[Tangent theorem] ∴ ∆PAD ≅ ∆QAD .....[ By Hypotenuse side test ] ∴ seg DP ≅ seg DQ .....[ Corresponding sides of congruent triangles ]
items.solution_html:
Given: A is the centre of the circle. Tangents through external point D Touch the circle at the points P and Q.

To Prove: seg DP ≅ seg DQ

Proof: 

In ∆PAD and ∆QAD,

seg PA ≅ seg QA      .....[Radii of the same circle]

seg AD ≅ seg AD    ......[Common side]

∠APD = ∠AQD = 90°     .....[Tangent theorem]

∴ ∆PAD ≅ ∆QAD    .....[By Hypotenuse side test]

∴ seg DP ≅ seg DQ  .....[Corresponding sides of congruent triangles]

items.title: The angle inscribed in the semicircle is a right angle. Prove the result by completing the following activity.Given: ∠ABC is inscribed angle in a semicircle with center M T - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: The angle inscribed in the semicircle is a right angle. Prove the result by completing the following activity. Given: ∠ABC is inscribed angle in a semicircle with center M To prove: ∠ABC is a right angle. Proof: Segment AC is a diameter of the circle. ∴ m(arc AXC) = `square` Arc AXC is intercepted by the inscribed angle ∠ABC ∠ABC = `square` ......[Inscribed angle theorem] = `1/2 xx square` ∴ m∠ABC = `square` ∴ ∠ABC is a right angle.
items.solution_text: Proof: Segment AC is a diameter of the circle. ∴ m(arc AXC) = 180° ......(i) [Measure of semi circular arc is 180°] Arc AXC is intercepted by the inscribed angle ∠ABC ∠ABC = `1/2 "m"("arc AXC")` ......[Inscribed angle theorem] = `1/2 xx 180^circ` ......[From (i)] ∴ m∠ABC = 90° ∴ ∠ABC is a right angle.
items.solution_html:
Proof: Segment AC is a diameter of the circle.

∴ m(arc AXC) = 180°    ......(i) [Measure of semi circular arc is 180°]

Arc AXC is intercepted by the inscribed angle ∠ABC

∠ABC = `1/2 "m"("arc AXC")`      ......[Inscribed angle theorem]

= `1/2 xx 180^circ`     ......[From (i)]

∴ m∠ABC = 90°

∴ ∠ABC is a right angle.

items.title: The chords AB and CD of the circle intersect at point M in the interior of the same circle then prove that CM × BD = BM × AC - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: The chords AB and CD of the circle intersect at point M in the interior of the same circle then prove that CM × BD = BM × AC
items.solution_text: Given: Chords AB and CD intersect at point M. To prove: CM × BD = BM × AC Proof: In ∆AMC and ∆DMB, ∠AMC ≅ ∠DMB ......[Vertically opposite angles] ∠ACD ≅ ∠ABD ......[Angles inscribed in the same arc] ∴ ∆AMC ∼ ∆DMB ......[AA test of similarity] ∴ `"CM"/"BM" = "AC"/"BD"` .....[Corresponding sides of similar triangles] ∴ CM × BD = BM × AC
items.solution_html:
Given: Chords AB and CD intersect at point M.

To prove: CM × BD = BM × AC

Proof: In ∆AMC and ∆DMB,

∠AMC ≅ ∠DMB   ......[Vertically opposite angles]

∠ACD ≅ ∠ABD   ......[Angles inscribed in the same arc]

∴ ∆AMC ∼ ∆DMB   ......[AA test of similarity]

∴ `"CM"/"BM" = "AC"/"BD"`   .....[Corresponding sides of similar triangles]

∴ CM × BD = BM × AC

items.title: The chords corresponding to congruent arcs of a circle are congruent. Prove the theorem by completing following activity.Given: In a circle with centre B arc APC ≅ arc DQE To Prove: Chord AC ≅ chord - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: The chords corresponding to congruent arcs of a circle are congruent. Prove the theorem by completing following activity. Given: In a circle with centre B arc APC ≅ arc DQE To Prove: Chord AC ≅ chord DE Proof: In ΔABC and ΔDBE, side AB ≅ side DB ......`square` side BC ≅ side `square` .....`square` ∠ABC ≅ ∠DBE ......[Measure of congruent arcs] ∆ABC ≅ ∆DBE ......`square`
items.solution_text: In ΔABC and ΔDBE, side AB ≅ side DB ...... [Radii of the same circle] side BC ≅ side BE ..... [Radii of the same circle] ∠ABC ≅ ∠DBE ......[Measure of congruent arcs] ∴ ∆ABC ≅ ∆DBE ...... [SAS test of congruency] ∴ chord AC ≅ chord DE ......[Corresponding sides of congruent triangles]
items.solution_html:
In ΔABC and ΔDBE,

side AB ≅ side DB    ......[Radii of the same circle]

side BC ≅ side BE    .....[Radii of the same circle]

∠ABC ≅ ∠DBE    ......[Measure of congruent arcs]

∴ ∆ABC ≅ ∆DBE    ......[SAS test of congruency]

∴ chord AC ≅ chord DE    ......[Corresponding sides of congruent triangles]

items.title: The coordinates of diameter AB of a circle are A(2, 7) and B(4 , 5), then find the coordinates of the centre - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: The coordinates of diameter AB of a circle are A(2, 7) and B(4, 5), then find the coordinates of the centre
items.solution_text: Let C(x, y) be the centre of the circle, A(x 1 , y 1 ) = A(2, 7), B(x 2 , y 2 ) = B(4, 5) ∴ x 1 = 2, y 1 = 7, x 2 = 4, y 2 = 5 C is the mid-point of seg AB. ∴ By midpoint formula, C(x, y) = `((x_1 + x_2)/2, (y_1 + y_2)/2)` = `((2 + 4)/2, (7 + 5)/2)` = `(6/2, 12/2)` ∴ C(x, y) = C(3, 6) ∴ The co-ordinates of the centre of the circle are (3, 6).
items.solution_html:
Let C(x, y) be the centre of the circle,

A(x1, y1) = A(2, 7), B(x2, y2) = B(4, 5)

∴ x1 = 2, y1 = 7, x2 = 4, y2 = 5

C is the mid-point of seg AB.

∴ By midpoint formula,

C(x, y) = `((x_1 + x_2)/2, (y_1 + y_2)/2)`

= `((2 + 4)/2, (7 + 5)/2)`

= `(6/2, 12/2)`

∴ C(x, y) = C(3, 6)

∴ The co-ordinates of the centre of the circle are (3, 6).

items.title: The coordinates of the vertices of a triangle ABC are A (–7, 6), B(2, –2) and C(8, 5). Find coordinates of its centroid. Solution: Suppose A(x1, y1) and B(x2, y2) and C(x3, y3) x1 = –7, y1 = 6 and - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: The coordinates of the vertices of a triangle ABC are A (–7, 6), B(2, –2) and C(8, 5). Find coordinates of its centroid. Solution: Suppose A(x 1 , y 1 ) and B(x 2 , y 2 ) and C(x 3 , y 3 ) x 1 = –7, y 1 = 6 and x 2 = 2, y 2 = –2 and x 3 = 8, y 3 = 5 Using Centroid formula ∴ Coordinates of the centroid of a traingle ABC = `((x_1 + x_2 + x_3)/3, (y_1 + y_2 + y_3)/3)` = `(square/3, square/3)` ∴ Coordinates of the centroid of a triangle ABC = `(3/3, square)` ∴ Coordinates of the centroid of a triangle ABC = `(1 , square)`
items.solution_text: Suppose A(x 1 , y 1 ) and B(x 2 , y 2 ) and C(x 3 , y 3 ) x 1 = –7, y 1 = 6 and x 2 = 2, y 2 = –2 and x 3 = 8, y 3 = 5 Using Centroid formula ∴ Coordinates of the centroid of a traingle ABC = `((x_1 + x_2 + x_3)/3, (y_1 + y_2 + y_3)/3)` = `((-7 + 2 + 8)/3, (6 - 2 + 5)/3)` ∴ Coordinates of the centroid of a triangle ABC = `(3/3, 9/3)` ∴ Coordinates of the centroid of a triangle ABC = (1 , 3 )
items.solution_html:
Suppose A(x1, y1) and B(x2, y2) and C(x3, y3)

 x1 = –7, y1 = 6 and x2 = 2, y2 = –2 and x3 = 8, y3 = 5

Using Centroid formula

∴ Coordinates of the centroid of a traingle

ABC = `((x_1 + x_2 + x_3)/3, (y_1 + y_2 + y_3)/3)`

= `((-7 + 2 + 8)/3, (6 - 2 + 5)/3)`

∴ Coordinates of the centroid of a triangle ABC = `(3/3, 9/3)`

∴ Coordinates of the centroid of a triangle ABC = (1 , 3)

items.title: The distance between point P(2, 2) and Q(5, x) is 5 cm, then the value of x ______ - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: The distance between point P(2, 2) and Q(5, x) is 5 cm, then the value of x ______
items.solution_html:
6

Let P(x1, y1) = P( 2, 2) and Q(x2, y2) = Q(5, x)

Here, x1 = 2, y1 = 2, x2 = 5, y2 = x

By distance formula,

d(P, Q) = `sqrt((x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2)`

∴ 5 = `sqrt((5 - 2)^2 + (x - 2)^2)`

∴ 5 = `sqrt(9 + x^2 - 4x + 4)`

∴ 52 = x2 - 4x + 13      ...[Squaring both sides]

∴ 25 = x2 - 4x + 13 

∴ x2 - 4x + 13 - 25 = 0

∴ x2 - 4x - 12 = 0

∴ (x - 6) (x + 2) = 0

∴ x - 6 = 0  or x + 2 = 0

∴ x = 6  or x = -2

items.title: The distance between points P(–1, 1) and Q(5, –7) is - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: The distance between points P(–1, 1) and Q(5, –7) is ______
items.solution_html:
10 cm

Let P(x1, y1) = P( -1, 1) and Q(x2, y2) = Q(5, -7)

Here, x1 = -1, y1 = 1, x2 = 5, y2 = -7

By distance formula,

d(P, Q) = `sqrt((x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2)`

∴ d(P, Q) = `sqrt([5 - (-1)]^2 + (-7- 1)^2)`

∴ d(P, Q) = `sqrt(36 + 64)`

∴ d(P, Q) = `sqrt(100)`

∴ d(P, Q) = 10 cm

items.title: The figure ΔABC is an isosceles triangle with a perimeter of 44 cm. The sides AB and BC are congruent and the length of the base AC is 12 cm. If a circle touches all three sides as shown in the fi - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: The figure ΔABC is an isosceles triangle with a perimeter of 44 cm. The sides AB and BC are congruent and the length of the base AC is 12 cm. If a circle touches all three sides as shown in the figure, then find the length of the tangent segment drawn to the circle from point B.
items.solution_text: Given: AB + BC + AC = 44 cm AC = 12 cm To find: l(BP) , l(BR) Solution: `{:("seg AP" ≅ "seg AQ"),("seg QC" ≅ "seg RC"),("seg BP" ≅ "seg BR"):}}` .......[Tangent Segment theorem] `{:("Let" l("AP") = l("AQ") = x","),(l("QC") = l("RC") = y","),(l("BP") = l("BR") = "z"):}}` .....(i) AC = AQ + QC ......[A – Q – C] ∴ AC = x + y ∴ x + y = 12 ......(ii) [Given] AB + BC + AC = 44 ......[Given] ∴ (AP + PB) + (BR + RC) + (AQ + QC) = 44 ......[A–P–B, B–R–C, A–Q–C] ∴ x + z + z + y + x + y = 44 ......[From (i)] ∴ 2x + 2y + 2z = 44 ∴ 2(x + y) + 2z = 44 ∴ 2(12) + 2z = 44 ......[From (ii)] ∴ 24 + 2z = 44 ∴ 2z = 44 – 24 ∴ 2z = 20 ∴ z = 10 ......(iiii) ∴ l(BP) = l(BR) = 10 cm ......[From (i) and (iii)]
items.solution_html:
Given: AB + BC + AC = 44 cm

AC = 12 cm

To find: l(BP) , l(BR)

Solution:

`{:("seg AP" ≅ "seg AQ"),("seg QC" ≅ "seg RC"),("seg BP" ≅ "seg BR"):}}` .......[Tangent Segment theorem]

`{:("Let"  l("AP") = l("AQ") = x","),(l("QC") = l("RC") = y","),(l("BP") = l("BR") = "z"):}}`   .....(i)

AC = AQ + QC    ......[A – Q – C]

∴ AC = x + y

∴ x + y = 12    ......(ii) [Given]

AB + BC + AC = 44   ......[Given]

∴ (AP + PB) + (BR + RC) + (AQ + QC) = 44  ......[A–P–B, B–R–C, A–Q–C]

∴ x + z + z + y + x + y = 44   ......[From (i)]

∴ 2x + 2y + 2z = 44

∴ 2(x + y) + 2z = 44

∴ 2(12) + 2z = 44    ......[From (ii)]

∴ 24 + 2z = 44

∴ 2z = 44 – 24

∴ 2z = 20

∴ z = 10   ......(iiii)

∴ l(BP) = l(BR) = 10 cm    ......[From (i) and (iii)]

items.title: The point Q divides segment joining A(3, 5) and B(7, 9) in the ratio 2 : 3. Find the X-coordinate of Q - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: The point Q divides segment joining A(3, 5) and B(7, 9) in the ratio 2 : 3. Find the X-coordinate of Q
items.solution_text: Let the co-ordinates of point Q be (x, y) and A (x 1 , y 1 ), B (x 2 , y 2 ) be the given points. Here, x 1 = 3, y 1 = 5, x 2 = 7, y 2 = 9, m = 2, n = 3 ∴ By section formula, x = `("m"x_2 + "n"x_1)/("m" + "n")` = `(2(7) + 3(3))/(2 + 3)` = `(14 + 9)/5` = `23/5` y = `("m"y_2 + "n"y_1)/("m" + "n")` = `(2(9) + 3(5))/(2 + 3)` = `(18 + 15)/5` = `33/5` ∴ The co-ordinates of point Q are `(23/5, 33/5)`.
items.solution_html:
Let the co-ordinates of point Q be (x, y) and A (x1, y1), B (x2, y2) be the given points.

Here, x1 = 3, y1 = 5, x2 = 7, y2 = 9, m = 2, n = 3

∴ By section formula,

x = `("m"x_2 + "n"x_1)/("m" + "n")`

= `(2(7) + 3(3))/(2 + 3)`

= `(14 + 9)/5`

= `23/5`

y = `("m"y_2 + "n"y_1)/("m" + "n")`

= `(2(9) + 3(5))/(2 + 3)`

= `(18 + 15)/5`

= `33/5`

∴ The co-ordinates of point Q are `(23/5, 33/5)`.

items.title: The points (7, – 6), (2, k) and (h, 18) are the vertices of triangle. If (1, 5) are the coordinates of centroid, find the value of h and k - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: The points (7, – 6), (2, k) and (h, 18) are the vertices of triangle. If (1, 5) are the coordinates of centroid, find the value of h and k
items.solution_text: Let (7, – 6) ≡ (x 1 , y 1 ), (2, k) ≡ (x 2 , y 2 ), (h, 18) ≡ (x 3 , y 3 ) be the three vertices of the triangle. (1, 5) are coordinates of centroid. ∴ By Centroid formula, 1 = `(x_1 + x_2 + x_3)/3` ∴ 1 = `(7 + 2 + "h")/3` ∴ 3 = 9 + h ∴ h = – 6 5 = `(y_1 + y_2 + y_3)/3` ∴ 5 = `(-6 + "k" + 18)/3` ∴ 15 = 12 + k ∴ k = 3
items.solution_html:
Let (7, – 6) ≡ (x1, y1), (2, k) ≡ (x2, y2), (h, 18) ≡ (x3, y3) be the three vertices of the triangle.

(1, 5) are coordinates of centroid.

∴ By Centroid formula,

1 = `(x_1 + x_2 + x_3)/3`

∴ 1 = `(7 + 2 + "h")/3`

∴ 3 = 9 + h

∴ h = – 6

5 = `(y_1 + y_2 + y_3)/3`

∴ 5 = `(-6 + "k" + 18)/3`

∴ 15 = 12 + k

∴ k = 3

items.title: There are two poles having heights 8 m and 4 m on plane ground as shown in fig. Because of sunlight shadows of smaller pole is 6m long, then find the length of shadow of longer pole. - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: There are two poles having heights 8 m and 4 m on plane ground as shown in fig. Because of sunlight shadows of smaller pole is 6m long, then find the length of shadow of longer pole.
items.solution_text: Here, AC and PR represent the bigger and smaller poles, and BC and QR represent their shadows respectively. Now, ΔACB ∼ ΔPRQ .....[Vertical poles and their shadows form similar figures] ∴ `"CB"/"RQ" = "AC"/"PR"` ......[Corresponding sides of similar triangles] ∴ `x/6 = 8/4` ∴ x = `(8 xx 6)/4` ∴ x = 12 m ∴ The shadow of the bigger pole will be 12 metres long at that time.
items.solution_html:
Here, AC and PR represent the bigger and smaller poles, and BC and QR represent their shadows respectively.

Now,

ΔACB ∼ ΔPRQ    .....[Vertical poles and their shadows form similar figures]

∴ `"CB"/"RQ" = "AC"/"PR"`   ......[Corresponding sides of similar triangles]

∴ `x/6 = 8/4`

∴ x = `(8 xx 6)/4`

∴ x = 12 m

∴ The shadow of the bigger pole will be 12 metres long at that time.

items.title: To prove cot θ + tan θ = cosec θ × sec θ, complete the activity given below. Activity: L.H.S = □ = □sinθ+sinθcosθ = cos2θ+sin2θ□ = 1sinθ⋅cosθ ......[cos2θ+sin2θ=□] = 1sinθ×1□ = □ = R.H.S - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: To prove cot θ + tan θ = cosec θ × sec θ, complete the activity given below. Activity: L.H.S = `square` = `square/sintheta + sintheta/costheta` = `(cos^2theta + sin^2theta)/square` = `1/(sintheta*costheta)` ......`[cos^2theta + sin^2theta = square]` = `1/sintheta xx 1/square` = `square` = R.H.S
items.solution_text: L.H.S = cot θ + tan θ = `costheta/sintheta + sintheta/costheta` = `(cos^2theta + sin^2theta)/(sintheta*costheta)` = `1/(sintheta*costheta)` ......[cos 2 θ + sin 2 θ = 1] = `1/sintheta xx 1/costheta` = cosecθ × secθ = R.H.S.
items.solution_html:
L.H.S = cot θ + tan θ

= `costheta/sintheta + sintheta/costheta`

= `(cos^2theta + sin^2theta)/(sintheta*costheta)`

= `1/(sintheta*costheta)`     ......[cos2θ + sin2θ = 1]

= `1/sintheta xx 1/costheta`

= cosecθ × secθ

= R.H.S.

items.title: Two Circles Having Radii 3.5 Cm and 4.8 Cm Touch Each Other Internally. Find the Distance Between Their Centres. - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: Two circles having radii 3.5 cm and 4.8 cm touch each other internally. Find the distance between their centres.
items.solution_text: It is given that two circle having radii 3.5 cm and 4.8 cm touch each other internally. We know, the distance between the centres of the circles touching internally is equal to the difference of their radii. ∴ Distance between the centres of the two circles = 4.8 cm − 3.5 cm = 1.3 cm Thus, the distance between their centres is 1.3 cm.
items.solution_html:
It is given that two circle having radii 3.5 cm and 4.8 cm touch each other internally.

We know, the distance between the centres of the circles touching internally is equal to the difference of their radii.

∴ Distance between the centres of the two circles = 4.8 cm − 3.5 cm = 1.3 cm

Thus, the distance between their centres is 1.3 cm.

items.title: Two triangles are similar. Smaller triangle’s sides are 4 cm, 5 cm, 6 cm. Perimeter of larger triangle is 90 cm then find the sides of larger triangle. - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: Two triangles are similar. Smaller triangle’s sides are 4 cm, 5 cm, 6 cm. Perimeter of larger triangle is 90 cm then find the sides of larger triangle.
items.solution_text: Given: ΔABC ~ ΔPQR In ΔABC, AB = 4 cm, BC = 5 cm, AC = 6 cm In ΔPQR, PQ + QR + PR = 90 cm To find: PQ, QR and PR ΔABC ~ ΔPQR .....[Given] ∴ `"AB"/"PQ" = "BC"/"QR" = "AC"/"PR"` .....[Corresponding sides of similar triangles] Let `"AB"/"PQ" = "BC"/"QR" = "AC"/"PR"` = k ∴ `4/"PQ" = 5/"QR" = 6/"PR"` = k .....[Given] ∴ `4/"PQ"` = k, `5/"QR"` = k and `6/"PR"` = k ∴ PQ = `4/"k"`, QR = `5/"k"` and PR = `6/"k"` ......(i) ∴ PQ + QR + PR = `4/"k" + 5/"k" + 6/"k"` ∴ 90 =`15/"k"` .....[Given] ∴ k = `15/90` = `1/6` ∴ PQ = `4/((1/6))` = 4 × 6 = 24 cm ...[From (i)] QR = `5/((1/6))` = 5 × 6 = 30 cm ...[From (i)] PR = `6/((1/6))` = 6 × 6 = 36 cm ...[From (i)] ∴ The sides of the larger triangle are 24 cm, 30 cm and 36 cm.
items.solution_html:
Given: ΔABC ~ ΔPQR

In ΔABC, AB = 4 cm, BC = 5 cm, AC = 6 cm

In ΔPQR, PQ + QR + PR = 90 cm

To find: PQ, QR and PR

ΔABC ~ ΔPQR       .....[Given]

∴ `"AB"/"PQ" = "BC"/"QR" = "AC"/"PR"`    .....[Corresponding sides of similar triangles]

Let `"AB"/"PQ" = "BC"/"QR" = "AC"/"PR"` = k

∴ `4/"PQ" = 5/"QR" = 6/"PR"` = k     .....[Given]

∴ `4/"PQ"` = k, `5/"QR"` = k and `6/"PR"` = k

∴ PQ = `4/"k"`, QR = `5/"k"` and PR = `6/"k"`   ......(i)

∴ PQ + QR + PR = `4/"k" + 5/"k" + 6/"k"`

∴ 90 =`15/"k"`      .....[Given]

∴ k = `15/90`

= `1/6`

∴ PQ = `4/((1/6))` = 4 × 6 = 24 cm    ...[From (i)]

QR = `5/((1/6))` = 5 × 6 = 30 cm       ...[From (i)]

PR = `6/((1/6))` = 6 × 6 = 36 cm        ...[From (i)]

∴ The sides of the larger triangle are 24 cm, 30 cm and 36 cm.

items.title: What is the distance between two parallel tangents of a circle having radius 4.5 cm? Justify your answer. - Geometry Mathematics 2
items.question: What is the distance between two parallel tangents of a circle having radius 4.5 cm? Justify your answer.
items.solution_text: In the given figure, O is the centre of the circle. Line PT and line QR are two parallel tangents to the circle at P and Q, respectively. ∴ ∠OPT + ∠OQR = 180º ......(Sum of adjacent interior angles on the same side of the transversal is supplementary) ⇒ POQ is a straight line segment. ∴ PQ is the diameter of the circle. PQ = Distance between the parallel tangents PT and QR = 2 × Radius = 2 × 4.5 = 9 cm Thus, the distance between two parallel tangents of the circle is 9 cm.
items.solution_html:
In the given figure, O is the centre of the circle. Line PT and line QR are two parallel tangents to the circle at P and Q, respectively.

∴ ∠OPT + ∠OQR = 180º    ......(Sum of adjacent interior angles on the same side of the transversal is supplementary)

⇒ POQ is a straight line segment.

∴ PQ is the diameter of the circle.

PQ = Distance between the parallel tangents PT and QR

= 2 × Radius

= 2 × 4.5

= 9 cm

Thus, the distance between two parallel tangents of the circle is 9 cm.
